doc_description: A table-of-contents-style USDA Rural Development HB-2-3550 handbook excerpt covering direct single-family housing program overview, servicing, payments, subsidies, special servicing, foreclosure, collections, appeals, appraisals, and the accompanying 7 CFR Part 3550 regulations.

structure.title: CHAPTER 1: OVERVIEW
structure.summary: This partial document is a table of contents for HB-2-3550, the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook. It outlines Chapter 1, which provides an overview of the handbook and the Direct Single Family Housing Program. Main topics include an introduction to how the handbook should be used, handbook symbols and citations, the organization of attachments and appendices, terminology, and the overall structure of the handbook. It also covers the goals and basic framework of the Direct Single Family Housing Program, including Section 502 and Section 504 loans and grants, and the role of field offices. Additional sections introduce automation systems used for loan origination and servicing, specifically the DLOS system with UniFi and LoanServ, and general program requirements such as civil rights, nondiscrimination, and reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: INTRODUCTION TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK
nodes.summary:
This partial document is the table of contents for the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550). It outlines the structure of Chapter 1, which serves as an overview of the handbook and the Direct Single Family Housing Program. The covered topics include how to use the handbook, handbook symbols and references, terminology, and organization.

It also summarizes the main program areas addressed in the handbook, including the goals of the Direct Single Family Housing Program, an overview of Section 502 program and nonprogram loans, Section 504 grants and loans, and the role of field offices. In addition, it introduces automation systems used in the program, specifically the Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, including UniFi and LoanServ.

Finally, the partial contents show that the handbook begins discussing general program requirements, with a focus on civil rights, nondiscrimination, and reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities.

nodes.title: Handbook Symbols
nodes.summary:
This partial document is the table of contents for Handbook HB-2-3550, which appears to be the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook for the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program. It outlines the first chapter, covering:

- An introduction to the handbook, including how to use it, handbook symbols, citations, text boxes, attachments, terminology, and organization.
- An overview of the Direct Single Family Housing Program, including its goals and explanations of Section 502 and Section 504 loan and grant programs, as well as the role of field offices.
- Automation systems used in the program, specifically the Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, including UniFi and LoanServ.
- General program requirements, beginning with civil rights topics such as nondiscrimination and reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities.

Overall, the section serves as a roadmap to the handbook’s structure and introduces the main program, operational, technical, and compliance topics that will be covered in later sections.

nodes.title: Reasonable Accommodations for Persons with Disabilities
nodes.summary:
This partial document is the table of contents for Chapter 1 of the HB-2-3550 Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook. It covers an overview of the handbook, how to use it, and the structure and terminology used throughout. It also introduces the Direct Single Family Housing Program, including goals of the program, Section 502 loans, Section 504 grants and loans, and the role of field offices. Another major section addresses automation through the Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, including UniFi and LoanServ.

The document then outlines general program requirements, with emphasis on civil rights, nondiscrimination, reasonable accommodations, and the review and appeals process. It details adverse decisions, responsibilities of state directors, ADR coordinators, and agency representatives, as well as informal administrative review, appeal rights, mediation, and NAD hearing preparation. Additional topics include conflict of interest rules, disclosure requirements, disposition of REO properties, other federal requirements such as environmental requirements, and exception authority. The table of contents also references attachments related to informal administrative review and appealable or non-appealable adverse decisions.

It covers adverse decisions and the responsibilities of state directors, ADR coordinators, and agency representatives; distinguishes between informal administrative review, appealable and non-appealable agency decisions; and explains participation rights in review, mediation, and appeal processes. It also includes sections on NAD hearing preparation, conflict of interest disclosures, other federal and environmental requirements, and exception authority, along with attachments defining informal administrative review and adverse decision categories.

nodes.title: Mediation
nodes.summary: The partial document appears to be a table of contents from HB-2-3550 covering administrative review and appeal procedures. It lists sections on adverse decisions, responsibilities of state directors, ADR coordinators, and agency representatives, as well as agency decisions, participation rights, and NAD hearing preparation. It also includes conflict of interest rules, other federal requirements such as environmental requirements, exception authority, and several attachments defining informal administrative review and appealable or non-appealable adverse decisions.

nodes.title: Appeal
nodes.summary: The partial document is a table of contents from HB-2-3550 covering Sections 1.9 through 1.15. It outlines the USDA/Rural Housing review and appeals process, including adverse decisions, responsibilities of state directors, ADR coordinators, and agency representatives, and the types of agency decisions that can or cannot be appealed. It also covers participation rights for informal administrative review, mediation, and appeal; preparation for NAD hearings including pre-hearing, post-hearing, timing, and director review; conflict of interest rules and required disclosures by borrowers and agency employees; requirements related to REO property disposition; other federal and environmental requirements; and exception authority. The section ends with attachments describing informal administrative review and examples of appealable and non-appealable adverse decisions.

nodes.title: Mediation
nodes.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction telling the responder to directly return a description of the partial document’s main points and not include any other text.

nodes.title: Appeal
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a collection of USDA Rural Housing Service (RHS) handbook letters and appeal rules covering debt collection, refinancing, and property sale processes.

Main points include:
- **Administrative offset notices and borrower rights**: explains RHS’s intent to collect delinquent debts through administrative offset, and describes the borrower’s rights to request a conference, submit written objections, inspect and copy records, and appeal the decision.
- **Request for administrative offset to another agency**: a formal letter RHS sends to another agency asking it to withhold program payments to satisfy a debtor’s RHS debt.
- **Request for salary offset**: a letter requesting an employer to withhold part of a borrower’s pay to recover a debt.
- **IRS tax refund offset**: notice that RHS may refer delinquent debts to the IRS to intercept tax refunds, including borrower rights to contest the offset within 60 days and information about joint tax returns.
- **Confirmation of IRS offset decision**: response letter stating whether the borrower will be removed from IRS offset records or remain subject to offset.
- **Refinancing-related letters**: several notices about whether RHS will continue the loan, requesting the borrower to refinance with private credit, reminders to take action, follow-up requests, and notices when the borrower is unresponsive.
- **Sale for less than the debt**: response to a borrower’s request to sell property for less than the amount owed, either approving the sale with lien release but leaving remaining debt owed, or denying consent.
- **National Appeals Division rules of procedure**: the document ends with the table of contents for USDA appeal procedures, listing topics such as definitions, hearings, reconsideration, and judicial review.

nodes.title: Sec. APPENDIX 4 7 CFR PART 11 --NATIONAL APPEALS DIVISION RULES OF PROCEDURE TABLE OF CONTENTS
nodes.summary: This excerpt is the opening of 7 CFR Part 11, which sets out the National Appeals Division rules of procedure for USDA-related agency appeals. It includes the table of contents and begins the definitions section, explaining key terms such as “adverse decision” and “agency.” The text also lists which USDA agencies are covered by the rules, including ASCS, CCC, FSA, FmHA, FCIC, NRCS, RBS, RDA, RHS, and RUS, with a note that some RUS programs are excluded.

nodes.title: § 11.1 Definitions
nodes.summary: The partial document provides the opening provisions of USDA National Appeals Division regulations. It begins with filing and timing authority, then defines key terms used throughout the part, including adverse decision, agency, agency record, appeal, appellant, authorized representative, case record, hearing, hearing officer, participant, record review, and Secretary. It also explains the scope of who may appeal and lists several categories of disputes excluded from this appeals process, such as certain contracting matters, FOIA disputes, suspension/debarment issues, employment disputes, tort claims, and discrimination complaints. The document then starts the general statement describing the National Appeals Division as an independent organization within USDA that handles these proceedings under the Secretary’s oversight.

nodes.title: § 11.2 General statement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the scope and procedures of the USDA National Appeals Division appeals process. It lists several types of disputes that are included or excluded, such as suspension and debarment cases, Commodity Credit Corporation export programs, crop insurance disputes, tenant grievances in multi-family housing, employment-related disputes, Federal Tort Claims Act claims, and discrimination complaints. It defines “record review” and “Secretary,” then states that the part sets forth how appeals before the National Appeals Division are handled, emphasizing its independence within the Department and the roles of the Hearing Officers and Director. The document also explains that program participants generally must use this administrative appeal process before seeking judicial review. Finally, it describes what kinds of adverse agency decisions can be appealed, including denial of program participation or benefits, compliance determinations, payment decisions, and wetland or highly erodible land findings, and notes that these proceedings are not governed by certain APA provisions, the Equal Access to Justice Act, or the Federal Rules of Evidence.

nodes.title: § 11.3 Applicability
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines the scope and purpose of the USDA administrative appeal regulations. It explains that program participants must first seek review of adverse decisions before a Hearing Officer, and may then appeal to the Director, before going to court. It also specifies which types of agency decisions are covered, including denial of program participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland or highly erodible land classifications. Finally, it notes important limits and exclusions: the procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations, and certain general legal rules such as the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence do not apply to these proceedings.

nodes.title: § 11.4 Inapplicability of other laws and regulations
nodes.summary: This excerpt outlines the USDA agricultural administrative appeal rules. It explains that program participants must first seek review of an adverse decision before a Hearing Officer, and may then appeal to the Director before going to court. It describes what types of agency decisions are covered, including denials of program participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland or highly erodible land findings. It also states the limits of the process, excluding challenges to statutes or USDA regulations, and notes that certain general legal provisions do not apply to these proceedings, including the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence.

nodes.title: § 11.5 Informal review of adverse decisions
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for informal review of adverse decisions before an appeal to the USDA National Appeals Division (NAD). It covers when informal review is mandatory for Farm Service Agency (FSA) field-level decisions, when it is optional for State-level FSA decisions and decisions by other agencies, and where the procedures for requesting review can be found. It also describes the right to use mediation or other alternative dispute resolution programs, and how requesting mediation affects appeal deadlines and hearing timelines.

nodes.title: § 11.6 Director review of agency determination of appealability and right of participants to Division hearing
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for appealing agency decisions within the Division. It covers how a participant can request Director review of an agency’s determination that a decision is not appealable, the Director’s authority to decide whether a matter is appealable, and that the Director’s decision is final. It also describes the process and deadlines for filing appeals of adverse decisions, including what must be included in a written hearing request and how representation by an authorized representative must be documented. Finally, it sets out restrictions on ex parte communications, prohibiting off-the-record merit discussions during an appeal except for certain procedural or noticed communications.

nodes.title: § 11.7 Ex parte communications
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines procedures for requesting a hearing or record review after an adverse decision, including the need for a written, personally signed request, required supporting information, and rules for authorized representatives. It then explains restrictions on ex parte communications during an appeal, what communications are permitted, and how improper communications must be documented in the record. Finally, it describes general division hearing procedures, including access to the agency record and the authority to administer oaths and issue subpoenas for witnesses and evidence.

nodes.title: § 11.8 Division hearings
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines procedural rules for Division hearings and appeals review. It first addresses improper ex parte communications, requiring that any such communications and responses be documented in the hearing record, and allowing penalties or adverse consequences if a party knowingly violates the rule. It then explains general hearing authority and evidence procedures, including access to the agency record, the power to administer oaths and issue subpoenas, service and enforcement of subpoenas, and who bears witness-related costs.

The document next describes hearing procedures: timely hearing rights, required pre-hearing submissions from both the appellant and agency, notice requirements, pre-hearing conferences, conduct of hearings, official recording of proceedings, and what happens if a party fails to appear. It also covers post-hearing submissions, states that interlocutory review is not allowed, places the burden of proof on the appellant, and sets deadlines for issuing a determination.

Finally, it begins the process for Director review of a Hearing Officer’s determination, including who may request review, filing deadlines, required contents of the request, service on other parties, notification procedures, and the opportunity for written responses.

nodes.title: § 11.9 Director review of determinations of Hearing Officers
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the procedures and deadlines for administrative appeal review after a Hearing Officer’s determination. It explains when the Hearing Officer must issue a notice of determination, how that notice becomes final if not appealed, and the process for requesting Director review by either the appellant or the agency. It also covers notification and response requirements for other parties, the standards and time limits the Director uses to review or remand decisions, the Director’s authority to delegate review, and the power to grant equitable relief.

nodes.title: § 11.10 Basis for determinations
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for how appeal determinations are made and reviewed. It states that Hearing Officers and the Director are not bound by earlier factual findings, but must ensure decisions comply with agency laws, regulations, and general interpretations, and rely on the case record and applicable legal authorities in effect at the relevant time. It also describes the process for requesting reconsideration of the Director’s determination: either party may request it within 10 days, but only if they identify a material factual error or a legal inconsistency. The Director must then notify all parties, allow responses from the non-requesting parties within 5 days, and issue a reconsideration decision within 5 days after receiving responses. If the reconsideration changes the original final determination, the reconsideration decision becomes the final determination.

nodes.title: § 11.11 Reconsideration of Director determinations
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains the standards and procedures for agency appeal determinations and reconsideration. It states that Hearing Officers and the Director are not required to follow prior factual findings from the adverse decision, but must base decisions on the case record, applicable laws and regulations, and ensure consistency with agency rules and interpretations. It also sets out the process for requesting reconsideration of the Director’s decision, including a 10-day filing window, required grounds such as material factual error or conflict with law, notice to parties, opportunity for non-requesting parties to respond, and a deadline for the Director to issue a reconsideration decision.

nodes.title: § 11.12 Effective date and implementation of final determinations of the Division
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for final determinations by the Division, including when they take effect and how agencies must implement them, the availability and limits of judicial review, and procedures for filing appeals and calculating deadlines. It states that agencies must act within 30 days after a final determination becomes effective, explains how the effective date is determined, requires appellants to obtain a final determination before seeking court review, and defines when filings are considered received and how filing deadlines are extended when they fall on weekends or holidays.

nodes.title: § 11.13 Judicial review
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for final determinations, appeals, and filing deadlines within the Division. It covers:

- **Implementation of final determinations (§ 11.12):** Once a case is returned to an agency, the agency head must carry out the Division’s final determination within 30 days after it becomes effective. It also states when a final determination takes effect, depending on the applicable statute or regulation.

- **Judicial review (§ 11.13):** Final determinations by the Division can be reviewed and enforced by a U.S. District Court under the Administrative Procedure Act, but an appellant must first obtain a final determination from the Division before seeking judicial review of an agency adverse decision.

- **Filing and time computation (§ 11.14):** This section defines when a document is considered filed, explains how deadlines are extended when they fall on weekends or federal holidays, and sets the deadline for filing at 5:00 p.m. local time on the due date.

nodes.title: § 11.14 Filing of appeals and computation of time
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedural rules and appraisal guidance related to agency appeals and property servicing actions. It explains when a Division’s final determination becomes effective and how quickly an agency must implement it, the availability of judicial review in U.S. District Court, and how filing deadlines are calculated, including what counts as a filed document and how deadlines are extended when they fall on non-business days. It then shifts to appraisal guidelines, describing servicing actions that require an appraisal, why appraisals are needed, and the basic valuation methods used by appraisers, especially the sales comparison approach.

nodes.title: APPENDIX 5 APPRAISAL GUIDELINES [7 CFR 3550.62]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains appraisal guidelines for servicing actions under HB-2-3550. It describes when the Servicing Office may need an appraisal, such as to determine subsidy recapture, evaluate partial release requests, assess deed-in-lieu offers, verify lien value, set foreclosure bid prices, or support protective advances. It also gives basic information about real estate appraisal, noting that value is influenced by factors like location, market conditions, construction quality, and amenities. The document outlines the main appraisal methods used to estimate value, especially the sales comparison approach and the cost approach, and mentions that a broker’s price opinion may sometimes be used.

nodes.title: A. SERVICING ACTIONS REQUIRING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: The partial document provides appraisal guidelines for USDA servicing actions under 7 CFR 3550.62. It explains when the Servicing Office may need an appraisal to determine a property’s market value, such as for subsidy recapture, partial release of security, deed-in-lieu decisions, determining lien value, foreclosure bid pricing, and protective advances. It also introduces basic appraisal concepts, noting that appraisers consider factors like location, market conditions, construction quality, and amenities, and typically use the sales comparison approach and cost approach, with a broker’s price opinion sometimes used in certain servicing actions.

nodes.title: B. APPRAISAL BASICS
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines appraisal guidelines used by the servicing office for special servicing actions that require determining a property’s market value. It explains when appraisals are needed, such as for subsidy recapture, partial release of security, deed-in-lieu decisions, lien valuation, foreclosure bid pricing, and protective advances. The document then summarizes basic appraisal methods, including the sales comparison approach, cost approach, and the limited use of a broker’s price opinion, and distinguishes between “as is” value and “as developed/improved” value. It also covers how appraisals are ordered, including timelines for field staff and contract appraisers, and emphasizes the need for due diligence related to environmental conditions. Finally, it addresses special rules for remote rural areas and Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be unavailable and a different appraisal form and Agency staff appraisal authority are required.

nodes.title: C. APPRAISAL ORDERS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains several appraisal-related topics and procedures. It describes different appraisal methods, including the cost approach and the BPO (broker price opinion), and explains that appraisers may provide either an “as is” value or an “as developed/improved” value depending on the loan purpose. It then outlines how appraisal orders are handled, including ordering through Field Staff or contract appraisers, along with expected completion timeframes. The document also introduces the requirement for due diligence when market value must be determined. Finally, it covers special rules for appraisals in remote rural areas and on Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be scarce, requiring use of the Marshall and Swift Square Foot Appraisal form and Agency staff with appraisal authority rather than contract appraisers.

nodes.title: D. DUE DILIGENCE
nodes.summary: The partial document explains appraisal methods and procedures used in loan servicing, especially for rural and tribal properties. It covers the cost approach and broker price opinions (BPOs), and distinguishes between “as is” and “as developed/improved” property values. It outlines how appraisals are ordered by the Servicing Office, including timelines for field staff and contract appraisers. The document also describes due diligence requirements related to environmental contamination, including use of the Transaction Screen Questionnaire and referral to an Environmental Coordinator if concerns arise. In addition, it explains how appraisals are reviewed through administrative and technical reviews, the criteria used in each, and the requirement to order a new appraisal if one is unacceptable. Finally, it notes special appraisal rules for remote rural areas and Tribal lands, where the sales comparison approach may not be required and a Marshall and Swift Square Foot Appraisal must be used by authorized Agency staff.

nodes.title: E. REVIEWING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains environmental due diligence requirements and appraisal review procedures. It states that due diligence must begin with the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528) to identify possible contamination from hazardous substances, wastes, or petroleum products, and that any concerns must be referred to an Environmental Coordinator. It then outlines how the Servicing Office reviews appraisals to verify property value, using administrative reviews and random spot-checks, and requires a new appraisal if one is unacceptable. The document also distinguishes between administrative reviews, which check completeness, math, comparables, and valuation methods and must be done within 7 days, and technical reviews, which assess the quality and support for the appraisal’s conclusions and are required for the first appraisal by an appraiser and in certain other cases.

nodes.title: 1. Administrative Review
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for environmental due diligence and appraisal review in loan servicing. It states that due diligence is required to check for contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products and to assess any effect on property value, beginning with the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528). If concerns are identified, the issue must be referred to an Environmental Coordinator.

It then covers appraisal review requirements. The Servicing Office must review contract appraisals for value determination using administrative reviews and random spot-checks, while field-generated appraisals do not require this separate review. If an appraisal is unacceptable, a new one must be obtained before servicing can continue.

The document also describes two types of appraisal reviews. An administrative review checks completeness, arithmetic accuracy, use of current comparables, and whether both cost and comparable sales approaches were used; it must be completed within 7 days and is required before paying the appraiser. A technical review evaluates whether the appraisal is complete, well reasoned, and adequately supported; it is required for the first appraisal by each contract or fee appraiser, may be repeated if problems arise, and can also be used as a random spot-check or when administrative review reveals serious issues.

nodes.title: 2. Technical Review
nodes.summary: The partial document explains environmental due diligence and appraisal review procedures. It states that due diligence must identify contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products and assess any effect on property market value, beginning with the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528); any concerns must be referred to an Environmental Coordinator. It also outlines how the Servicing Office reviews appraisals to verify property value, including administrative reviews and random spot-checks. Administrative reviews check completeness, calculations, comparable data, and valuation methods within 7 days and are required before paying a contract appraiser. Technical reviews assess the quality and support for the appraiser’s conclusion of value, are required for the first appraisal from each contract or fee appraiser, may be done randomly, and can also be triggered by serious issues found in administrative review.

nodes.title: 3. Entering the Appraisal Results
nodes.summary: The partial document explains that after an appraisal is reviewed and found acceptable, the Servicing Office must enter the property’s appraised value into MortgageServ and use that value to decide on the proposed servicing action. It also states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action requiring an appraisal, and that this fee is billed to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: F. PAYING FOR APPRAISALS [7 CFR 3550.62(b)]
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics: appraisal results and the introduction to the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook. It explains that if an appraisal is acceptable, the Servicing Office must record the property’s appraised value in MortgageServ and use that value when deciding on the servicing action. It also states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action requiring an appraisal, with the fee added to the borrower’s account.

The handbook introduction describes its purpose as a guide for Agency staff to service loans efficiently and effectively while meeting legal and administrative requirements. It focuses on loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, notes that detailed procedures are in a separate handbook, and emphasizes consistency with applicable laws and regulations. It also explains how the handbook is organized and mentions that symbols and references are used to help readers find information.

nodes.title: SECTION 1: INTRODUCTION TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document is an introduction to the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550). It explains the handbook’s purpose: to give Agency staff guidance for servicing loans efficiently while meeting legal and administrative requirements, especially for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants. It also notes that detailed procedures are covered in a separate handbook and that the guidance must remain consistent with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and regulations. In addition, the section explains how to use the handbook, including that it is organized for easy reference and that it includes symbols such as a book icon to point readers to additional reference sources.

nodes.title: 1.1 WELCOME TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550) and explains its purpose: to help Agency staff service loans efficiently while meeting legal and administrative requirements. It states that the handbook covers loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, with detailed procedures contained in a separate handbook. It also notes that the handbook is meant to align with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and regulations, and does not override them. Finally, it explains how the handbook is organized for easy reference and begins describing handbook symbols, including references that point readers to additional information sources.

nodes.title: 1.2 USING THIS HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook, explaining that it is designed to help Agency staff service loans efficiently while meeting legal and administrative requirements. It states that the handbook covers loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, with separate procedures provided elsewhere. It also notes that the handbook is meant to align with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and regulations. The section further explains how the handbook is organized and used, including that it can serve as both a learning tool for new staff and a reference for experienced staff, and begins describing handbook symbols, such as the reference symbol used to point readers to additional sources of information.

nodes.title: A. Handbook Symbols
nodes.summary:
This section introduces the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook, explaining its purpose, audience, and scope. It states that the handbook is meant to help Agency staff service loans efficiently while meeting legal and administrative requirements, with policies covering Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants. It also notes that detailed procedures are contained in a separate handbook and that the guidance must remain consistent with applicable laws and regulations.

The section also explains how to use the handbook. It describes its organization as a reference tool for both new and experienced staff and outlines the symbols and formatting conventions used throughout, including references, state supplements, civil rights indicators, deadlines, and documentation reminders. It further explains citation styles for regulations, forms, letters, helpful hints, and the use of examples and exhibits to clarify the material.

nodes.title: B. Citations and Text Boxes
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how to use the USDA Rural Development HB-2-3550 handbook. It describes the symbols and formatting conventions used throughout the handbook, including indicators for state supplements, civil rights/fair housing issues, deadlines, and required documentation. It also explains how regulatory citations, forms, handbook letters, helpful hints, examples, and exhibits are presented, and how to interpret these references when reading the handbook.

nodes.title: C. Attachments and Appendices
nodes.summary: This section explains how to use the handbook’s reference materials and standard terminology. It describes the purpose of the glossary and acronym list, attachments, and appendices, and explains how attachments are numbered and used. It also defines key terms used throughout the handbook for consistency, including “Agency,” “borrower,” “Servicing Office,” “Field Office,” “Field Staff,” and “Approval Official,” noting that these terms are standardized across states and may differ from local usage.

nodes.title: D. Terminology
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the handbook is structured and how to use it. It describes the purpose of attachments, appendices, the glossary, and acronym lists, and explains the standardized terminology used throughout the handbook for terms like Agency, borrower, Servicing Office, Field Office, and Field Staff/Approval Official. It also outlines the organization of the handbook by chapter, summarizing what each chapter covers, including program overview, regular servicing, escrow/taxes/insurance, payment subsidies and income determinations, special servicing, liquidation and acquisition, and special collections.

nodes.title: E. How This Handbook Is Organized
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines how Handbook HB-1-3550 is organized, specifically summarizing the purpose and content of Chapters 1 through 7. It explains that Chapter 1 covers Section 502 and 504 overview information and general administrative requirements, Chapter 2 addresses regular loan servicing, Chapter 3 focuses on escrow, taxes, and insurance, Chapter 4 covers payment subsidies and income determinations, Chapter 5 discusses special servicing for delinquent borrowers, Chapter 6 explains liquidation and acquisition through foreclosure when servicing fails, and Chapter 7 describes special collections involving unauthorized assistance, offsets, and debt settlement.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: OVERVIEW OF THE DIRECT SINGLE FAMILY HOUSING PROGRAM
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the purpose and goals of the USDA Direct Single Family Housing Program, emphasizing access to safe, modest housing for low- and very low-income rural households. It highlights three core goals: providing customer-friendly service, using public resources efficiently, and streamlining loan processing.

It then gives an overview of Section 502 direct loans, describing them as loans for acquiring, building, rehabilitating, improving, or relocating rural homes. The section notes typical loan terms, possible exceptions, interest rates set by the Agency, and the availability of payment subsidies. It also begins listing key eligibility requirements, focusing on income limits for applicants and special rules for assumed loans and REO property purchases.

nodes.title: 1.3 GOALS OF THE DIRECT SINGLE FAMILY HOUSING PROGRAM
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the goals and overview of the USDA Direct Single Family Housing Program, especially the Section 502 direct loan program. It explains that the program is designed to help low- and very low-income people obtain modest, decent, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas. Key program goals include providing customer-friendly service, using public funds efficiently, and streamlining loan processing.

It also gives an overview of Section 502 program loans, which can be used to acquire, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate rural housing. The section notes typical loan terms, possible shorter or longer terms in certain cases, the use of Agency-established interest rates, and the availability of payment subsidies for some borrowers. Finally, it begins listing basic eligibility requirements, including income limits for applicants depending on the type of loan or property involved.

nodes.title: 1.4 SECTION 502 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the goals and key features of the USDA Direct Single Family Housing Program. It explains that the program is meant to help low- and very low-income people obtain adequate, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas while emphasizing customer service, efficient use of resources, and efficient processing.

It then gives an overview of Section 502 direct loans, including their purpose, loan terms, interest rate setting, payment assistance, and applicant eligibility requirements such as income limits, inability to obtain credit elsewhere, occupancy, citizenship, legal capacity, and willingness to repay. It also describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations like loan assumptions or REO property purchases when in the government’s best interest.

Finally, the document summarizes Section 504 loans and grants, which help homeowners repair or improve homes to address health and safety hazards. It notes that grants are for very low-income applicants age 62 or older, may be used for hazard removal or accessibility improvements, and have a lifetime maximum amount.

nodes.title: 1.5 SECTION 504 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key USDA Rural Development housing loan and grant programs and related field office responsibilities. It explains eligibility and basic requirements for Section 502 loans, including creditworthiness, occupancy, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity, and repayment ability, and describes nonprogram loans used in limited situations for government benefit. It then summarizes Section 504 assistance, which helps very low-income homeowners repair homes and remove health and safety hazards, including grants for eligible homeowners age 62 or older and loans with specific limits, terms, and interest rates. Finally, it describes the role of field offices in originating and closing loans, the servicing office’s role after closing, and the obligation of field staff to report property or borrower issues that may affect loan security or servicing.

nodes.title: 1.6 THE ROLE OF FIELD OFFICES
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers Section 504 loans and the role of field offices. It explains that Section 504 loan funds can be used for general repairs, property improvements, and removal of health and safety hazards, with limits of up to $20,000 in outstanding Section 504 debt, a maximum 20-year term, and a 1 percent interest rate. It also describes how loans are originated and closed in Field Offices, then serviced by the Servicing Office, with Field Staff assisting when local support is needed. Finally, it outlines Field Staff responsibilities to report issues affecting property security or borrower circumstances, such as hazardous conditions, environmental hazards, non-occupancy, abandonment, or income changes, and to direct borrowers to the Servicing Office when appropriate.

nodes.title: SECTION 3: AUTOMATION
nodes.summary: This section explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is used to speed up loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two connected parts of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant information and tracks applications from pre-qualification through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage Agency loans and record servicing actions. The text also notes that LoanServ supports coordination between Field Staff and the Servicing Office by allowing work queues and shared information exchange, helping different staff members work on the same borrower account over time.

nodes.title: 1.7 THE DEDICATED LOAN ORIGINATION AND SERVICING (DLOS) SYSTEM
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is designed to streamline loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes DLOS as two connected systems: UniFi, a web-based loan origination tool used to store applicant information, perform calculations, and track applications from pre-qualification through closing; and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage Agency loans, monitor loan performance, and record all servicing actions. The document also notes that LoanServ can be accessed in part by Field Offices to share information and coordinate servicing tasks, including sending work queues for assistance, and emphasizes that consistent use of LoanServ helps multiple servicing staff work on the same borrower account over time.

nodes.title: A. UniFi
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is designed to speed up loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two connected components of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant information and application activities from pre-qualification through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage Agency loans and track every servicing action. The text also notes that UniFi data is uploaded nightly into LoanServ. In addition, it explains how LoanServ supports coordination between Field Offices and the Servicing Office through shared access and work queues, and highlights the benefit of consistent use of LoanServ for allowing multiple servicing staff to work on the same borrower account over time.

nodes.title: B. LoanServ
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics from the USDA Rural Development handbook: the agency’s loan automation system and civil rights requirements for its programs.

First, it explains the Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is used to streamline loan processing, standardize records, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. DLOS consists of two linked applications:
- UniFi, a web-based loan origination tool that stores applicant data, performs calculations, and tracks application activity from pre-qualification through closing.
- LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage and monitor Agency loans after origination, record servicing actions, and share information between servicing staff and field staff through work queues.

Second, the document outlines civil rights compliance expectations. It states that the Agency administers programs fairly and in accordance with equal opportunity and fair housing laws, referencing RD Instruction 1901-E. It also lists major civil rights laws and executive orders relevant to the Direct Single Family Loan Program, including:
- Equal Credit Opportunity Act
- Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
- Fair Housing Act
- Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act
- Age Discrimination Act
- Executive Orders related to housing discrimination, equal employment opportunity, and access for persons with Limited English Proficiency.

nodes.title: SECTION 4: GENERAL PROGRAM REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the civil rights requirements for the Agency’s direct single-family loan program. It states that the Agency must administer its programs fairly and in accordance with equal opportunity and fair housing laws, as well as applicable Executive Orders, with compliance rules contained in RD Instruction 1901-E. It also lists major civil rights laws and policies affecting the program, including the Equal Credit Opportunity Act, Title VI of the Civil Rights Act, the Fair Housing Act, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, the Age Discrimination Act, Executive Orders 11063, 11246, and 13166. These provisions collectively prohibit discrimination in credit, housing, federally assisted programs, employment by contractors, and access for limited English proficient persons.

nodes.title: 1.8 CIVIL RIGHTS [7 CFR 3550.3]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the civil rights and nondiscrimination requirements for the USDA Rural Development direct single-family loan program. It states that the Agency must administer its programs fairly under equal opportunity and fair housing laws, and it lists the major federal laws and Executive Orders that apply, including ECOA, Titles VI and VIII of the Civil Rights Act, Section 504, the Age Discrimination Act, and Executive Orders covering housing discrimination, employment nondiscrimination, and limited English proficiency access.

The section then describes how these civil rights rules apply to program servicing. It emphasizes that loans, grants, services, and benefits must not be denied on the basis of race, color, national origin, sex, religion, marital status, familial status, age, disability, source of income, or protected credit rights. It also highlights the importance of consistent procedures, fair treatment, broad outreach, equal rigor in determining eligibility, and consistent standards for making exceptions. The document concludes by identifying key civil rights issues for loan servicers: access, consistency and fairness of treatment, disparate impacts, and record keeping.

nodes.title: A. Nondiscrimination
nodes.summary: The partial document covers civil rights requirements and review procedures for the USDA Section 502 and 504 loan programs. It explains nondiscrimination rules prohibiting denial of loans, grants, services, or employment opportunities based on protected characteristics, and emphasizes that loan servicers must use consistent, fair procedures in outreach, eligibility determinations, exceptions, subsidies, and hearings/appeals. It also outlines the requirement to provide reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities so they can access and benefit from agency programs. Finally, it introduces the review and appeals process by defining adverse decisions, stating that such decisions must be based on applicable federal regulations, and noting the role of the National Appeals Division and standardized adverse decision letters.

nodes.title: B. Reasonable Accommodations for Persons with Disabilities [7 CFR 15e.130]
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers key civil rights and appeals requirements for the Rural Housing program. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing remedies, hearings, and appeals must be offered consistently. It also outlines the Agency’s duty to provide reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities so they can access and benefit from programs, including accessible facilities, effective communication tools, and process modifications. Finally, it discusses review and appeals procedures for adverse decisions, defining adverse decisions, requiring them to be based on CFR regulations rather than handbook guidance, and noting use of the National Appeals Division process and standardized adverse decision letters.

nodes.title: 1.9 REVIEW AND APPEALS [7 CFR 3550.4]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers civil rights requirements and applicant protections under the USDA Rural Housing program. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing remedies, hearings, and appeals must be provided consistently. It also details the Agency’s obligation to provide reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, including accessible facilities, effective communication tools, and process modifications to ensure equal access to programs. Finally, it introduces the review and appeals process, defining adverse decisions, stating that they must be based on federal regulations rather than handbook guidance, and noting the role of the National Appeals Division and standardized adverse decision letters.

nodes.title: A. ADVERSE DECISIONS
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers civil rights requirements, reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, and the agency’s review, appeals, and adverse decision procedures. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing, hearings, and appeals must be offered consistently and without discrimination. It also describes the obligation to provide reasonable accommodations or modifications so people with disabilities can access and benefit from programs, including accessible facilities, effective communication tools, and alternative ways to meet program requirements.

The review and appeals section defines adverse decisions, states that they must be based on published regulations rather than handbook guidance, and notes that National Appeals Division procedures apply when decisions are appealed. It includes an example adverse decision letter showing how denials should explain the specific regulatory reasons for the decision and inform applicants of their rights. The document further outlines required notices to participants, including information about informal administrative review, mediation/alternative dispute resolution, NAD appeal rights, and civil rights language.

Finally, it assigns responsibilities to State Directors and ADR Coordinators for handling appeals and mediation requests, maintaining lists of approved mediation providers, ensuring mediators rather than arbitrators are used, and verifying timely mediation requests.

nodes.title: Paragraph 1.9 Reviews and Appeals [7 CFR 3550.4]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for reviews, appeals, mediation, and ADR under HB-2-3550. It states that adverse decision letters must include information about informal administrative meetings, mediation/ADR, NAD appeal rights, and civil rights, with specific attachments used as needed. It also outlines State Director responsibilities to issue state guidance on appeal and mediation requests, including state-specific mediation providers and an ADR Coordinator. The ADR Coordinator duties include maintaining an approved list of mediation providers, ensuring referrals are made in sequence and documented fairly, distinguishing mediators from arbitrators, and verifying whether mediation requests were filed within the required 30-day period. It also describes issuing the correct attachments when mediation is requested and when a participant fails to select a provider within the allowed time.

nodes.title: B. State Director Responsibilities

It also assigns responsibilities to State Directors to issue state-level guidance and identify mediation providers and ADR coordinators. Finally, it details the ADR Coordinator’s duties, including maintaining a list of approved mediation providers, ensuring proper referral procedures, distinguishing mediators from arbitrators, and verifying timely mediation requests and related notices to participants.

It also outlines State Director duties to issue state supplements with mediation guidance and ADR coordinator contact information. nodes.title: Amount of Subordination

The partial document is a table of contents from a USDA-style housing or loan servicing handbook (HB-2-3550). It outlines several major topics related to borrower actions and loan management. Main points covered include:

- **Fees**: A section on fees associated with borrower actions.
- **Borrower Actions Requiring Approval**: Procedures and guidelines for reviewing and approving borrower requests, including:
  - General review guidelines
  - Obtaining appraisals and environmental reviews
  - Evaluating requests and using proceeds
- **Subordination**: Rules for when and how the lender may subordinate its lien, including criteria and amount limits.
- **Mineral Leases**: Guidance on handling mineral lease requests.
- **Partial Release of Security**: Conditions under which part of the secured property may be released.
- **Lease of Security Property**: Rules for allowing leases on property securing the loan.
- **Assumption of Indebtedness**: Procedures for transferring the loan obligation to another party, including:
  - New rate and term assumptions
  - Same rate and term assumptions
  - Agency approval
  - Procedural requirements
  - Releasing a departing borrower from liability
- **Refinancing with Private Credit**: Policies for refinancing the loan using private financing, including:
  - Borrower selection criteria
  - Consideration of subsequent credit
  - Subordination of recapture
  - Processing refinancing requests
  - Special rules for unincorporated borrowers and acceleration
- **Recapture**: Circumstances that trigger recapture of subsidy or benefits, including:
  - Ceasing to occupy the property
  - Death of borrowers
  - Transfer of title

Overall, the document section appears to focus on the administrative and approval processes governing changes to a borrower’s loan, property rights, and repayment obligations.

It lists sections on presidentially declared disasters, repairs completed within or beyond 30 days, and losses not covered by insurance, plus attachments for insurance policy requirements and tax service fees. The main focus is Chapter 4, which introduces payment subsidies, explains types of subsidies, borrower eligibility, loan and reporting requirements, recapture rules, and methods for calculating payment assistance and interest credit. It also covers how to provide payment assistance to borrowers not currently receiving subsidies, including eligibility, processing, effective dates, and annual/interim/in-depth subsidy reviews, including special considerations for trust loan borrowers.

nodes.title: Processing Delinquency Workout Agreements
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a table of contents from HB-2-3550 covering several major sections of loan servicing and borrower evaluation procedures.

Main points covered include:
- **Evaluating Borrower Income**: how to determine, verify, and calculate annual, adjusted, and repayment income, including deductions for dependents, child care, elderly households, disability assistance, and medical expenses, as well as income from assets.
- **Special Servicing**: procedures for handling distressed accounts, including identifying special servicing cases, developing servicing strategies, working with borrowers in bankruptcy, and preparing problem case reports.
- **Delinquency Workout Agreements**: eligibility requirements and processing steps for agreements aimed at resolving delinquency.
- **Use of Payment Assistance as a Special Servicing Action**: refinancing and interim servicing actions while subsidy requests are processed.
- **Protective Advances**: advances made for property repairs or other purposes.
- **Moratoriums**: eligibility, request process, evaluation, monitoring, cancellation, and resumption of payments.
- **Liquidation Recommendation**: circumstances and process for recommending liquidation.
- **Liquidation and Acquisition**: options such as voluntary liquidation, foreclosure, and release of valueless liens, plus calculation of net recovery value and basic security loss, including appraisal, environmental, holding period, and market value considerations.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF LIQUIDATION OPTIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document is a table of contents from HB-2-3550 covering servicing and liquidation procedures for loans. It outlines Chapter 5 topics such as using payment assistance as a special servicing action, protective advances, moratoriums, and liquidation recommendations. It also begins Chapter 6 on liquidation and acquisition, including liquidation options like voluntary liquidation, foreclosure, and release of a valueless lien, along with sections on net recovery value, market value assessment, environmental considerations, appraisals, holding periods, deductions/additions to value, and basic security loss.

nodes.title: Voluntary Liquidation
nodes.summary: The partial document is a table of contents from a servicing and liquidation handbook. It outlines several major topics: using payment assistance as a special servicing action; protective advances for property repairs and other purposes; moratoriums, including eligibility, request processing, monitoring, cancellation, and resumption of payments; and liquidation recommendations. It then moves into Chapter 6 on liquidation and acquisition, covering liquidation options such as voluntary liquidation, foreclosure, and release of a valueless lien; net recovery value calculations, including appraisal, holding period, deductions and additions to market value; basic security loss; deed in lieu of foreclosure; and the foreclosure process from acceleration decisions through sale. Finally, Chapter 7 covers special collections, focusing on unauthorized assistance cases, how they are identified, investigated, notified, appealed, and adjusted, as well as servicing actions and offsets, including annual review of delinquent accounts.

- **Chapter 5: Special Servicing**, including servicing timelines, identifying special servicing accounts, developing servicing strategies, handling bankruptcies, problem case reports, delinquency workout agreements, use of payment assistance, protective advances, moratoriums, and liquidation recommendations.
- **Chapter 6: Liquidation and Acquisition**, including liquidation options such as voluntary liquidation, foreclosure, and release of valueless liens, along with net recovery value calculations, market value and appraisal considerations, holding periods, value adjustments, and basic security loss.

nodes.title: ANNUAL REVIEW OF DELINQUENT ACCOUNTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document is mainly a USDA Rural Housing Service handbook table of contents and related reference materials. It covers procedures for foreclosure and deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, then moves into Chapter 7 on special collections, including unauthorized assistance, offsets, debt settlement, compromise and adjustment of debt, debt cancellation, and handling promissory notes and security instruments. It also lists glossary items, acronyms, appendices, and many official forms used in servicing and eligibility verification.

A large portion of the text consists of appendices with standardized verification forms for income, expenses, benefits, support payments, business income, asset disposition, and oral verification, all used to document borrower financial information. Another appendix lists handbook letters used for borrower notifications and collection actions, such as adverse decisions, bankruptcy-related continuation, salary offset, administrative offset, IRS offset, and refinancing requests.

The final section provides sample letter templates for specific servicing situations, including adverse decision notices, bankruptcy continuation notices, salary offset agreements and notifications, administrative and IRS offset notices, loan refinancing requests and reminders, and responses to requests to sell property for less than the debt.

nodes.title: APPENDIX 4
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the USDA National Appeals Division rules of procedure. It includes a table of contents for sections covering definitions, applicability, informal review, hearing procedures, director review, reconsideration, final determinations, judicial review, and filing deadlines.

The text then defines key terms used throughout the rules, such as adverse decision, agency, appeal, appellant, authorized representative, case record, hearing record, equitable relief, and participant. It also specifies which USDA agencies and types of disputes are covered or excluded.

Next, it explains the general role and independence of the National Appeals Division, noting that participants must seek administrative review through the Division before pursuing judicial review. It also describes the scope of the rules, identifying the kinds of adverse decisions they apply to, such as benefit denials, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland or highly erodible land findings.

Finally, it states that certain general legal procedures and evidentiary rules do not apply to these proceedings, including most Administrative Procedure Act adjudication provisions, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence.

nodes.title: § 11.3 Applicability
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains the scope and purpose of the USDA administrative appeal regulations. It states that program participants must first seek review of an adverse decision before a Hearing Officer and may then appeal to the Director before going to court. It describes what kinds of agency decisions can be appealed, including denial of participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland or highly erodible land findings. It also notes that these procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations themselves. Finally, it clarifies that certain general laws and rules for administrative proceedings, such as the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence, do not apply to these cases.

nodes.title: § 11.4 Inapplicability of other laws and regulations
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the authority and appeal process for USDA program adverse decisions. It states that program participants must first seek review before a Hearing Officer and may then appeal to the Director before pursuing judicial review. It also defines what types of adverse decisions are covered, such as denial of program participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland/highly erodible land findings. Finally, it clarifies that these procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations, and that certain general legal rules, including parts of the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence, do not apply to these proceedings.

nodes.title: § 11.5 Informal review of adverse decisions
nodes.summary: The passage explains the required and optional steps a participant must take to seek informal review of adverse agency decisions before appealing to the National Appeals Division (NAD). It states that FSA field office adverse decisions must first undergo informal review by the responsible county or area committee, with possible further review by the State FSA committee or direct appeal to NAD. It also notes that participants may request optional informal review for State-level FSA decisions and decisions from other agencies, following specified USDA regulations. In addition, the section describes the right to use mediation or other alternative dispute resolution programs, and explains how doing so affects appeal deadlines and hearing timelines before NAD.

nodes.title: § 11.6 Director review of agency determination of appealability and right of participants to Division hearing.
nodes.summary: The document explains the rules for appealing agency decisions and requesting Division hearings. It covers how participants can ask the Director to review a determination that a decision is not appealable, the Director’s authority to decide whether a matter can be appealed, and the finality of that decision. It also describes the procedure and deadlines for requesting hearings on adverse decisions, the required contents of a written hearing request, and how representation by an authorized representative must be documented. In addition, it sets out restrictions on ex parte communications, prohibiting off-the-record discussions about the merits of an appeal and allowing only limited procedural or fully noticed discussions.

nodes.title: § 11.7 Ex parte communications
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines the procedures for requesting a hearing or record review, including the requirement that the request be written and personally signed, identify the adverse decision if available, and explain why the decision or agency inaction was wrong. It also explains representation rules for authorized representatives, who must submit a sworn declaration and written authorization.

The document then sets out rules on ex parte communications, prohibiting off-the-record merit discussions with interested persons during an appeal, allowing only procedural discussions or merits discussions with notice to all parties, and requiring any improper communications to be documented in the hearing record. It also gives the Hearing Officer or Director authority to address violations, including potentially dismissing or adversely affecting a party’s claim.

Finally, it describes general hearing procedures before the Division, including access to the agency record, the right to request a copy of it, and the authority of the Director and Hearing Officer to administer oaths and issue subpoenas for witnesses or evidence, with specific timing requirements for subpoena requests and issuance.

nodes.title: § 11.8 Division hearings
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures governing Division hearings and appeals of adverse agency decisions. It covers rules on ex parte communications, including how improper communications must be placed in the hearing record and possible sanctions for violations. It then outlines hearing procedures such as access to the agency record, subpoena authority, service and enforcement of subpoenas, timing and notice requirements, pre-hearing conferences, presentation and admission of evidence, recording of hearings, handling of party absences, post-hearing submissions, burden of proof, and deadlines for issuing determinations. Finally, it explains Director review of Hearing Officer determinations, including who may request review, filing deadlines, required contents of requests, notification to other parties, and the opportunity for responses.

nodes.title: § 11.9 Director review of determinations of Hearing Officers
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the process for reviewing Hearing Officer determinations and the role of the Director in that review. It covers the deadlines and procedures for requesting Director review by either an appellant or an agency, including required written requests, signatures, and reasons for disagreement. It also describes notification and response procedures for other parties.

The document further outlines how the Director evaluates the case, what records and materials may be considered, the possible outcomes of the review, and the circumstances under which the Director may remand a case for more proceedings or a new hearing. It specifies time limits for the Director’s decision, allows delegation of review authority to deputy or associate directors, and grants the Director authority to provide equitable relief.

nodes.title: § 11.10 Basis for determinations
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains how Hearing Officers and the Director should make determinations in agency appeals, emphasizing that decisions must be based on the case record, applicable laws and regulations, and consistent legal interpretations rather than prior factual findings. It also describes the process for requesting reconsideration of the Director’s decisions, including the 10-day filing deadline, the requirement to show a material factual or legal error, notice to other parties, their opportunity to respond, and the Director’s duty to issue a reconsideration decision promptly. It further notes that if reconsideration changes the original determination, the new decision becomes the final determination.

nodes.title: § 11.11 Reconsideration of Director determinations
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains how appeal determinations are made and reviewed. It states that Hearing Officers and the Director are not limited by prior factual findings and must base decisions on the case record, applicable laws, and regulations in effect at the relevant time. It also sets out the process for requesting reconsideration of the Director’s decision, including a 10-day filing deadline, the need to show a material factual error or legal conflict, notice to other parties, their right to respond within 5 days, and the Director’s duty to issue a reconsideration decision within 5 days after responses are received. If the reconsideration changes the original decision, that new decision becomes the final determination.

nodes.title: § 11.12 Effective date and implementation of final determinations of the Division
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedural rules for appeals and final determinations by a Division. It covers when a final determination becomes effective and how agencies must implement it, the availability and limits of judicial review in federal court, and the rules for filing appeals or other documents, including what counts as a filing and how filing deadlines are computed when the due date falls on a weekend or holiday.

nodes.title: § 11.13 Judicial review
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedural rules for appeals and final determinations by the Division. It covers when final determinations become effective and how agencies must implement them, the availability of judicial review in U.S. District Court, and the requirement that appellants first obtain a final determination before seeking court review. It also sets rules for filing appeals and related documents, including when a filing is considered received, how deadlines are extended when they fall on weekends or holidays, and the exact time a filing deadline expires.

nodes.title: § 11.14 Filing of appeals and computation of time
nodes.summary: The partial document covers administrative rules for handling Division appeals and appraisal guidance for property servicing actions. It explains when a final determination must be implemented, when such determinations become effective, and how judicial review works. It also defines filing rules and deadlines for appeals and related documents. The appendix then shifts to appraisal guidelines, describing when the agency needs appraisals in servicing situations, what factors appraisers consider, and the main valuation methods used, especially the sales comparison approach.

nodes.title: APPENDIX 5
nodes.summary: The partial document provides appraisal guidelines for servicing actions under HB-2-3550. It explains when the Servicing Office may need an appraisal to determine a property’s market value, including cases such as subsidy recapture, partial release of security, deed-in-lieu decisions, lien valuation, foreclosure bid pricing, and protective advances. It also introduces the basics of real estate appraisal, noting that appraisers consider factors like location, market conditions, construction quality, and amenities. The document outlines the main valuation methods, especially the sales comparison approach, where recent comparable property sales are adjusted for differences to estimate market value, and references the cost approach and broker’s price opinion as additional valuation tools.

nodes.title: A. SERVICING ACTIONS REQUIRING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: This partial document provides appraisal guidelines for servicing actions under HB-2-3550. It explains when the Servicing Office may need an appraisal to determine a property’s market value, including situations such as recapturing subsidy, releasing part of the security property, considering a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, assessing lien value, setting a foreclosure bid price, or evaluating a protective advance. It also introduces basic appraisal concepts, describing the factors appraisers consider and the main valuation methods used, especially the sales comparison approach, with mention that cost approach and broker’s price opinion may also be used in some cases.

nodes.title: B. APPRAISAL BASICS
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides appraisal guidelines for servicing actions that require determining a property’s market value. It explains when the Servicing Office may need an appraisal, such as for subsidy recapture, partial release of security, deed-in-lieu decisions, lien value, foreclosure bid pricing, and protective advances.

It then outlines appraisal basics, describing common valuation methods: the sales comparison approach, the cost approach, and in limited cases a broker’s price opinion (BPO). It also distinguishes between “as is” value and “as developed/improved” value, noting that “as is” value is used for most servicing actions.

The document next covers how appraisals are ordered, either through Field Staff or contract appraisers, and sets timelines for ordering and completion.

Finally, it addresses due diligence requirements and special rules for appraisals in remote rural areas or on Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be limited. In those cases, the sales comparison approach is not required, and a specific square foot appraisal form must be used by Agency staff with appraisal authority.

nodes.title: C. APPRAISAL ORDERS
nodes.summary: The document explains several appraisal-related methods and procedures used in loan servicing. It describes the cost approach and broker price opinion (BPO), and explains that appraisers may provide either an “as is” value or an “as developed/improved” value depending on the purpose of the loan. It outlines how appraisals are ordered through the Servicing Office, including timelines for Field Staff and contract appraisers, and emphasizes the need for due diligence when determining market value. The document also covers special rules for appraisals in remote rural areas and on Tribal lands, noting that sales comparison may not be required there and that a Marshall and Swift Square Foot Appraisal form must be used by Agency staff with appraisal authority.

nodes.title: D. DUE DILIGENCE
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains appraisal-related procedures and standards for loan servicing. It covers the main appraisal methods mentioned, including the cost approach and BPOs, and explains how appraisers determine either “as is” value or “as developed/improved” value depending on the loan purpose. It also describes how appraisals are ordered by the Servicing Office, including timing requirements for Field Staff and contract appraisers. Another key section addresses due diligence for environmental concerns, requiring use of the Transaction Screen Questionnaire and further review if contamination issues are suspected.

The document also outlines how appraisals are reviewed after completion. It distinguishes between administrative reviews, which check completeness, math, comparables, and use of required valuation approaches, and technical reviews, which assess the quality of the appraiser’s reasoning and support for the value conclusion. Finally, it includes a special rule for remote rural areas and Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be scarce, requiring use of a Marshall and Swift Square Foot Appraisal and Agency staff rather than contract appraisers.

nodes.title: E. REVIEWING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses environmental due diligence and appraisal review procedures. It explains that due diligence for property transactions must check for contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous waste, or petroleum products using the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528), and any concerns must be referred to an Environmental Coordinator. It then outlines how the Servicing Office should review appraisals to determine property value, using administrative reviews and random spot-checks, and requiring a new appraisal if one is found unacceptable. The document also distinguishes between administrative reviews, which verify completeness, math accuracy, comparables, and valuation methods within 7 days, and technical reviews, which assess whether the appraisal is well-supported and may be required for initial or problematic appraisals.

nodes.title: Administrative Review
nodes.summary:
The document section focuses on environmental due diligence and appraisal review procedures. It explains that due diligence is used to check for contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products, and to assess how contamination could affect property market value. The process begins with the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (TSQ), and any concerns must be referred to an Environmental Coordinator.

It then outlines how appraisals are reviewed by the Servicing Office. Appraisals are checked for accuracy through administrative reviews and random spot-checks, and if an appraisal is unacceptable, a new one must be obtained before servicing can continue. The administrative review verifies completeness, math accuracy, adequate current comparables, and use of both cost and comparable sales approaches, and must be completed within 7 days.
