The technical review is more detailed, assessing whether the appraisal reasoning and support for value are adequate; it is required for the first appraisal by any contract or fee appraiser and may also be used for spot-checks or when significant issues arise.

nodes.title: Technical Review
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses environmental due diligence and appraisal review procedures. It explains that due diligence is used to check for contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products and to assess any effect on property market value. The process begins with completing the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (TSQ), and any concerns are referred to the Environmental Coordinator.

It then outlines how appraisals must be reviewed by the Servicing Office to confirm property value and appraisal accuracy. Two types of reviews are described: administrative reviews, which check completeness, math, comparables, and valuation methods and must be finished within 7 days; and technical reviews, which examine whether the appraisal is well-supported and properly reasoned. The document notes that unacceptable appraisals require a new one before servicing can continue, and that appraisal reviews are required before paying contract appraisers.

nodes.title: Entering the Appraisal Results
nodes.summary: The document explains that after an appraisal review, if the appraisal is acceptable, the Servicing Office must enter the appraised value into MortgageServ and use that value when deciding on the proposed servicing action. It also states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action requiring an appraisal, and that this fee is billed to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: F. PAYING FOR APPRAISALS [7 CFR 3550.62(b)]
nodes.summary: The document explains that after an appraisal review, if the appraisal is acceptable, the Servicing Office must enter the property’s appraised value into MortgageServ and use that value to determine the servicing action. It also states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action requiring an appraisal, and this fee is charged to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: HB-2-3550 SECTION 1: INTRODUCTION TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550) and explains its purpose, scope, and use. It states that the handbook is intended to help Agency staff service loans efficiently and assist borrowers while meeting legal and administrative requirements. It covers loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, with detailed procedures located in a separate handbook. The document also notes that the handbook must remain consistent with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and Departmental regulations. In addition, it explains how the handbook is organized for easy reference and mentions the use of symbols, such as a book icon to point readers to additional information sources.

nodes.title: 1.1 WELCOME TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK
nodes.summary:
The partial document is an introduction to the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook. It explains the handbook’s purpose: to help Agency staff service loans efficiently and effectively while meeting legal and administrative requirements. It notes that the handbook covers loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, with detailed procedures provided in a separate handbook. The document also states that the handbook is meant to align with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and Departmental/Agency regulations.

In addition, it explains how to use the handbook. It is organized for easy reference by topic, may be read cover to cover by new staff, and can be used as a reference by experienced staff. The section also introduces handbook symbols and conventions, including a “References” symbol that points readers to additional sources such as laws, regulations, or instructions.

nodes.title: 1.2 USING THIS HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550) and explains its purpose: to provide Agency staff with guidance for servicing loans efficiently and effectively while meeting legal and administrative requirements. It covers loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, noting that detailed procedures are contained in a separate handbook. The section also emphasizes that the handbook must be used consistently with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and Department regulations. Finally, it explains how the handbook is organized for easy reference and introduces handbook symbols, such as the reference symbol used to point readers to additional sources of information.

nodes.title: A. Handbook Symbols
nodes.summary:
The partial document is an introductory section of the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550). It explains the handbook’s purpose: to help Agency staff service loans efficiently and effectively while meeting legal and administrative requirements, with a focus on loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants. It also notes that detailed procedures are contained in a separate handbook and that the guidance must align with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and regulations.

The section then describes how to use the handbook, emphasizing that it is organized for easy reference by topic. It explains several symbols and conventions used throughout the handbook, including references to outside sources, State supplement indicators, civil rights-related highlights, deadline formatting, and documentation reminders. It also covers how citations, form references, helpful hint boxes, examples, and exhibits are presented so readers can locate regulatory authority and supporting information more easily.

nodes.title: B. Citations and Text Boxes
nodes.summary: This section explains how to use the handbook and interpret its formatting and symbols. It describes state supplements and notes that state, local, tribal, civil rights, deadline, and documentation symbols highlight important subject areas. It also explains how regulatory citations, form and letter references, helpful hint boxes, examples, and exhibits are presented. In addition, it outlines the purpose of attachments, appendices, the glossary, and acronym lists. Finally, it defines standard terminology used throughout the handbook, including Agency, borrower, Servicing Office, Field Office, Field Staff, and Approval Official.

nodes.title: C. Attachments and Appendices
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to use the handbook’s supporting materials and defines standardized terminology used throughout it. It describes the purpose and location of the glossary, acronyms list, attachments, and appendices, noting that attachments provide chapter-specific technical information while appendices contain forms and reference materials used across multiple chapters. It also clarifies key terms such as “Agency,” “Borrower,” “Servicing Office,” “Field Office,” and “Field Staff and Approval Official” to ensure consistent meaning across states and over time.

nodes.title: D. Terminology
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the handbook is organized and how to use it. It describes the purpose of the glossary, acronyms list, attachments, and appendices, and explains the standard terminology used throughout the handbook, such as “Agency,” “borrower,” “Servicing Office,” “Field Office,” and “Field Staff and Approval Official.” It also summarizes the contents of the main handbook chapters, including overview requirements, regular servicing, escrow/taxes/insurance, payment subsidies and income determinations, special servicing for delinquent borrowers, liquidation and acquisition, and special collections.

nodes.title: E. How This Handbook Is Organized
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the structure of the HB-1-3550 handbook and summarizes the main topics covered in each chapter, including overview requirements, regular servicing, escrow/taxes/insurance, payment subsidies and income determinations, special servicing, liquidation and acquisition, and special collections. It then introduces the direct single family housing program, explaining its goals of helping low- and very low-income people obtain decent rural housing, while emphasizing customer service, efficient use of resources, and streamlined processing. Finally, it begins an overview of Section 502 loans, describing their purpose, typical terms, eligibility income limits, and the availability of payment subsidies for some borrowers.

nodes.title: SECTION 2: OVERVIEW OF THE DIRECT SINGLE FAMILY HOUSING PROGRAM
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines the goals and overview of the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, especially Section 502 loans. It explains that the program is designed to help low- and very low-income people in rural areas obtain modest, decent, safe, and sanitary housing. The document emphasizes three main goals: providing customer-friendly service, using public funds efficiently, and streamlining loan processing. It then introduces Section 502 direct loans, describing their purpose for acquiring, building, rehabilitating, improving, or relocating homes in rural areas. It also notes standard loan terms, interest rate setting, possible payment subsidies, and basic eligibility requirements related to income limits.

nodes.title: 1.3 GOALS OF THE DIRECT SINGLE FAMILY HOUSING PROGRAM
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides an overview of the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, focusing on its goals and the Section 502 direct loan program. It explains that the program is designed to help low- and very low-income people obtain modest, decent, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas. The goals highlighted include improving customer service, using public resources efficiently, and processing loans efficiently.

It then introduces Section 502, describing it as a loan program for purchasing, building, repairing, improving, or relocating rural housing. The section notes typical loan terms, the possibility of longer terms in some cases, and that loan rates are set by the Agency. It also mentions that payment subsidies may be available to reduce monthly payments and lists basic eligibility requirements, especially income limits for applicants and special rules for assumed loans and REO property purchases.

nodes.title: 1.4 SECTION 502 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the goals and basic overview of the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, especially Section 502 direct loans. It explains that the program is meant to help low- and very low-income people obtain adequate, modest, decent, safe, and sanitary housing in rural areas. The document highlights three main program goals: providing customer-friendly service, using public resources efficiently, and processing loans effectively and efficiently.

It then gives an overview of Section 502 program loans, noting that they can be used to acquire, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas. It describes standard loan terms, mentions that payment subsidies may be available, and begins listing borrower eligibility requirements based on income limits, including different rules for assumed loans and REO property purchases.

nodes.title: A. Program Loans
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides an overview of the USDA Rural Development direct single-family housing program and its main goals and loan types. It explains that the program is designed to help low- and very low-income people obtain adequate, decent, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas, while emphasizing customer service, efficient use of public funds, and streamlined processing.

It then summarizes Section 502 direct loans, which help eligible borrowers acquire, build, repair, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas. The document outlines basic eligibility requirements such as income limits, inability to get credit elsewhere, occupancy requirements, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity, and willingness and ability to repay. It also describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations such as loan assumptions or REO property purchases when the borrower is not eligible for the regular program.

Finally, it introduces Section 504 direct loans and grants, which assist homeowners who cannot obtain other credit to repair homes and address health and safety hazards. It notes that grants are specifically for very low-income elderly homeowners age 62 or older, and that grant funds can be used for repairs, accessibility improvements, and related health and safety corrections, with a lifetime maximum amount mentioned.

nodes.title: B. Nonprogram Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines key housing loan and grant programs under USDA Rural Development. It summarizes Section 502 eligibility requirements, including creditworthiness, occupancy, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity, and willingness and ability to repay. It also explains nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations such as assuming an existing loan, purchasing REO property, or handling certain loan conversions, and notes that these are only made when in the government’s best interest. The document then introduces Section 504, a direct loan and grant program for very low-income homeowners who cannot get other credit, intended to repair homes or correct health and safety hazards. It highlights Section 504 grants for homeowners age 62 and older, specifying that grant funds can be used for hazard removal or accessibility improvements, with a lifetime maximum grant amount of $7,500.

nodes.title: 1.5 SECTION 504 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines USDA Rural Housing program loan and grant eligibility rules, focusing on Section 502 and Section 504. It lists key Section 502 borrower requirements such as inability to obtain other credit, intent to personally occupy the dwelling, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity to incur debt, and willingness and ability to repay the loan. It also explains nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited cases such as assuming an existing loan, purchasing REO property, converting improperly assisted loans, or continuing loans after partial security release, and notes that these loans are made only when in the Government’s best interest. The document then describes Section 504 direct loans and grants for homeowners with very low income who cannot get other credit to repair or rehabilitate homes or address health and safety hazards. Finally, it explains that Section 504 grants are specifically for homeowners age 62 or older, can be used for hazard removal or accessibility improvements, and have a lifetime maximum of $7,500.

nodes.title: A. Section 504 Grants
nodes.summary:
The excerpt summarizes key USDA housing loan programs and field office responsibilities. It explains Section 502 eligibility basics for borrowers, including inability to obtain other credit, intent to occupy the home, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity to take on debt, and willingness and ability to repay. It then describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations such as assumption of existing loans or purchase of REO property when the borrower is not eligible for Section 502, and notes they are only made when in the government’s best interest.

The document also outlines Section 504 direct loans and grants for homeowners who cannot get other credit to repair or rehabilitate homes or address health and safety hazards. Section 504 grants are limited to eligible applicants age 62 or older, can be used only for hazard removal or accessibility-related repairs, and have a lifetime maximum of $7,500. Section 504 loans may be used for general repairs and hazard removal, have a maximum combined outstanding balance of $20,000, a 20-year term, and a 1% interest rate.

Finally, it describes the role of field offices: they originate and close loans, while servicing is handled by the Servicing Office after final disbursement. Field staff may assist with servicing actions and must report conditions or borrower changes that could affect property security or loan status, such as hazardous conditions, environmental issues, non-occupancy, abandonment, or income changes.

nodes.title: B. Section 504 Loans
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains the Section 504 loan program and the role of field offices. It states that Section 504 loans can be used for general repairs, improvements, and removal of health and safety hazards; borrowers may have multiple loans, but the total outstanding balance cannot exceed $20,000, with a 20-year term and 1% interest rate. It also describes that loans are originated and closed in Field Offices, then serviced by the Servicing Office after closing. Field staff may assist with servicing actions when local presence is needed and are responsible for reporting conditions that threaten the security of the property or indicate borrower changes, such as hazardous conditions, environmental hazards, non-occupancy, abandonment, or income changes, and may direct borrowers to the Servicing Office for help.

nodes.title: 1.6 THE ROLE OF FIELD OFFICES
nodes.summary: The partial document explains Section 504 loans and how they are administered. It says these loans can be used for general repairs, improvements, and removal of health and safety hazards, with a combined outstanding balance limit of $20,000, a 20-year maximum term, and 1% interest. It then describes the role of Field Offices in originating, closing, and supporting servicing actions, including reporting property hazards or changes in borrower circumstances. Finally, it introduces the Agency’s DLOS system, which includes UniFi for loan origination and LoanServ for loan servicing, and explains how these systems help track loan activity and communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office.

nodes.title: SECTION 3: AUTOMATION
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system and its purpose in streamlining loan processing, standardizing recordkeeping, and improving communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two connected components of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system used to store applicant information and track applications through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage loans, monitor performance, and record servicing actions. The text also notes that LoanServ supports coordination between Field Staff and the Servicing Office through shared access and work queues, helping them exchange information and handle servicing tasks more efficiently.

nodes.title: 1.7 THE DEDICATED LOAN ORIGINATION AND SERVICING (DLOS) SYSTEM
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is designed to speed up loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes how DLOS tracks loans through two linked applications: UniFi and LoanServ. UniFi is a web-based loan origination tool used to store applicant information, perform calculations, and maintain a central record from pre-qualification to closing, with data uploaded nightly to LoanServ. LoanServ is a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage Agency loans, monitor loan performance, and record all servicing actions. The text also notes that LoanServ supports coordination between Field Staff and the Servicing Office through shared access and work queues, helping them exchange information and handle servicing tasks more efficiently.

nodes.title: A. UniFi
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the USDA Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is designed to speed up loan-making, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two connected components of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant data, performs calculations, and tracks applications from pre-qualification through closing; and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage and monitor loans after origination. The document also notes that LoanServ records servicing actions, helps Field Offices and the Servicing Office share information, and supports work queues for coordination on servicing tasks.

nodes.title: B. LoanServ
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains two main topics from a USDA Rural Development loan handbook:

1. **Automation and the DLOS system**
   - Introduces the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is designed to speed up loan processing, standardize records, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office.
   - Explains that DLOS has two linked parts:
     - **UniFi**: a web-based loan origination system used to store applicant information, perform calculations, and track an application from pre-qualification to closing.
     - **LoanServ**: a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage Agency loans, monitor performance, and record all servicing actions.
   - Notes that LoanServ also supports communication between Servicing Office staff and Field Staff through work queues.

2. **Civil rights and nondiscrimination requirements**
   - States that the Agency must administer programs in accordance with civil rights, equal opportunity, and fair housing laws.
   - Points readers to RD Instruction 1901-E for compliance requirements.
   - Lists major federal laws and executive orders affecting the Direct Single Family Loan Program, including:
     - Equal Credit Opportunity Act
     - Title VI of the Civil Rights Act
     - Fair Housing Act
     - Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act
     - Age Discrimination Act
     - Executive Orders related to housing discrimination, employment nondiscrimination, and limited English proficiency.

nodes.title: SECTION 4: GENERAL PROGRAM REQUIREMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document describes civil rights and equal opportunity requirements for the Agency’s program administration. It states that the program must be carried out fairly and in compliance with civil rights and fair housing laws, as well as applicable Executive Orders, with detailed compliance guidance referenced in RD Instruction 1901-E. It also lists the major federal laws and orders affecting the Direct Single Family Loan Program, including protections against discrimination in credit, housing, federally assisted programs, employment, disability access, age discrimination, and language access for persons with limited English proficiency.

nodes.title: 1.8 CIVIL RIGHTS [7 CFR 3550.3]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the civil rights requirements for the Agency’s single-family loan program. It states that the Agency must administer programs fairly and comply with equal opportunity and fair housing laws, with detailed compliance rules found in RD Instruction 1901-E. It then lists major federal civil rights laws and executive orders that apply, including protections against discrimination based on race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, disability, familial status, and public assistance status. It also covers equal credit and housing access, employment nondiscrimination for federally assisted construction, and language access requirements for individuals with limited English proficiency.

nodes.title: CIVIL RIGHTS [7 CFR 3550.3]
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers civil rights and appeal requirements for the USDA Rural Development direct single family housing loan program. It explains that the Agency must administer programs fairly and in compliance with equal opportunity, fair housing, and related civil rights laws. A list of major laws and Executive Orders is included, such as ECOA, Title VI, the Fair Housing Act, Section 504, the Age Discrimination Act, and executive orders addressing housing discrimination, employment nondiscrimination, and limited English proficiency.

It also outlines nondiscrimination requirements for loan, grant, and servicing activities, emphasizing consistent treatment in outreach, eligibility verification, exceptions, subsidies, and hearings/appeals. The document highlights that poor program implementation can create civil rights issues and stresses fair access, consistency, and recordkeeping.

In addition, the text explains reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, requiring the Agency to provide equal access through accessible facilities, effective communication tools, and process modifications when needed.

Finally, it introduces review and appeals procedures, defining adverse decisions, stating that they must be based on the CFR rather than handbook guidance, and noting the role of the National Appeals Division regulations and standardized adverse decision letters.

nodes.title: REVIEW AND APPEALS [7 CFR 3550.4]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers civil rights requirements and review/appeals procedures for the agency’s direct SFH programs. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing remedies, hearings, and appeals must be provided consistently. It then details reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, emphasizing equal access, non-discrimination, and accommodations such as accessible facilities, effective communication tools, and procedural modifications. Finally, it outlines adverse decisions, stating that such decisions must be based on federal regulations, identifies examples of adverse actions, and references the National Appeals Division process and standardized adverse decision letters.

nodes.title: A. ADVERSE DECISIONS
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers civil rights requirements and the appeals process for USDA Rural Development Single Family Housing programs. It explains that subsidies, favorable servicing, hearings, and appeals must be made available consistently. It also describes reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities, including accessible facilities, effective communication, and process modifications to ensure equal access to program benefits.

The document then outlines review and appeals procedures for adverse decisions, defining adverse decisions and emphasizing that they must be based on published regulations rather than handbook guidance. It notes the use of standardized adverse decision letters and includes a sample letter showing how denials should explain the reasons and inform applicants of their rights.

Finally, it details the contents of adverse decision notices and the roles of State Directors and ADR Coordinators. These responsibilities include providing guidance on appeals and mediation, maintaining lists of mediation providers, ensuring mediation requests are timely, and distinguishing mediation from arbitration.

nodes.title: NAD Hearing Preparation
nodes.summary: The partial document only contains an instruction asking for a description of the main points covered in the document, but it does not include any substantive content to summarize.

nodes.title: Pre-Hearing Conference
nodes.summary: This partial document is a Rural Housing Service handbook letter template for notifying a borrower about administrative offset to collect delinquent debt. It explains that RHS intends to offset money owed to the borrower by another federal agency, states the amount past due and accruing interest, and gives a 30-day period before offset begins if the debt is paid in full. It also outlines the borrower’s right to a pre-offset conference to dispute the debt amount or argue hardship or unfairness.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 105(3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 7 SUBJECT: Notification of Administrative Offset
nodes.summary: This document is a Rural Housing Service notice template informing a borrower that RHS intends to collect delinquent debt through administrative offset. It explains the amount owed, that offset may begin after 30 days, and that the borrower can avoid offset by paying in full within that period. The notice also outlines the borrower’s rights to a pre-offset conference, to dispute the debt or hardship, to inspect and copy loan records, to submit written objections, and to request an administrative appeal. It includes deadlines for each request, explains that RHS may proceed with offset during appeals, and states that this is the final notice before offset action begins.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 106(3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 7 SUBJECT: Request for Administrative Offset
nodes.summary: The document is a handbook letter template used by the Rural Housing Service (RHS) to request administrative offset of program payments from another agency to collect a delinquent debt. It includes placeholders for the agency address, debtor information, account details, delinquency amounts, interest, and due process letter date. The letter certifies that RHS has met legal and regulatory requirements for offset under federal debt collection rules, states that no bankruptcy has been reported, and instructs the agency to make checks payable to RHS and send the offset funds to a specified address.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 107(3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 7 SUBJECT: Request for Salary Offset
nodes.summary: This document is a sample form letter used by the Rural Housing Service to request salary offset from an agency. It includes placeholders for agency and debtor information, explains that a portion of the employee’s disposable pay will be withheld to collect a delinquency or debt owed to the Department of Agriculture, and cites the Debt Collection Act of 1982 and USDA salary offset regulations as the authority for the action.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 108(3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 7 SUBJECT: Notification of IRS Offset
nodes.summary: The document is a formal RHS notice letter informing a borrower that their delinquent debt may be collected through IRS tax refund offset. It explains that the borrower is more than two months past due, that RHS will refer the debt to the IRS, and that collected amounts will be applied to the loan after processing fees. The letter also serves as the required legal notice, describing the federal offset process and the 60-day opportunity for the borrower to dispute the debt or provide evidence against offset. It includes guidance for married borrowers filing joint tax returns, explains possible exceptions such as bankruptcy or approved repayment arrangements, and warns that the account may also be reported to credit bureaus. The letter ends with instructions to direct all contact to the servicing Customer Service Department and contains placeholders for dates, borrower information, and RHS official signature details.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 109(3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 7 SUBJECT: Confirmation of IRS Offset
nodes.summary: This document is a formal USDA Rural Housing Service letter template used to confirm the outcome of an IRS tax refund offset review. It instructs the sender to fill in borrower and date details, then provides two possible responses: either the borrower’s name has been removed from IRS offset records and no refund offset will occur, or the debt is considered legally enforceable, the borrower is at least two months delinquent, the name will remain on IRS records, refunds will be offset, and the account may be reported to a credit bureau. It also includes a closing signature block for an RHS official.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 110 (3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 2 SUBJECT: Notification That the Agency Will Continue With Loan
nodes.summary: The partial document is a USDA Rural Housing Service handbook letter template used to notify a borrower that the agency will continue servicing their loan and that they are not currently required to refinance. It states that, based on the borrower’s financial situation and lender requirements, RHS has determined the borrower may have difficulty obtaining suitable outside credit to refinance the RHS loan. The letter explains that the loan remains subject to future refinancing requirements, meaning RHS may ask the borrower to refinance the remaining debt at a later date. It also includes placeholders for the date, borrower information, and approval official signature details.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 111 (3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 2 SUBJECT: Request for Borrower to Refinance With Private Credit
nodes.summary: This letter informs a borrower that, based on their eligibility for private credit, they are required to refinance their RHS housing loan with a private lender within 90 days. It explains that current low interest rates may help reduce their mortgage payments and provides a 25% discount on subsidy recapture if certain conditions are met, including paying recapture with the final loan payment, remaining in the home, and retaining title to the property. The letter also explains how payoff information can be obtained from the Servicing and Asset Management Office, outlines what documentation is required if the borrower cannot refinance, and provides contact information for assistance.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 112 (3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 2 SUBJECT: Reminder Letter
nodes.summary: The document is a reminder letter from the Rural Housing Service (RHS) to borrowers who previously received a request to refinance their RHS loan. It explains that the borrower must take action to refinance within 90 days and that failure to cooperate may lead to legal proceedings. The letter provides guidance on how borrowers can contact the Servicing and Asset Management Office to submit financial information, learn whether they qualify to refinance, and obtain a list of guaranteed lenders. It also includes instructions for obtaining payoff information by phone, mentions access through the Voice Response Unit, and notes that the office can provide information about deferring subsidy recapture, possible discounts, and final payments.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 113 (3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 2 SUBJECT: Follow up Request for Borrower to Refinance With Private Credit
nodes.summary: The document is a handbook letter template used to follow up with a borrower about refinancing a Rural Housing Service (RHS) loan with private credit. It states that, after reviewing the borrower’s recent financial information, the agency determined that other credit is available at rates and terms the borrower may reasonably afford. The letter instructs the borrower to take positive steps to refinance within 90 days from the date of an earlier handbook letter and includes placeholders for borrower details, dates, and the approving official’s name and title.

nodes.title: Handbook Letter 114 (3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 2 SUBJECT: Unresponsive
nodes.summary: This partial document is a form letter from the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook about handling an “unresponsive” borrower regarding refinancing. It includes a template dated letter to borrowers who have not responded to a refinancing request, reminding them that they may be missing an opportunity to lower their mortgage interest rate by refinancing. The letter states that RHS loans can be refinanced at any time without penalty and that the loan remains subject to the refinancing requirement. It also contains fill-in blanks for borrower names and address information, as well as the approving official’s name and title.

nodes.title: HB-2-3550 Handbook Letter 115(3550) REFERENCE: Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook Chapter 6 SUBJECT: RESPONSE TO REQUEST TO SALE FOR LESS THAN THE DEBT
nodes.summary: This document is a USDA Rural Development sample letter used to respond to a borrower’s request to sell a property for less than the debt owed. It includes two possible outcomes: approval, where the agency consents to the sale and releases its first mortgage lien upon receipt of specified net proceeds but notes the borrower remains liable for any remaining debt; or denial, where the agency explains that it does not consent to the sale and provides space for reasons. The letter also contains placeholders for borrower information, property details, account number, date, and official signature, and references a debt settlement application for any remaining balance.

nodes.title: Sec. APPENDIX 4 7 CFR PART 11 --NATIONAL APPEALS DIVISION RULES OF PROCEDURE TABLE OF CONTENTS
nodes.summary: This partial document is the opening section of USDA’s National Appeals Division rules of procedure in 7 CFR Part 11. It provides the table of contents for the rules and begins the regulatory text with key definitions. The excerpt defines terms such as “adverse decision” and “agency,” explaining that adverse decisions include negative administrative actions, denials of equitable relief, or failures to act within required timeframes. It also lists the USDA agencies covered by these rules, such as ASCS, CCC, FSA, FmHA, FCIC, NRCS, RBS, RDA, RHS, and RUS, with some program-specific exceptions.

nodes.title: 11.1 Definitions.
nodes.summary: The partial document defines key terms used in the National Appeals Division regulations and begins the general statement of the part. It explains what counts as an adverse decision, which USDA agencies are covered, what constitutes agency records, appeals, appellants, authorized representatives, hearing records, and other procedural terms such as equitable relief, ex parte communication, hearing, hearing officer, implementation, participant, record review, and Secretary. It also lists major exclusions from the appeal process, including contract disputes, FOIA matters, personnel issues, tort claims, and discrimination complaints. The opening of § 11.2 states that the part establishes procedures for proceedings before the National Appeals Division, describing it as an independent organization within USDA under the Secretary’s general supervision.

nodes.title: 11.2 General statement.
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines the scope and procedures of the USDA National Appeals Division. It lists types of disputes excluded from certain appeal processes, defines “record review” and “Secretary,” and explains that the Division operates independently under the Secretary of Agriculture. It also states that program participants generally must exhaust Division review before seeking judicial review. Finally, it describes the kinds of adverse agency decisions covered by these regulations, including program participation, compliance, payments, and wetland or highly erodible land determinations, while noting that these proceedings are not governed by the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, or the Federal Rules of Evidence.

nodes.title: 11.3 Applicability.
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains the purpose and scope of the USDA administrative appeal procedures. It states that program participants must first seek review of adverse decisions before a Hearing Officer and may then appeal to the Director before going to court. It also describes what kinds of agency decisions are covered, including denial of program participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland or highly erodible land decisions. Finally, it notes important limits: the procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations, and several general legal rules, including the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence, do not apply to these proceedings.

nodes.title: 11.4 Inapplicability of other laws and regulations.
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the purpose and scope of a set of USDA administrative appeal regulations. It states that program participants must first seek review of adverse agency decisions through a Hearing Officer, and may then seek further review by the Director before going to court. It also outlines what kinds of decisions can be appealed, such as denial of program participation or benefits, compliance issues, payment determinations, and wetland or highly erodible land classifications. Finally, it notes important limits: these procedures cannot be used to challenge statutes or USDA regulations themselves, and certain general legal rules like the Administrative Procedure Act, the Equal Access to Justice Act, and the Federal Rules of Evidence do not apply to these proceedings.

nodes.title: 11.5 Informal review of adverse decisions.
nodes.summary: This section explains the informal review options a participant must or may use before appealing an adverse USDA agency decision to the National Appeals Division (NAD). It requires informal review for certain Farm Service Agency (FSA) field-level decisions, with review procedures handled by the relevant county or area committee and further review available by the State FSA committee or directly to NAD. It also allows optional informal review for State-level FSA decisions and decisions of other agencies, with references to the applicable CFR procedures. In addition, it describes the right to use mediation or other alternative dispute resolution programs before a NAD hearing and explains how requesting mediation affects appeal deadlines and hearing timelines.

nodes.title: 11.6 Director review of agency determination of appealability and right of participants to Division hearing.
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for appealing agency decisions within the Division. It covers how a participant can request Director review of an agency’s claim that a decision is not appealable, the Director’s authority to decide whether a matter is appealable and issue a final, non-appealable determination, and the Director’s ability to delegate that review. It also outlines the process and deadlines for participants to request hearings on adverse decisions, including requirements for written requests, required supporting information, and options such as record review. In addition, it describes requirements for authorized representatives to prove they are duly authorized to act for a participant. Finally, it sets out restrictions on ex parte communications, prohibiting off-the-record discussions about the merits of an appeal with interested persons and requiring any permitted substantive discussions to be shared with all parties and documented in the record.

nodes.title: 11.7 Ex parte communications.
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the procedures for filing an appeal and requesting a hearing, including required written content, signatures, supporting documents, and the option to request a record review instead of a hearing. It also describes how authorized representatives must prove they have written permission to act for a participant.

Another major section covers rules on ex parte communications during an appeal, prohibiting private merit-based communications with Division officers or employees, outlining limited exceptions, and requiring that any improper communications be included in the hearing record. It further explains possible consequences for violating these rules.

Finally, the document outlines general rules for Division hearings, including access to the agency record, the authority to administer oaths and issue subpoenas, and the timing and requirements for requesting subpoenas for witnesses or evidence.

nodes.title: 11.8 Division hearings.
nodes.summary:
This section outlines procedures for handling appeals and hearings within the Division. It covers rules on ex parte communications, including how improper communications must be added to the hearing record and how violations may affect a party’s claim. It then explains hearing procedures such as access to the agency record, authority to issue subpoenas, service and enforcement of subpoenas, and who pays related costs.

The document also details how hearings are conducted: deadlines for submitting documents, notice requirements, pre-hearing conferences, hearing format, evidence rules, recordkeeping, and procedures when a party fails to appear. It addresses post-hearing submissions, the lack of interlocutory review, the appellant’s burden of proof, and the timing for issuing determinations. Finally, it begins the process for Director review, setting deadlines and requirements for requests by appellants or agencies, notification of parties, and responses to review requests.

nodes.title: 11.9 Director review of determinations of Hearing Officers.
nodes.summary: The document describes the appeal and review process for hearing determinations. It explains when a Hearing Officer must issue a notice of determination after a hearing or record review, including deadlines and the requirement to provide procedures for requesting Director review. It then outlines § 11.9, covering how appellants and agencies may request Director review, the time limits for doing so, and the need to provide reasons and copies to other parties. The section also details notification and response procedures, the Director’s review standards and possible outcomes, deadlines for the Director’s decision, delegation of review authority, and the Director’s power to grant equitable relief.

nodes.title: 11.10 Basis for determinations.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains the rules governing how Hearing Officers and the Director make appeal determinations and what sources they must rely on. It states that they are not bound by prior factual findings from the agency’s adverse decision, but their decisions must be consistent with applicable agency laws, regulations, and general interpretations. It also requires that determinations be based only on the case record and the relevant laws and regulations in effect at the proper time.

The section then describes the process for reconsidering a Director’s determination. Either the appellant or the agency may request reconsideration within 10 days, but only if they identify a material factual error or explain how the decision conflicts with law or regulation. The Director must notify all parties whether the request meets these requirements, allow non-requesting parties 5 days to respond, and issue a reconsideration decision within 5 days after responses are received. If the reconsideration changes the outcome, that new decision becomes the final Director determination.

nodes.title: 11.11 Reconsideration of Director determinations.
nodes.summary: The document explains how appeal determinations are made and reviewed. It states that Hearing Officers and the Director are not restricted by prior factual findings, but their decisions must align with applicable laws, regulations, and interpretations, and must rely on the case record and relevant published regulations in effect at the proper time. It also outlines the process for requesting reconsideration of the Director’s determinations, including a 10-day filing deadline, required grounds based on material factual error or legal inconsistency, notification to other parties, their opportunity to respond, and the Director’s timeline for issuing a reconsideration decision. If reconsideration changes the original determination, that new decision becomes the final determination.

nodes.title: 11.12 Effective date and implementation of final determinations of the Division.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for implementing and challenging final determinations made by the Division, as well as filing deadlines and timing rules for appeals. It explains when an agency must carry out a final determination, when such determinations become effective, how judicial review may be sought in federal court, and that appellants must first obtain a final Division determination before seeking court review. It also defines when documents are considered filed, how deadlines are extended when they fall on non-business days, and the time of day by which filings must be received.

nodes.title: 11.13 Judicial review.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers administrative procedures for the Division’s final determinations, including when they become effective, how agencies must implement them, and how judicial review works. It explains that agency heads must act within 30 days after the effective date of a final determination, and that the effective date depends on the relevant statute or regulation, such as the filing date, transaction date, or date of the original adverse decision. It also states that final determinations are reviewable and enforceable in U.S. District Court, but only after the appellant has received a final determination from the Division. Finally, it describes filing rules for appeals and related documents, including what counts as “filed,” extensions when deadlines fall on weekends or holidays, and the 5:00 p.m. local time cutoff for submissions.

nodes.title: 11.14 Filing of appeals and computation of time.
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains several procedural rules for appeals and final determinations by a Division. It covers:

- **Effective date and implementation of final determinations (§ 11.12):** Agencies must implement a final determination within 30 days after it becomes effective, and the effective date depends on the applicable program rules—such as the filing date, transaction date, or original adverse decision date.

- **Judicial review (§ 11.13):** Final determinations can be reviewed and enforced by a U.S. District Court, but an appellant must first obtain a final determination from the Division before seeking judicial review.

- **Filing and time computation (§ 11.14):** It defines when documents are considered filed, allows deadline extensions when the final day falls on a weekend or federal holiday, and states that filing deadlines end at 5:00 p.m. local time at the relevant Division office.

nodes.title: HB-2-3550 APPENDIX 5 APPRAISAL GUIDELINES [7 CFR 3550.62]
nodes.summary: This partial document is an appraisal guidelines appendix that explains when the agency may need a property appraisal during servicing actions and outlines the basics of how property value is estimated. It lists situations that require an appraisal, such as determining subsidy recapture, evaluating partial release of security, deciding on a deed-in-lieu, confirming lien value, setting a foreclosure bid price, or assessing whether a protective advance is justified. It also introduces appraisal fundamentals, noting that value is influenced by factors like location, market conditions, construction quality, and amenities. The document briefly describes the sales comparison approach as a method that estimates value by comparing recent sales of similar properties and adjusting for differences.

nodes.title: A. SERVICING ACTIONS REQUIRING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines appraisal guidelines for USDA servicing actions, explaining when the Servicing Office may need a property appraisal and how appraisals are used in special servicing decisions. It lists situations requiring an appraisal, such as determining subsidy recapture, evaluating partial release requests, assessing deed-in-lieu offers, confirming lien value, setting foreclosure bid prices, and deciding on protective advances. It also introduces appraisal basics, describing the factors appraisers consider and the primary valuation methods used, including the sales comparison approach, the cost approach, and sometimes a broker’s price opinion.

nodes.title: B. APPRAISAL BASICS
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines appraisal guidelines for USDA Rural Development servicing actions. It explains when the Servicing Office must obtain an appraisal, such as for subsidy recapture, partial release of security, deed-in-lieu decisions, lien value determinations, foreclosure bid pricing, and protective advances. It summarizes the main appraisal methods used to estimate property value, including the sales comparison approach, the cost approach, and in limited cases a broker’s price opinion (BPO). The document also distinguishes between “as is” value and “as developed/improved” value, noting that “as is” value is used for most servicing actions. In addition, it describes procedures for ordering appraisals, including timelines for Field Staff and contract appraisers, and emphasizes the need for due diligence related to environmental condition. Finally, it provides special rules for appraisals in remote rural areas or on Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be unavailable, requiring use of Form 1007 and Agency staff with appraisal authority instead of contract appraisers.

nodes.title: C. APPRAISAL ORDERS
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains key appraisal methods and procedures used to determine property value. It describes the cost approach, where value is estimated from construction costs plus site value, and the BPO, a broker’s opinion of market value used only in limited cases. It also explains that appraisers may provide either an “as is” value or an “as developed/improved” value depending on the loan purpose, with “as is” usually used for servicing actions. The document then outlines appraisal ordering procedures, including who may order appraisals, expected completion timeframes for field staff and contract appraisers, and the need for due diligence in servicing activities involving market value. Finally, it addresses special appraisal rules for remote rural areas and Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be scarce, requiring use of Form 1007 and Agency staff with appraisal authority rather than contract appraisers, along with a definition of remote rural areas.

nodes.title: D. DUE DILIGENCE
nodes.summary: The partial document explains appraisal methods and procedures used for property valuation. It describes the cost approach and the BPO (broker price opinion), and notes that appraisers may provide either an “as is” value or an “as developed/improved” value depending on the loan purpose. It then outlines how appraisals are ordered, including options for Servicing Office staff or contract appraisers, and gives timeframes for completing appraisal orders. The document also covers due diligence requirements tied to appraisals. Finally, it addresses special rules for appraising new construction in remote rural areas or on Tribal lands, where comparable sales may be unavailable, requiring use of Form 1007 and limiting such appraisals to Agency staff with appraisal authority.

nodes.title: Appraisals in Remote Rural Areas or Tribal Lands
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains appraisal procedures and standards for rural housing servicing. It covers two appraisal methods: the cost approach, which estimates value based on construction cost plus site value, and the BPO, a broker opinion of market value used only in limited cases. It distinguishes between “as is” value and “as developed/improved” value, noting that “as is” is used for most servicing actions.

The document also describes how appraisals are ordered, either through Field Staff or contract appraisers, and gives timeframes for completion. It outlines due diligence requirements for environmental concerns, including use of the Transaction Screen Questionnaire and referral to an Environmental Coordinator if issues are found.

A major section addresses appraisals in remote rural areas and on Tribal lands, where comparable sales are limited. In those cases, the sales comparison approach is not required, and Form 1007/Marshall and Swift Square Foot Appraisal must be used by authorized Agency staff rather than contract appraisers.

Finally, the document explains how appraisals are reviewed. It details administrative reviews, which check completeness, math, comparables, and required valuation methods, and technical reviews, which assess the quality and support for the appraiser’s conclusions. It emphasizes that unacceptable appraisals must be redone before servicing can proceed.

nodes.title: E. REVIEWING APPRAISALS
nodes.summary: The partial document describes due diligence and appraisal review procedures for property transactions. It explains that due diligence involves checking for contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products, using the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528) as the first step, and referring any concerns to the Environmental Coordinator. It then outlines how servicing offices review appraisals to determine property value, including administrative reviews for completeness, accuracy, comparables, and valuation methods, as well as technical reviews for appraisal quality, support, and reasoning. It also states that appraisals found unacceptable must be redone before the servicing action continues, and that appraisal payment cannot be made until the review is complete.

nodes.title: 1. Administrative Review
nodes.summary: The partial document explains due diligence requirements for determining environmental contamination risks on a property, including use of the Transaction Screen Questionnaire (TSQ) and referral of concerns to an Environmental Coordinator. It then covers procedures for reviewing appraisals, stating that the Servicing Office must evaluate contract appraisals for property value and accuracy through administrative reviews and random spot-checks. It describes the administrative review process, including checks for completeness, math accuracy, current comparables, and use of both cost and comparable sales approaches, with a 7-day completion deadline and payment contingent on review completion. Finally, it outlines technical reviews, which assess the quality and support for the appraiser’s conclusions, are required for the first appraisal by each contract or fee appraiser, may be done after problems are found, and can also be conducted as random spot checks.

nodes.title: 2. Technical Review
nodes.summary: The partial document covers environmental due diligence and appraisal review procedures for property transactions. It explains that due diligence includes checking for contamination from hazardous substances, hazardous wastes, or petroleum products by completing a Transaction Screen Questionnaire (ASTM E-1528), and referring any concerns to an Environmental Coordinator. It then outlines how appraisals are reviewed by the Servicing Office, including administrative reviews and random spot-checks to verify completeness, mathematical accuracy, comparables, and valuation methods. It also describes technical reviews, which assess whether the appraisal reasoning and support are adequate, when they are required, and how unacceptable appraisals must be replaced before servicing can continue.

nodes.title: 3. Entering the Appraisal Results
nodes.summary: This section explains that once an appraisal is reviewed and accepted, the Servicing Office must enter the property’s appraised value into MortgageServ and use that value when deciding on the proposed servicing action. It also states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action requiring an appraisal, and that the fee is billed to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: F. PAYING FOR APPRAISALS [7 CFR 3550.62(b)]
nodes.summary: The document explains that after an appraisal review, if the appraisal is acceptable, the Servicing Office must enter the property’s appraised value into MortgageServ and use that value when deciding on the proposed servicing action. It also states that borrowers are charged a $280 fee for each servicing action that requires an appraisal, and this fee is added to the borrower’s account.

nodes.title: HB-2-3550 SECTION 1: INTRODUCTION TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document is an introductory section of the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook. It explains the handbook’s purpose: to provide Agency staff with guidance for servicing loans efficiently and effectively while helping borrowers and ensuring legal and administrative requirements are met. It states that the handbook covers loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants, with separate procedures provided elsewhere. The section also notes that the handbook is intended to align with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and Departmental/Agency regulations and does not override them. Finally, it describes how the handbook is organized for easy reference and begins explaining handbook symbols, including a reference symbol that points readers to additional sources such as laws, regulations, or instructions.

nodes.title: 1.1 WELCOME TO THE SERVICING AND ASSET MANAGEMENT OFFICE HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550). It explains that the handbook is designed to help Agency staff service loans efficiently and in compliance with legal and administrative requirements, with a focus on Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants. It also notes that detailed procedures are covered in a separate handbook and that the handbook must be used consistently with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and regulations. The document then explains how to use the handbook, stating that it is organized for easy reference by topic, intended both as a full read for new staff and a reference for experienced staff, and begins describing handbook symbols such as references to additional sources of information.

nodes.title: 1.2 USING THIS HANDBOOK
nodes.summary: The partial document is an introduction to the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook (HB-2-3550). It explains the handbook’s purpose: to give Agency staff guidance for servicing loans efficiently while meeting legal and administrative requirements, with emphasis on Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants. It notes that detailed procedures are provided in a separate handbook and that the guidance must remain consistent with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and Departmental regulations. The document also explains how to use the handbook, including that it is organized for easy reference by topic and includes symbols such as a book icon to point readers to additional sources like laws, regulations, and instructions.

nodes.title: A. Handbook Symbols
nodes.summary:
The partial document is the introduction to the Servicing and Asset Management Office Handbook. It explains the handbook’s purpose: to help agency staff service loans efficiently while meeting legal and administrative requirements, with a focus on loan servicing policies for Section 502 direct loans and Section 504 loans and grants. It also notes that detailed procedures are contained in a separate handbook and that the guidance must remain consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, and regulations.

The section also explains how to use the handbook. It is organized for easy reference, with new staff encouraged to read it fully and experienced staff using it as a reference. It describes the symbols and formatting conventions used throughout the handbook, including references, state supplements, civil rights indicators, deadlines, and documentation symbols. In addition, it explains citation styles for regulations, forms, handbook letters, helpful hint boxes, and example/exhibit text boxes.

nodes.title: B. Citations and Text Boxes
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to use the handbook and interpret its formatting and symbols. It describes state supplement indicators for laws that may affect program implementation, a fair housing symbol for civil rights-related procedures, underlining for deadlines, and a notepad symbol for required documentation. It also explains citation conventions for regulations, forms, and handbook letters, as well as the use of text boxes for helpful hints, cautions, examples, and exhibits.

nodes.title: C. Attachments and Appendices
nodes.summary: The passage explains how to use the handbook’s supporting materials and standardized terminology. It describes the glossary and acronym list at the end of the handbook, chapter-specific attachments, and appendices that contain forms and reference materials. It also defines key terms used throughout the handbook, including “Agency,” “Borrower,” “Servicing Office,” “Field Office,” and “Field Staff and Approval Official,” to ensure consistent meaning across different states and offices.

nodes.title: D. Terminology
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to use the handbook and outlines its structure. It describes the purpose of the glossary, acronyms list, attachments, and appendices, and defines standard terminology used throughout the handbook such as Agency, borrower, Servicing Office, Field Office, and Field Staff/Approval Official. It then summarizes the organization of the handbook by chapter, covering topics such as program overview, regular servicing, escrow/taxes/insurance, payment subsidies and income determinations, special servicing for delinquent borrowers, liquidation and acquisition through foreclosure, and special collections including unauthorized assistance, offsets, and debt settlement.

nodes.title: E. How This Handbook Is Organized
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines how HB-1-3550 is organized and summarizes the purpose of each chapter in the handbook. It explains that Chapter 1 covers Section 502 and Section 504 requirements plus general program and administrative policies. Chapter 2 addresses regular loan servicing, including borrower payments, approvals, final payments, and recapture. Chapter 3 focuses on escrow, taxes, and insurance responsibilities. Chapter 4 covers payment subsidies and income determinations. Chapter 5 discusses special servicing actions for delinquent borrowers such as workout agreements, moratoriums, protective advances, and reamortization. Chapter 6 explains liquidation and acquisition procedures when foreclosure is necessary. Chapter 7 covers special collections involving unauthorized assistance, offsets, and debt settlement.

nodes.title: HB-2-3550 SECTION 2: OVERVIEW OF THE DIRECT SINGLE FAMILY HOUSING PROGRAM
nodes.summary:
This partial document provides an overview of the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, focusing on its goals and the Section 502 direct loan program. It explains that the program is intended to help low- and very low-income people in rural areas obtain adequate, modest, decent, safe, and sanitary housing. The document highlights three main program goals: providing customer-friendly service, using public funds efficiently, and streamlining processing to save time and maximize benefits to borrowers.

It also introduces Section 502 loans, describing their purpose as helping eligible applicants acquire, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas. The section notes standard loan terms, possible shorter or longer terms in certain cases, and that loans are issued at a note rate set by the Agency. It also mentions that payment subsidies may be available and begins outlining income-based eligibility requirements for applicants, including adjusted income limits for standard, assumed, and REO property loans.

nodes.title: 1.3 GOALS OF THE DIRECT SINGLE FAMILY HOUSING PROGRAM
nodes.summary: The document section gives an overview of the USDA Direct Single Family Housing Program and its Section 502 direct loan program. It explains the program’s main goals: helping low- and very low-income rural residents obtain decent, safe, and sanitary homes; providing customer-friendly service; using public funds efficiently; and streamlining loan processing. It then outlines Section 502 loans, which can be used to acquire, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas, describes typical loan terms and note rates, notes the availability of payment subsidies, and begins listing basic eligibility requirements based on income limits.

nodes.title: 1.4 SECTION 502 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines the goals and basic overview of the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, especially Section 502 direct loans. It explains that the program helps low- and very low-income people obtain modest, decent, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas. The document emphasizes three main goals: providing customer-friendly service, using public funds efficiently, and streamlining loan processing.

It also gives an overview of Section 502 program loans, which can be used to acquire, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate homes in rural areas. The section notes standard loan terms, possible longer terms in some cases, the use of Agency-set interest rates, and the availability of payment subsidies for some borrowers. Finally, it begins listing eligibility requirements, including income limits for applicants and special rules for assumed loans or REO property purchases.

nodes.title: A. Program Loans
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides an overview of the USDA Rural Development Direct Single Family Housing Program, focusing on its goals, the Section 502 loan program, nonprogram loans, and the Section 504 loan and grant program. It explains that the program is designed to help low- and very low-income people obtain modest, decent, safe, and sanitary homes in rural areas, while emphasizing customer service, efficient use of public funds, and streamlined processing.

It outlines Section 502 direct loans, including their purpose to help eligible applicants acquire, build, repair, improve, or relocate homes; typical loan terms and note rate setting; and key eligibility requirements such as income limits, inability to obtain credit elsewhere, occupancy, citizenship status, legal capacity, and repayment ability. It also describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations such as loan assumptions or REO property purchases when applicants are not eligible for standard program loans.

Finally, the document summarizes Section 504 loans and grants, which assist homeowners with repairs and rehabilitation to address health and safety hazards. It highlights that Section 504 grants are reserved for eligible applicants age 62 or older and may be used to remove hazards or make homes accessible for persons with disabilities, with a lifetime grant limit.

nodes.title: B. Nonprogram Loans
nodes.summary:
The document section provides an overview of USDA Section 502 and Section 504 housing assistance programs. It explains the basic eligibility requirements for Section 502 borrowers, including inability to obtain other credit, intent to occupy the home, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity to take on the loan, and willingness and ability to repay. It also describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in special circumstances such as assuming an existing loan, purchasing an REO property, correcting unauthorized assistance, or continuing a loan after a partial release of security, and notes that these loans are only made when beneficial to the government.

The section then outlines the Section 504 direct loan and grant program, which helps very low-income homeowners repair, rehabilitate, or correct health and safety hazards in their homes when they cannot obtain other credit. It further explains Section 504 grants, which are limited to eligible applicants aged 62 or older and can only be used for repairs or improvements that remove health and safety hazards or make homes accessible for people with disabilities. The grant program has a lifetime maximum of $7,500.

nodes.title: 1.5 SECTION 504 -- AN OVERVIEW
nodes.summary: The document section provides an overview of USDA Section 502 and Section 504 housing programs. It outlines eligibility requirements for Section 502 loans, including inability to obtain other credit, personal occupancy of the dwelling, citizenship or eligible non-citizen status, legal capacity to incur debt, and willingness and ability to repay. It also explains nonprogram loans, which may be used in special situations such as assuming an existing loan or purchasing an REO property when a borrower is ineligible for Section 502, and notes that these loans are only made when in the government’s best interest. The section then summarizes Section 504 direct loans and grants, which help very low-income homeowners repair or rehabilitate homes and correct health and safety hazards. Finally, it describes Section 504 grants, including their use for hazard removal or accessibility improvements, age eligibility for applicants 62 and older, and the lifetime grant maximum of $7,500.

nodes.title: A. Section 504 Grants
nodes.summary:
The partial document provides an overview of USDA housing loan programs, focusing on eligibility requirements and loan types under Section 502, Section 504, and the responsibilities of field offices. It explains that Section 502 loans require borrowers to lack sufficient credit elsewhere, occupy the dwelling personally, meet citizenship and legal capacity requirements, and show willingness and ability to repay. It also describes nonprogram loans, which may be used in limited situations such as assuming existing loans or buying REO property when the borrower is not eligible for Section 502, and notes that these loans are only made when beneficial to the government.

The document then outlines the Section 504 direct loan and grant program, which helps low-income homeowners repair properties or remove health and safety hazards. Section 504 grants are limited to homeowners age 62 or older and may be used for hazard removal or accessibility improvements, with a lifetime maximum of $7,500. Section 504 loans can fund repairs and hazard removal, have a 20-year term, a 1 percent interest rate, and a maximum outstanding balance of $20,000 across multiple loans.

Finally, it explains the role of field offices in originating and closing loans, while servicing is handled by the Servicing Office after final disbursement. Field staff are also responsible for reporting risks to the security property or changes in borrower circumstances, such as hazardous conditions, abandonment, non-occupancy, or income changes, and may direct borrowers to the Servicing Office for help.

nodes.title: B. Section 504 Loans
nodes.summary: This section explains Section 504 loans and the role of field offices. It notes that Section 504 loan funds can be used for general repairs, improvements, and removal of health and safety hazards, with limits on total outstanding loan balance, term, and interest rate. It also describes how loans are originated and closed in Field Offices, then serviced by the Servicing Office, and outlines field staff responsibilities to report property risks or borrower changes and to help borrowers contact the Servicing Office when needed.

nodes.title: 1.6 THE ROLE OF FIELD OFFICES
nodes.summary: The passage covers two main topics: Section 504 loans and the role of field offices. It explains that Section 504 loan funds can be used for general repairs, improvements, and removing health and safety hazards, with limits on total outstanding balance, loan term, and interest rate. It also describes how field offices handle loan origination and closing, while servicing is done by the servicing office after closing, with field staff assisting when needed and reporting borrower or property issues such as hazards, abandonment, non-occupancy, or income changes.

nodes.title: HB-2-3550 SECTION 3: AUTOMATION
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is used to speed up loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two connected parts of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant information and tracks an application from pre-qualification through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage loan accounts, monitor performance, and record servicing actions. The document also notes that LoanServ helps Field Offices and the Servicing Office share information and coordinate servicing tasks through work queues, and it highlights that consistent use of LoanServ allows multiple servicing staff to work on the same borrower account over time.

nodes.title: 1.7 THE DEDICATED LOAN ORIGINATION AND SERVICING (DLOS) SYSTEM
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is designed to speed up loan processing, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It describes the two connected parts of DLOS: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system that stores applicant information and tracks applications from pre-qualification through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to manage Agency loans, monitor loan performance, and record servicing actions. The document also notes that information from UniFi is uploaded nightly into LoanServ, and that LoanServ can be used to share work and information between Servicing Office staff and Field Staff.

nodes.title: A. UniFi
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system, which is used to speed up loan-making, standardize recordkeeping, and improve communication between Field Offices and the Servicing Office. It explains that DLOS includes two linked systems: UniFi, a web-based loan origination tool that stores applicant information and tracks an application from pre-qualification through closing, and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to monitor loans and record servicing actions. The text also notes that LoanServ helps Field Staff and the Servicing Office share information, send work queues for assistance, and allows multiple servicing operators to work on the same borrower account over time.

nodes.title: B. LoanServ
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the Agency’s Dedicated Loan Origination and Servicing (DLOS) system and its purpose in supporting loan processing from application through servicing. It describes two connected components: UniFi, a web-based loan origination system used to store applicant information, perform calculations, and track application activities through closing; and LoanServ, a mainframe-based servicing system used to record servicing actions, monitor loan performance, and coordinate communication between the Servicing Office and Field Offices. The document also highlights how LoanServ work queues help staff share information and coordinate servicing tasks, and notes the advantage of consistent use of LoanServ so multiple staff can work on the same borrower account over time.

nodes.title: HB-2-3550 SECTION 4: GENERAL PROGRAM REQUIREMENTS
