Finally, it defines PRAS and explains that it arose because subsidy lowered the effective interest rate, accelerating principal paydown, making that amount subject to recapture.

nodes.title: A. Recapture Formulas
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how recapture owed is calculated for certain USDA/Rural Housing loans. It describes the automated recapture calculation, the role of the Final Payoff Worksheet and case study references, and the general formula for recapture based on the borrower’s equity at payoff. It explains that recapture may include Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy (PRAS) plus the lesser of total subsidy received or the portion of property value appreciation subject to recapture. It also distinguishes normal payoff situations from foreclosure or deed in lieu of foreclosure, where the full subsidy amount is recovered from the property only, not as personal borrower liability, and outlines the order in which sale proceeds are applied. Finally, it defines PRAS, explains that it applies to certain loans that received interest credit between 1979 and 1989, and notes why PRAS accumulated and remains subject to recapture.

nodes.title: B. Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how recapture owed on certain housing loans is calculated and what factors are included in that calculation. It says the calculation is automated, but servicers should understand it well enough to explain it to borrowers. It references a Final Payoff Worksheet and a case study for examples.

The document then outlines the recapture formula. In general, recapture is based on the borrower’s equity at payoff and includes Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy (PRAS) for certain loans, plus the lesser of the subsidy received or the portion of property value appreciation subject to recapture. It also explains that if there is no equity, PRAS is not collected. For foreclosure or deed in lieu of foreclosure, the full subsidy received is recaptured, excluding PRAS, and the recapture is limited to the property rather than being a personal liability. It also describes the order in which sale proceeds are applied: recoverable costs, accrued interest, principal, and then subsidy.

A major section is devoted to PRAS, explaining that it applies to Section 502 loans that received interest credit between October 1, 1979 and December 31, 1989. It describes how subsidy reduced the effective interest rate, accelerated principal paydown, and created PRAS as the difference between actual principal balance and what it would have been at the note rate. It notes that PRAS can continue to accumulate after subsidy ends, but the system later reduces it over time and loans made since January 1, 1990 do not have PRAS.

The final part explains value appreciation, which is used in recapture cases other than foreclosure and deed in lieu of foreclosure. It says borrowers may need to provide a current appraisal or sales contract to establish market value. It defines appreciation and provides a formula for calculating the portion subject to recapture, considering items such as prior liens, settlement costs, principal reduction at note rate, PRAS, original equity, capital improvements, outstanding loan balances, and recapture percentage.

nodes.title: C. Value Appreciation
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Agency calculates recapture owed on subsidized housing loans, focusing on Principal Reduction Attributable to Subsidy (PRAS) and value appreciation. It describes how PRAS accumulates, how it is reduced over time, and why loans made since January 1, 1990 do not have PRAS. The document then outlines the recapture formula for determining the portion of a property’s value appreciation subject to recapture, including the use of current market value, prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products, Agency loan payoff balances, FLP equity recapture if applicable, reasonable settlement costs, original equity, and capital improvements. It also explains how market value must be documented, which sources are acceptable for appraisals or value evidence, and which are not.

nodes.title: 1. Current Market Value
nodes.summary: This section explains how to calculate recapture owed when a property is sold or otherwise settled. It outlines the key elements used in the calculation: current market value, original amounts of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing assistance, balances owed on Agency loans, any applicable equity recapture from a Farm Loan Program loan, and reasonable settlement costs. It also describes acceptable ways to determine market value, including appraisals, sales contracts, assessed value, tax records, or other reliable evidence, and notes that broker price opinions are not acceptable.

nodes.title: 2. Original Amount of Prior Liens and Subordinate Affordable Housing Products
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed under HB-2-3550, focusing on the main components used in the calculation. It covers how current market value must be established, including acceptable evidence such as appraisals, sales contracts, assessed values, or tax records, and notes that broker’s price opinions are not acceptable. It also describes the role of the original amount of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products in determining original equity, the balance owed on Agency loans, any applicable equity recapture from a prior Farm Loan Program loan, and reasonable settlement costs that may be deducted if they are actual, customary, and directly paid by the borrower.

nodes.title: 3. Balance to be Paid Off on Agency Loans
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed under HB-2-3550, focusing on the elements used in the calculation. It covers determining current market value using acceptable evidence such as appraisals, sales contracts, tax records, or other reliable sources; the role of original prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products in establishing original equity; the balance owed on Agency loans including principal, interest, fees, and advances; possible inclusion of recapture from a previously accelerated Farm Loan Program loan; and allowable reasonable settlement costs directly paid by the borrower.

nodes.title: 4. Equity Recapture Due from Farm Loan Program Loan (if applicable)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a property by identifying the key components used in the calculation. It covers determining current market value through acceptable evidence such as appraisals, sales contracts, assessed values, or tax records; using the original amount of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products that affected the borrower’s original equity; totaling all amounts owed to the Agency on the loan, including fees and advances; including any equity recapture related to a previously accelerated Farm Loan Program loan if applicable; and accounting for reasonable, actual settlement costs directly paid by the borrower in a sale or refinance.

nodes.title: 5. Reasonable Settlement Costs
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a Rural Housing Service loan. It covers the key components used in the recapture formula, including current market value, original amounts of prior liens and subordinate affordable housing products, the balance owed on Agency loans, possible equity recapture from a prior Farm Loan Program loan, reasonable settlement costs, principal reductions, original equity, and credits for capital improvements. It also describes acceptable methods for determining market value, required documentation, and how original equity is calculated based on property type and loan approval value.

nodes.title: 6. Principal Reductions
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed under HB-2-3550, focusing on three key components: principal reductions, original equity, and capital improvements. It defines principal reduction at note rate as the borrower’s actual RHS loan principal paid, excluding subsidy-related payments, and refers to PRAS for additional treatment. It then describes how original equity is determined using the property’s market value at loan approval, with rules for subtracting prior liens and certain loans, and explains how market value is defined for self-help loans and other transaction types. Finally, it states that borrowers must identify capital improvements to receive credit, and that an appraiser—not the improvement cost—determines the value added to the property for recapture calculations.

nodes.title: 7. Original Equity
nodes.summary: This excerpt from HB-2-3550, Paragraph 2.23 on calculating recapture owed, explains how to determine key factors used in the recapture calculation. It covers principal reductions, including what counts as principal paid at the note rate and excluding payment assistance subsidy amounts; original equity, including how it is calculated from market value at loan approval and what deductions apply; and capital improvements, including how borrowers must request appraisal of improvements and how their value is determined based on added property value rather than cost.

nodes.title: 8. Capital Improvements
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains how recapture owed is calculated by defining key components used in the computation. It covers principal reductions, including what counts as principal paid at the note rate and what is excluded as payment assistance subsidy. It then defines original equity, explaining how it is determined from market value at the time of loan approval and how liens and certain costs are treated. Finally, it describes capital improvements, noting that borrowers must identify them, that an appraiser determines their value based on added property value rather than cost, and that borrowers must request this appraisal service to receive credit.

nodes.title: 9. Amount Due if There is No Value Appreciation
nodes.summary: This document section explains how to calculate recapture owed on a loan payoff using the Final Payoff Worksheet. It covers cases where there is no value appreciation, how to determine the share of appreciation attributable to loans subject to recapture based on outstanding balances, how the recapture percentage is set by loan term and subsidized interest rate, how borrower equity return is calculated, and how Part IV computes the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture, including the range of recapture factors and credit for original borrower equity.

nodes.title: 10. Percentage of Outstanding Balance of Loans (Percentage of Total Debt Subject to Recapture to be Paid-Off)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a loan payoff using the Final Payoff Worksheet. It covers: when no value appreciation has occurred, how to compute the portion of debt subject to recapture based on the outstanding balance of recapturable loans versus all open loans, how the recapture percentage is determined from loan term and subsidized interest rate, how to calculate the borrower’s return on equity, and how to determine the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture, including the range of recapture percentages and credit for the borrower’s original equity.

nodes.title: 11. Recapture Percentage
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on an Agency loan payoff using the Final Payoff Worksheet. It covers how to determine the amount due when there is no value appreciation, how to allocate value appreciation based on the proportion of outstanding loans subject to recapture, how the recapture percentage is set from loan term and subsidized interest rate, how borrower equity return is calculated, and how Part IV determines the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture using a recapture factor and credit for the borrower’s original equity.

nodes.title: 12. Return on Borrower’s Equity
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a loan payoff using the Final Payoff Worksheet. It covers situations with no value appreciation, how to determine the portion of outstanding loan balance subject to recapture, how the recapture percentage is set based on loan duration and subsidized interest rate, how to calculate return on the borrower’s equity, and how to determine the portion of property value appreciation subject to recapture.

nodes.title: 13. Portion of Value Appreciation Subject to Recapture
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how subsidy recapture is calculated when a borrower pays off a Rural Housing loan, including cases with no value appreciation and cases where the property has increased in value. It outlines the worksheet-based formulas used to determine the portion of appreciation subject to recapture, the recapture percentage based on loan term and subsidized interest rate, and how the borrower’s original equity affects the final amount due. It also describes the overall payoff calculation when appreciation exists, combining loan repayment, FLP equity recapture, PRAS, and subsidy recapture.

The document then moves into the process for collecting recapture information, stating that subsidy recapture is calculated when a final payoff is requested or a loan is paid in full. It explains that the Servicing Office typically handles the process, first issuing a statement of loan balance and then requiring supporting documentation such as an appraisal or arm’s-length sales contract before preparing the final payoff statement. It also specifies the key information needed for a final payoff statement, including the payoff date and documented market value of the property.

nodes.title: 14. Amount Due if There is Value Appreciation
nodes.summary: The document explains how to calculate recapture owed on a loan payoff, including how value appreciation affects the amount due and how the final payoff worksheet combines loan repayment, FLP equity recapture, PRAS, and recaptured subsidy. It also describes the process for collecting recapture information when a borrower requests payoff: the Servicing Office initially provides a loan balance statement, then requests documentation such as an appraisal or arm’s-length sales contract to determine subsidy recapture, and finally issues a final payoff statement. It notes that field offices are usually not involved and specifies the requirements for a payoff with a final payoff statement, including the payoff date and proof of market value, with broker price opinions not accepted.

nodes.title: COLLECTING RECAPTURE INFORMATION
nodes.summary: This document section explains how to calculate recapture owed when a borrower repays an Agency loan, especially when the property has appreciated in value. It describes how the final payoff amount is built in the Final Payoff Worksheet by combining the loan balance with other amounts such as equity recapture, PRAS, and the lesser of subsidy received or the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture. It also outlines the process for collecting recapture information: subsidy recapture is determined when a borrower requests a final payoff or pays the account in full, and the Servicing Office typically requests required documentation such as the payoff date and proof of market value through an appraisal or arm’s length sales contract.

nodes.title: A. Payoff With Final Payoff Statement
nodes.summary:
The excerpt explains how subsidy recapture is calculated and collected when a borrower pays off an Agency loan. It describes the components included in the final payoff amount, such as the loan balance, FLP equity recapture, PRAS, and the lesser of subsidy received or the portion of value appreciation subject to recapture.

It then outlines the process for collecting recapture information. When a borrower requests a payoff statement, the Servicing Office issues an initial balance statement and then requires documentation such as the payoff date, current market value, evidence of capital improvements, and settlement statements before calculating the final amount due. It notes that broker price opinions are not acceptable for market value and that capital improvements must be documented through an appraisal addendum.

The passage also covers situations where a borrower pays off the loan without first contacting the Servicing Office. In those cases, the borrower receives a “Congratulations” letter and instructions for calculating recapture, while the Agency determines market value using appraisal, sales contract, tax, assessed value, or other reliable evidence. It further explains that if an appraisal is needed, the Agency pays for it, and borrowers may submit their own appraisal if they disagree. Finally, it begins a discussion of acceptable methods for determining market value, including abbreviated appraisals and assessed values.

nodes.title: B. Payoff Without Contacting the Servicing Office for a Final Payoff Statement, or Final Installment Payment
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how subsidy recapture information is collected and how market value is determined when a borrower pays off a loan without first obtaining a final payoff statement. It covers:

- Documentation of capital improvements through an appraisal addendum, with receipts allowed only as supporting evidence of what improvements were made.
- Documentation of settlement costs for refinancing or sale transactions using lender good faith estimates or closing-agent settlement statements, with itemized borrower-paid costs required.
- The process used when a borrower pays off a loan without contacting the servicing office, including issuance of a “Congratulations” letter and instructions for calculating subsidy recapture.
- Agency responsibility for establishing market value in these cases, using appraisals, sales contracts, assessed values, tax records, or other reliable evidence.
- Borrower rights to submit an independent appraisal if they disagree with the Agency’s valuation.
- Guidance on acceptable methods for determining market value, including abbreviated or limited appraisals and assessed value when appropriate.

nodes.title: 1. Determining Market Value
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to collect information needed to calculate subsidy recapture when a borrower pays off a loan or sells/refinances a property. It covers:

- How to document the added value of capital improvements through an appraisal addendum, and that receipts alone are not enough to establish value.
- How settlement costs may be credited if supported by a good faith estimate or estimated settlement statement, and what documentation is acceptable.
- What happens when a borrower pays off a loan without first requesting a final payoff statement: the Servicing Office sends a “Congratulations” letter and the Agency determines market value for recapture purposes.
- Acceptable ways to determine market value, including appraisals, sales contracts, assessed value, tax records, or other reliable evidence.
- Procedures for using appraisals and addenda when capital improvements are involved, including who pays the appraisal cost.
- The notification and coordination process between the Servicing Office and field offices, including task assignments, timelines, borrower response periods, and how to submit recapture calculations.

nodes.title: 2. Borrower Notification – Servicing Office and Field Office Interface
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how the Agency collects and processes recapture information when a borrower pays off a loan. It covers how market value is established, including when appraisals or written value statements are needed, who pays appraisal costs, and how capital improvements must be documented. It also describes the communication workflow between the Servicing Office and Field Office, including notification tasks, timelines, borrower contact, and submission of supporting documents for recapture calculation.

The document then outlines what happens after recapture is calculated: the borrower is notified and given 120 days to either pay the recapture amount or defer it, with a possible 25% discount if paid promptly while retaining and occupying the property. Finally, it explains the deferral process, including when deferral is allowed, how a recapture receivable account is set up, annual statement requirements, subordination rules for refinancing with private credit, and the incentive of offering a discount to reduce long-term receivable tracking.

nodes.title: DEFERRING PAYMENT OF RECAPTURE
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how recapture amounts are handled when a borrower repays a loan, including calculation, notification, and payment options. It describes that the Servicing Office sends a letter giving the borrower 120 days to pay or defer the recapture amount, and that a 25% discount may apply if the borrower pays within that period while keeping title and occupying the property. It also covers the process for marking accounts as recapture receivables and preparing lien releases when paid in full.

The document further outlines deferring recapture payment, including the conditions under which deferral is allowed: the borrower must keep title and continue occupying the property. It explains that deferred recapture becomes an interest-free receivable account, payable when the borrower stops occupying the property or transfers title, and that annual statements must be sent. In addition, it discusses refinancing with private credit, the Agency’s ability to subordinate its interest under certain value limits, and the incentive of a 25% discount to encourage borrowers to pay recapture instead of deferring it.

nodes.title: A. Refinancing with Private Credit
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for collecting and deferring loan recapture amounts in the Agency’s servicing process. It states that the Servicing Office calculates the recapture due, notifies the borrower, allows 120 days for payment or deferral, and gives a 25% discount if the borrower pays within that period while keeping title and occupying the property. It also notes that the case file must be marked as a recapture receivable account and, if paid in full, the field office prepares the release of lien.

The document then describes conditions for deferring recapture payment. Borrowers may defer if they continue to occupy the property and title does not transfer, commonly when refinancing or paying off the final installment. In such cases, an interest-free recapture receivable account is set up, annual statements are required, and payment becomes due if the borrower moves or transfers title.

Finally, it covers refinancing with private credit and the Agency’s subordination rules, explaining that the Agency may subordinate its interests if the combined prior lien debt and recapture do not exceed property value, subject to limits on what can be included. It also discusses the purpose of offering a 25% discount as an incentive to reduce long-term recapture receivable accounts, particularly for borrowers refinancing while remaining in the home.

nodes.title: B. Incentive to Pay Recapture
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how the Agency handles recapture payments after a borrower repays or refinances a loan, including:

- How the Servicing Office calculates recapture due and notifies the borrower, giving 120 days to pay or defer.
- The 25% discount available if recapture is paid within 120 days while the borrower keeps title and continues living in the property.
- How to set up and manage an interest-free recapture receivable account when payment is deferred.
- Conditions under which the Agency may subordinate its interest to allow refinancing with private credit.
- Rules for when the recapture discount may or may not be offered, especially for borrowers who sell, transfer title, or stop occupying the home.
- Special handling for Trust accounts from the 1987 rural housing asset sale, including reassignment to the Agency for deferred recapture and recordation procedures.

nodes.title: TRUST ACCOUNTS
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for deferring payment of recapture on certain rural housing loans and explains the handling of trust accounts. It states that borrowers who sell their homes, transfer title, or stop occupying the property may be subject to recapture, and that in some cases they can receive a 25 percent discount if payment is made within 120 days of receiving notice of the recapture amount. It also explains that some former Agency loans were transferred to a Trust but remain subject to recapture, and that if a Trust borrower wants to defer recapture payment, the account must be reassigned back to the Agency so the Servicing Office can set up the receivable. The section further notes procedures for sending reassigned documents to the Field Office for recording and specifies that related recordation costs are non-recoverable.

nodes.title: FINAL PAYMENT AMOUNT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the USDA Rural Housing final payment process for loans. It covers how the final payment amount is determined, including the outstanding account balance, fees, protective advances, equity recapture, unauthorized assistance, and subsidy recapture, and notes that an account is not closed until all required amounts are paid. It also explains that borrowers with loans approved before October 1, 1979 are exempt from subsidy recapture, and that if the Agency approves a debt settlement, the account is satisfied once the settlement amount is repaid. In addition, it describes how remaining escrow or supervised bank account funds are returned separately to the borrower. The document further outlines the procedure for obtaining payoff statements, including who may request them, required authorization, the Servicing Office’s role in issuing written statements, the prohibition on giving payoff amounts by telephone, and how Field Offices may request payoffs.

nodes.title: PAYOFF STATEMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Development loan final payment procedures. It defines the final payment amount as the outstanding loan balance plus recapture and other possible charges, and notes when a loan is considered paid in full. It also describes how escrow or supervised bank account funds are returned separately. The section then covers payoff statements, explaining that borrowers should request a written payoff statement from the Servicing Office before paying off a loan, that payoff amounts are not given by phone, and that different request methods exist for borrowers and field offices. Finally, it distinguishes between two types of payoff statements: a loan balance statement showing current balances and subsidy history, and a final payoff statement giving the expected amount due on a specific payoff date based on recapture and other provided information.

nodes.title: A. Statement of Loan Balance
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payoff statements are handled for a loan when the final payment amount cannot be determined in advance because it depends on the payment date and any recapture that may be owed. It distinguishes between two types of statements: a Statement of Loan Balance, which shows the current loan balance and provides instructions for submitting documents needed to calculate recapture, and a Final Payoff Statement, which gives the actual amount due based on the proposed payoff date and borrower-provided information. It also notes that once the needed information is received, the Servicing Office issues the final payoff statement, and if the borrower later updates details, a revised final payoff statement is generated.

nodes.title: B. Final Payoff Statement
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how payoff statements are handled for certain loans, especially when final payment depends on timing and subsidy recapture. It distinguishes between two statement types: a statement of loan balance, which shows the current outstanding loan amounts and gives the borrower information needed to calculate recapture; and a final payoff statement, which estimates the exact amount due on a proposed payoff date and is updated if new information is provided.

It also covers processing final payments. Payments are date-stamped upon receipt, and the actual payoff amount is recalculated based on that date. If the amount received is too low, the payment is held in suspense until the borrower provides the remaining funds; if the borrower overpays, the excess is also addressed. After the correct amount is received, the office must close out the account by releasing security documents when appropriate, with instructions varying by state. If the loan is paid in full including subsidy recapture, security instruments are generally released; if recapture is deferred, the security instruments remain in place and may be subordinated instead.

nodes.title: PROCESSING FINAL PAYMENTS
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains how final loan payments are processed and how payoff discrepancies are handled. It says payments are date-stamped when received, the payoff amount is recalculated based on that date, and if the payment is too low the funds are held in suspense until the borrower pays the difference and a new payoff statement is issued. It also notes that once the correct payoff amount is received, the Servicing Office must close out the account by arranging for release of security documents, with instructions varying by state. If the loan is paid in full including subsidy recapture, security interests are released; if subsidy recapture is deferred, the security documents are not released and may instead be subordinated.

nodes.title: A. Release Security Documents
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Servicing Office should process final loan payments. It says payments are date-stamped when received and the payoff amount is recalculated based on that date. If the payment is too low, it is held in suspense until the borrower provides the remaining funds and a new payoff statement is issued; if there is an overpayment, the office handles the excess accordingly. After the correct payoff amount is received, the office must close the account by releasing security documents, notifying the insurance company that the Agency’s interest has ended, terminating billing and preauthorized debit requests, and ending any assignments of income by forwarding future checks to the borrower and notifying the payer.

nodes.title: B. Release Insurance
nodes.summary: The passage outlines tasks the Servicing Office must complete after a loan is paid in full. These include notifying the property insurance company that the Agency’s interest in the property has ended, stopping billing statements and preauthorized debit requests, and ending assignments of income by forwarding any received checks to the borrower and informing the sender to pay the borrower directly.

nodes.title: C. Terminate Billing and Preauthorized Debits
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the servicing office procedures for processing final loan payments. It covers releasing insurance by notifying the property insurance company that the Agency’s interest has ended, terminating billing and preauthorized debits once a loan is paid off, and ending assignments of income by forwarding any received checks to the borrower and informing the sender to direct future payments to the borrower.

nodes.title: D. Terminate Assignments of Income
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the servicing office’s responsibilities when a borrower has fully paid off a loan. It covers notifying the property insurance company that the Agency no longer has an interest in the property, stopping billing statements and preauthorized debit requests, and ending income assignment arrangements by forwarding any received checks to the borrower and telling the sender to make future payments directly to the borrower.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 2-A
nodes.summary:
The document is a “Final Payoff Worksheet” used to calculate the amount owed when an RHS loan is being paid off, including any recapture of subsidy or equity appreciation. It is organized into five parts:

- **Part I: Value Appreciation** — Calculates whether the property has gained value after subtracting prior liens, loan payoffs, recapture amounts, settlement costs, principal reductions, subsidy-related reductions, original equity, and capital improvements.
- **Part II: Amount Due if There Is No Value Appreciation** — Determines the payoff amount when no appreciation exists, including loan payoff, FLP equity recapture, and PRAS collection.
- **Part III: Percentage of Total Debt Subject to Recapture to Be Paid Off** — Used for leveraged loans or cases where only part of the debt is subject to recapture, to compute the share of loans being repaid.
- **Part IV: Value Appreciation Subject to Recapture** — Computes the portion of appreciation that is attributable to recapturable loans and adjusts it by the recapture percentage and original equity percentage.
- **Part V: Amount Due if There Is Value Appreciation** — Calculates the final payment amount, including payment subsidy received, recapture amount, possible discounted recapture, and final payoff total.

Overall, the worksheet provides a step-by-step formula for determining the final payoff amount and any recaptured subsidy or appreciation owed.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 2-B
nodes.summary:
This partial document is a subsidy recapture case study showing how to calculate the final payoff amount for the Potter family when they sell their home. It explains the full situation: the original home purchase, the Section 502 loan and payment subsidy history, a subordinate loan taken for roof repairs, capital improvements such as a deck, and the planned sale of the house with associated closing costs.  

The document then identifies the key inputs needed for two calculations: value appreciation and recapture. These include the current market value, outstanding loan balances, unreimbursed selling costs, principal reduction, original equity, capital improvement credit, the share of debt subject to recapture, and the recapture percentage from the subsidy repayment agreement.  

Finally, it presents a detailed worksheet with step-by-step calculations. It computes value appreciation, determines the portion subject to recapture, applies the subsidy received, and arrives at the final payoff amount due from the Potters.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 2-C
nodes.summary:
This partial document is a USDA servicing attachment that explains how Field Offices should handle borrowers who have both Rural Housing Service (RHS) loans and Farm Service Agency (FSA) Farm Credit Program (FCP) loans. It covers the background for splitting servicing responsibilities after agency reorganization, the purpose of the guidance, and contact points for questions.

Main points covered include:
- How to separate borrower case files into RHS and FSA files, including which original documents stay with FSA and which copies or originals move to RHS.
- How to flag accounts and records so both agencies can identify joint security instruments and cross-collateralized loans.
- Guidance on releasing additional security and handling mortgages or deeds of trust that involve both agencies.
- Insurance policy requirements so both RHS and FSA are properly listed as mortgagees and receive notices.
- Special servicing rules for RHF loans, which are RHS-funded farm building loans now serviced by FSA, including identification, transfer procedures, and loan servicing treatment.
- General servicing procedures such as payoff requests and ensuring the borrower is directed to the correct agency for each loan.

nodes.title: 3 RHF Loans ( Continued)
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures for handling Rural Housing/Farm Service (RHF) loans and general servicing steps. It explains how to identify RHF loans by requesting transfer to FSA and changing fund and kind codes, then outlines how RHF loans are serviced: they are not counted as Farm Ownership loans for FO limits, may be included in DALR$ as fund code 46 for restructuring, use the lower of the original note rate or current RH loan rate, can be amortized up to 33 years from the original note date, and require Form RD 1940-17. It also covers a general servicing rule for payoff requests when FSA and RHS are joint creditors, stating that the contacted agency must provide payoff information and inform the requester that the other agency also has a loan and how to obtain its payoff information.

nodes.title: A Identifying RHF Loans (Continued)
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures for handling RHF loans and general servicing guidance. It explains how to identify RHF loans by requesting transfer to FSA and changes to fund and kind codes, and how RHF loans are serviced in future Farm Ownership lending and restructuring, including how they are counted, reported in DALR$, interest rate treatment, amortization terms, and required promissory note form. It also covers a general servicing procedure for payoff requests when FSA and RHS are joint creditors, directing the contacted agency to provide payoff information and inform the requester about obtaining payoff details from the other agency as well.

nodes.title: B Servicing RHF Loan
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines procedures for handling RHF loans and general servicing. It explains how to identify RHF loans by requesting transfer to FSA and changes to fund and kind codes. It also describes servicing rules for RHF loans, including how they are treated in relation to Farm Ownership loan limits, how they are recorded for restructuring, interest rate and amortization terms, and the requirement for the borrower to sign a promissory note. Finally, it covers general servicing procedures for payoff requests when FSA and RHS are joint creditors, stating that the contacted agency should provide payoff information and direct the requester to the other agency for its loan payoff details.

nodes.title: 4 General Servicing Procedures
nodes.summary: This partial document covers procedures for Rural Housing Farm (RHF) loans and general servicing steps. It explains how to identify RHF loans by requesting transfer to FSA and changes to fund and kind codes. It also outlines how RHF loans are handled in future Farm Ownership lending and servicing, including that they do not count toward FO loan limits, may be considered in DALR$ as fund code 46 for restructuring, are charged the lower of the original or current RH loan rate, can be amortized up to 33 years from the original note date, and require signing Form RD 1940-17. Finally, it describes payoff request procedures for joint FSA and RHS creditors, stating that the contacted agency should provide payoff information and direct the requester to the other agency for the remaining loan payoff details.

nodes.title: A Payoff Requests
nodes.summary: The partial document covers servicing rules and procedures for Rural Housing/Farm Service (RHF) loans and joint FSA/RHS credit situations. It explains how to identify RHF loans by transferring them to FSA and changing fund/kind codes, and how RHF loans are treated in servicing, including exclusion from Farm Ownership loan limits, use in DALR$ when restructuring, interest rate rules, amortization terms, and required promissory note signing. It also outlines general servicing procedures for joint creditors, such as handling payoff requests, keeping refinancing and classification separate by agency, and managing partial releases, subordinations, and consents for security instruments covering both RHS and FSA loans, including appraisal requirements, approval coordination, and denial if both agencies cannot approve.

nodes.title: B Refinancing and Classification
nodes.summary: The partial document describes general servicing procedures for jointly held RHS and FSA loans, focusing on two main areas. First, it explains that refinancing and account classification are handled separately by each agency based only on its own loans, though financial documents may be shared upon request. Second, it outlines procedures for partial releases, subordinations, and consents involving joint security instruments: both agencies must approve, only one Form RD 465-1 is needed, the receiving agency leads processing and obtains any required appraisals, and if both agencies cannot approve the request, it is denied. It also notes that responses may be issued jointly or separately by the agencies.

nodes.title: C Partial Releases, Subordinations, and Consents for Joint Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines general servicing procedures for loans involving both RHS and FSA. It explains that refinancing and classification are handled separately by each agency, though related financial documents may be shared. It describes how partial releases, subordinations, and consents for joint security instruments require approval from both agencies, with one agency leading the request, obtaining appraisals, and coordinating responses. It also covers the release of joint security instruments when one agency’s debt is paid off before the other, including how original documents and satisfaction letters are exchanged and how promissory notes are returned. Finally, it explains lien position determination when RHS and FSA loans were made at the same time, stating that lien rights are shared proportionately based on the debt owed and referencing a guide for calculating each agency’s share of security value or proceeds.

nodes.title: D Release of Joint Security Instruments
nodes.summary: This partial document covers general servicing procedures for joint security instruments involving both FSA and Rural Development (RHS) loans. It explains how security instruments are released when one agency’s debt is satisfied before the other, including required approvals, transfer of original documents, and handling of promissory notes and case file records. It also describes how lien position is determined when RHS and FSA loans are made at the same time and secured by the same mortgage or deed of trust, stating that the lien is shared proportionately based on the debt owed. The section references a guide for calculating the proportionate share of security value or proceeds for cases such as sales, foreclosures, voluntary conveyances, and insurance losses.

nodes.title: E Determining Lien Position When Both RHS and FSA Loan Were Made at the Same Time
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines general servicing procedures for joint RHS and FSA loans. It explains how to release joint security instruments when one agency’s debt is satisfied before the other, including required state office approvals, transfer of original documents, handling of promissory notes, and case file documentation. It also describes how lien position is determined when RHS and FSA loans are made at the same time, with shared lien rights based on the proportion of debt owed. In addition, it covers how offsets are processed between the agencies according to lien priority, delinquency date, and prorated shares when priorities are equal. Finally, it explains how insurance losses on jointly secured property are handled, including decisions about repairs, inspections, approval of proceeds, and distribution of insurance payments based on lien priority.

nodes.title: F Offsets
nodes.summary: The partial document covers general servicing procedures for joint loans handled by two agencies, focusing on how offset payments and insurance-related losses are managed. It explains that administrative and IRS offsets are distributed according to lien priority, delinquency date, or prorated share when priorities are equal, with review and processing by agency staff. It also describes how losses covered by insurance are handled, including which agency oversees repairs, how inspections and plans are shared, how insurance proceeds are applied based on lien priority, and that both agencies must approve any release of proceeds for jointly secured property.

nodes.title: G Losses Covered by Insurance
nodes.summary: The document section outlines servicing procedures for joint loans and cross-collateralized accounts involving the Farm Service Agency (FSA) and Rural Housing Service (RHS). It explains how offsets are handled, including lien priority and prorated distribution when both agencies have equal claims, and how IRS offsets are allocated based on delinquency date and borrower debt. It also covers insurance-related losses, requiring both agencies to coordinate on repairs, inspections, and approval of insurance proceeds, with responsibility generally following lien priority. The section describes ADPS inquiry screens and transaction processing, noting that some transactions affect both agencies and must be coordinated to avoid duplication. It also addresses labor housing loans and states that each agency is responsible for servicing its own loans, but collaboration is required when loans are cross-collateralized to protect the government’s security interest.

nodes.title: H ADPS Inquiry Screens and Processing of Transactions
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses general servicing procedures for RHS and FSA loans, focusing on how both agencies access inquiry screens and coordinate transaction processing, especially for transactions that affect both accounts. It explains servicing rules for Labor Housing (LH) loans, noting that RHS typically handles them unless the security is shared with an FCP loan. It then outlines responsibilities for servicing delinquent and financially distressed accounts, emphasizing that each agency services its own loans but must cooperate when loans are cross-collateralized to protect the government’s security interest.

nodes.title: I Labor Housing Loans
nodes.summary: The document section outlines general servicing procedures for shared USDA loan programs, focusing on how the Farm Service Agency (FSA) and Rural Housing Service (RHS) coordinate loan servicing. It explains that both agencies retain access to inquiry screens for RHS and FCP loans, and that most ADPS transactions are handled separately unless they affect both accounts, such as flags, acquisitions, and name/address changes, which require coordination to prevent duplication. It also notes that Labor Housing (LH) loans are usually serviced by the RHS official managing the Multi-Family Housing program, except when the same security backs both FCP and LH loans, in which case releases and liquidations follow the procedures in the attachment. Finally, the section addresses servicing of delinquent and financially distressed accounts, stating that each agency is responsible for its own loans but must cooperate on cross-collateralized loans to protect the government’s security interest, especially when both loans are secured by the same property or security instruments.

nodes.title: 5 Servicing Delinquent and Financially Distressed Accounts
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines servicing procedures for shared USDA loan programs handled by FSA and RHS. It explains that both agencies retain access to inquiry screens, but most ADPS transactions are processed separately unless they affect both accounts, such as flags, acquisitions, and name/address changes, which require coordination to prevent duplication or future issues. It also notes how Labor Housing loans are generally serviced by RHS, except when the same security covers both FCP and LH loans, in which case releases and liquidations follow the attachment’s rules. The section on delinquent and financially distressed accounts states that each agency is responsible for servicing its own loans, but when loans are cross-collateralized, the agencies must coordinate to protect the government’s security interest, with the level of cooperation depending on whether both loans are listed on the same security instruments.

nodes.title: A Debt Servicing Responsibilities
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes servicing procedures for USDA housing and farm credit loans, focusing on how the RHS and FSA agencies handle inquiries, transaction processing, and delinquent or financially distressed accounts. It explains that both agencies can access inquiry screens for both loan types, but some ADPS transactions affect both accounts and must be coordinated to avoid duplication. It also notes special handling for Labor Housing loans, especially when shared security is involved.

The main section covers debt servicing responsibilities when loans are cross-collateralized. It distinguishes between cases where security is not cross-collateralized, where each agency services its own loans under its own regulations and instructions, and cases where security is cross-collateralized. For cross-collateralized loans on separate security instruments, normal debt servicing follows the general rules, while foreclosures and conveyances follow a different paragraph. When RHS and FSA loans are on the same security instrument, both agencies must follow specific instructions because default on one loan is treated as default on the entire security instrument, affecting both agencies.

nodes.title: B Security Is Not Cross-Collateralized
nodes.summary: The document section explains how delinquent and financially distressed accounts are serviced depending on how the loan security is structured. It distinguishes three main situations: when security is not cross-collateralized, when it is cross-collateralized but the RHS and FSA loans are on separate security instruments, and when cross-collateralized RHS and FCP loans are on the same security instrument. For each case, it specifies which agency procedures apply for normal servicing and foreclosure/conveyance actions. A key point is that if loans share the same security instrument, default on one loan counts as default against the entire security instrument, making the borrower nonmonetarily in default with both agencies.

nodes.title: C Security Is Cross-Collateralized and Loans Are Described on Separate Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document explains servicing procedures for delinquent and financially distressed accounts involving RHS and FSA loans, depending on how the security is structured. It covers three main situations: when security is not cross-collateralized, when it is cross-collateralized but loans are on separate security instruments, and when RH and FCP loans are cross-collateralized on the same security instrument. It outlines which agency’s servicing rules apply in each case, notes that normal debt servicing follows the relevant agency procedures, and states that foreclosures and conveyances are handled under separate guidance when applicable. It also emphasizes that if loans are on the same cross-collateralized security instrument, default on one loan is considered default against the entire security instrument and creates nonmonetary default with the other agency.

nodes.title: D Security Is Cross-Collateralized and RH and FCP Loans Are Described on Same Security Instruments
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for servicing delinquent and financially distressed accounts when Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Credit Program/Farm Service Agency (FSA) loans are involved, especially in cross-collateralized situations. It distinguishes between cases where security is not cross-collateralized, where loans are on separate security instruments, and where RHS and FSA loans share the same security instrument.

Main points covered include:
- How RHS and FSA each service their own loans under their respective regulations and instructions when security is not cross-collateralized.
- Rules for handling normal debt servicing, foreclosures, and conveyances when loans are cross-collateralized but on separate security instruments.
- Special coordination requirements when cross-collateralized loans are on the same security instrument, including:
  - default on one loan counts as default on the shared security,
  - mutual notification duties between agencies,
  - sequencing of servicing actions, acceleration, and foreclosure,
  - consideration of moratorium, subsidy, or restructuring when appropriate,
  - restrictions on foreclosure until the other agency completes its servicing process.
- General rules requiring both agencies to keep each other informed, manage reamortization carefully, and separate appeals and adverse actions.
- Procedures for flagging accounts during acceleration or foreclosure, and how payments should be applied when only one account is accelerated.
- Instructions for buyouts and payoff of RHS debt, including the need to pay off the RHS loan for release of joint security.
- Special DALR$ reporting instructions for borrowers with RHS loans, including how to record the loan and reamortized payments.

nodes.title: E Special Instructions for DALR$
nodes.summary: The partial document provides servicing instructions for delinquent and financially distressed accounts, focusing on cases where RHS and FSA/FCP loans are tied to the same security instrument. It explains how payments should be applied when one account is accelerated, how accounts are flagged during foreclosure or referral actions, and how buyouts require payoff of related RHS debt before security can be released. It also gives special DALR$ reporting instructions for handling RHS loans in loan servicing calculations, including reamortization, prior liens, proportional sharing of joint security, and required notice language for borrowers. Finally, it outlines that bankruptcies involving joint security instruments are handled separately by each agency under their own regulations, with legal coordination managed by OGC.

nodes.title: F Bankruptcy Filings With Joint Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document is a policy/procedure section from HB-2-3550 covering how to manage delinquent and financially distressed accounts when RHS and FSA loans share collateral. It explains special instructions for DALR$ calculations, including how to account for prior liens, junior liens, and proportional shares of joint security interests, and when to add a notice requiring RHS loans to be paid off during buyout. It also states that bankruptcies involving joint security instruments are handled separately by each agency under their own regulations, with proof of claim forms prepared independently and legal coordination handled by OGC. The document then begins a section on handling foreclosures and voluntary conveyances against joint security loans described on separate security instruments, outlining steps for voluntary conveyance such as obtaining an appraisal and title opinion, determining lien position, and either releasing a valueless lien or paying off prior/junior liens before accepting the conveyance. It also introduces foreclosure procedures requiring a decision to either pay off the other agency’s prior lien or obtain a release if the lien is valueless.

nodes.title: 6 Handling Foreclosures and Voluntary Conveyances Against Joint Security
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains procedures for handling voluntary conveyances and foreclosures when RHS and FSA loans are secured by separate security instruments on cross-collateralized property. It outlines how the initiating agency must obtain a current appraisal and title opinion, then coordinate with the responding agency based on lien position. If the responding agency’s lien is valueless, it should release the lien; if it is a prior lien, it must be paid off before the conveyance is accepted; and if it is a junior lien, it is paid only up to the property’s current market value minus prior liens. For foreclosures, it states that the initiating agency must decide whether to pay off the other agency’s prior lien within the property’s value limits or obtain a release if the lien has no value.

nodes.title: A Loans Described on Separate Security Instruments
nodes.summary:
This document section explains procedures for handling voluntary conveyances, foreclosures, and third-party foreclosure actions involving joint security where Rural Housing Service (RHS) and Farm Service Agency (FSA) loans are cross-collateralized. It covers two main situations: loans described on separate security instruments and loans described on the same security instruments.

Key points include:
- How the initiating agency must obtain a current appraisal and title opinion before accepting a deed in lieu of foreclosure.
- How lien position affects the response of the other agency:
  - If the lien is valueless, a release is issued.
  - If the other agency has a prior lien, it must be paid off before conveyance.
  - If the other agency has a junior lien, payment is limited to the property’s market value minus prior liens.
- Foreclosure procedures when one agency initiates action, including possible payoff of prior liens, obtaining a release, or involving the other agency in the foreclosure suit.
- A note that agencies should try to avoid involving the other agency in foreclosure whenever possible.
- How third-party foreclosures are handled separately by RHS and FSA under their own regulations, with references to specific instructions and rules governing bidding.
- A transition to procedures for loans on the same security instruments, beginning with the same appraisal/title opinion requirement for voluntary conveyance.

nodes.title: B Loans Described on the Same Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for handling foreclosures and voluntary conveyances when RHS and FSA loans are tied to the same borrower security, including cases with separate security instruments, same security instruments, and third-party foreclosures. It covers how agencies should coordinate releases, appraisals, title opinions, liens, bidding, assignments, and distribution of sale proceeds or credits based on lien priority. It also outlines rules for debt settlement after foreclosure, requirements for using agency-specific property ID number ranges, and instructions for processing ADPS 3E acquisition transactions through the St. Louis Finance Office, including required forms and faxed documentation.

nodes.title: C Assigning Property ID Numbers
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for handling foreclosures and voluntary conveyances involving joint security, focusing on how property inventory IDs must be assigned uniquely by agency and state using specified number ranges for FSA and RHS. It explains that state and county codes from St. Louis Finance Office systems must be used, and that agency-based numbering helps prevent duplicate property ID assignments across counties. It also describes the processing of ADPS 3E transactions for property acquisitions, stating that when FSA or RHS acquires property from a borrower with both RHS and FCP loans, the St. Louis Finance Office must process the transaction. The document instructs the acquiring agency to complete the ADPS 3E Transaction Form and fax it, along with the Guide for Calculating the Proportionate Share of Security Value/Proceeds from Joint Mortgages if applicable, to the St. Louis Finance Office and, when cross-collateralized, to the nonacquiring agency as well.

nodes.title: D Processing ADPS 3E Transaction for Acquisitions
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how to handle foreclosures and voluntary conveyances involving joint security, with a focus on property identification and processing acquisitions between FSA and RHS. It specifies unique property ID number ranges for each agency to avoid duplication, using state and county codes plus consecutively numbered digits. It also outlines the required ADPS 3E transaction process when property is acquired from a borrower who has both RHS and FCP loans, including the need to complete and fax transaction forms to the St. Louis Finance Office and, when applicable, notify the nonacquiring agency for cross-collateralized security. The document then provides a worksheet for calculating the proportionate share of joint security interest for DALR$, showing how to determine RHS’s share of market value, subtract liquidation expenses, and calculate the amount of debt to enter as an RHS prior lien, with notes clarifying what debt is included in the calculation.

nodes.title: Guide for Calculating Proportionate Share of Joint Security Interest for DALR$
nodes.summary: This page explains how to calculate the proportionate share of a joint security interest for DALR$ using a three-step formula. It states that the share is based on market value minus authorized selling expenses. Step 1 determines the RHS percentage of total debt by dividing RHS debt by total RHS and FCP debt, then applies that percentage to the market value to get the RHS share of market value. Step 2 uses the same percentage to allocate liquidation expenses to the RHS share of expenses. Step 3 subtracts the RHS share of expenses from the RHS share of market value to determine the amount of debt to enter in DALR$ as RHS prior lien. The notes clarify that only equal-priority unpaid debt secured at the same time is counted, other loans are excluded, and debt includes unpaid principal and interest but not recapture.

nodes.title: Guide for Calculating the Proportionate Share of Security Value/Proceeds From Joint Mortgages
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to calculate the proportionate share of security value or sale proceeds for joint mortgages involving FSA and RHS/FCP loans. It provides step-by-step formulas for determining each agency’s share based on debt ratios, then subtracting liquidation or selling expenses to get the net share. It also defines what counts as jointly secured debt and notes how unpaid principal, interest, and recapture are treated.

The document then introduces an “ADPS 3E Transaction Form” for acquisitions when a borrower has both FSA and RHS loans. It instructs preparers to complete the form for each acquired property and not to process an ADPS 3E transaction directly. The form includes fields for agency name, case number, borrower name, acquisition date, loan information, property details, market value, amount credited, acquisition method, and related loan numbers.

nodes.title: ADPS 3E Transaction
nodes.summary: The partial document is an ADPS 3E transaction form used for reporting property acquisitions when a borrower has both an RHS and an FSA loan. It instructs the preparer not to process an ADPS 3E transaction and instead complete one form for each acquired property. The form is addressed to Janet Marxkors and includes fields for agency name, case number, borrower name, acquisition date, loan details, property identification, property description and suitability codes, taxpayer ID, property address, acres acquired, market value, appraisal date, amount credited, business and form codes, how the property was acquired, and the specific loans to be acquired.

nodes.title: A ADPS 3E Transaction Form
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes instructions for completing an ADPS 3E Transaction Form when a borrower has both FSA and RHS loans and acquires property. It explains that the form must be completed for each acquired property, but the ADPS 3E transaction itself should not be processed. The document provides the form fields to be filled in, including agency name, case number, borrower name, acquisition date, secured loan numbers, property ID, property description and suitability codes, taxpayer ID, property address, acreage acquired, market value, appraisal date, credited amount, submission date, form code, business code, how acquired, and loans to acquire.

It also includes detailed instructions for specific items, such as entering the deed recording date, determining when to complete FSA or RHS secured loan fields, assigning the next available property ID using specified number ranges for FSA and RHS, and selecting the correct property description and suitability codes. The instructions further clarify how to enter acreage for certain property types and note which fields are self-explanatory or should be left blank in some cases.

nodes.title: B Instructions
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics from USDA rural housing servicing guidance:

1. **ADPS 3E Transaction instructions**  
   - It gives item-by-item instructions for completing an ADPS 3E transaction form.  
   - The instructions explain how to enter dates, property IDs, property description and suitability codes, acreage, dollar amounts, preparation dates, commodity/type codes, foreclosure or conveyance codes, liability release codes, and loan/recoverable cost items.

2. **Chapter 3: Escrow, Taxes, and Insurance**  
   - It introduces the purpose of escrow accounts in protecting the Agency’s interest in security property.  
   - It explains that the Agency ensures real estate taxes and local assessments are paid and insurance is maintained, often through escrow accounts.  
   - It also begins Section 1 on tax and insurance requirements, including the use of a tax service and the tax service fee charged to borrowers.

structure.title: CHAPTER 3: INSURANCE
structure.summary:
The partial document explains Agency requirements and servicing procedures for escrow, taxes, insurance, and property losses on Rural Housing Service loans.

Main points covered:
- Why escrow accounts are used: to ensure taxes, assessments, and insurance premiums are paid to protect the Agency’s security interest.
- Tax requirements:
  - The Agency uses a tax service to track tax bills and delinquency status.
  - All borrowers pay a tax service fee.
  - Borrowers not on escrow must pay taxes themselves and provide proof.
  - If taxes are not paid, the Agency may advance funds, require escrow, and recover the advance over time.
  - Special handling is described for recapture receivable accounts and pending tax sales.
- Insurance requirements:
  - Borrowers must maintain continuous insurance on the security property.
  - Required insurance types include hazard insurance, flood insurance in SFHAs, and builder’s risk during construction.
  - The document explains acceptable evidence of insurance, force-placed insurance, and procedures when coverage is canceled, changed, or inadequate.
  - It also addresses vacant or leased units and insurance rules during property transfers or payoff.
- Servicing actions related to insurance:
  - How the Servicing Office reviews policies, pays annual premiums for escrowed loans, handles cancellations, and force places coverage if needed.
- Escrow requirements:
  - Most new loans require escrow, with listed exemptions.
  - Existing borrowers may establish escrow voluntarily or be required to do so under certain servicing actions or defaults.
  - The document explains how initial escrow deposits and monthly escrow amounts are calculated.
- Establishing escrow accounts:
  - Procedures differ for new loans, construction loans, and existing loans.
  - Field Office and Servicing Office responsibilities are described, including tax service notification, disclosure statements, and borrower notification.
- Annual escrow analysis:
  - RESPA requires yearly analysis and disclosure statements.
  - The document defines how surpluses and shortages are handled, including refunds or payment adjustments.
- Disclosure statements for transfers and payoffs:
  - Required disclosures are outlined for loan assumptions and loans paid in full.
- Losses and insurance claims:
  - Borrowers must report losses immediately and file claims with the insurer.
  - The Agency does not negotiate claims but may review settlements.
  - Insurance proceeds are handled differently depending on claim size, borrower account status, and whether rehabilitation is needed, including thresholds for borrower endorsement, escrow handling, or supervised construction.

nodes.title: Administering Insurance Proceeds
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how insurance loss proceeds are handled for USDA Rural Development loans, especially when the loan is secured by something other than a first lien. It covers when the Servicing Office can endorse loss drafts, how prior mortgagee interests affect the handling of funds, and the conditions for using proceeds to repair property or reduce prior debt. It also describes what to do if the Agency is not listed as a mortgagee, including contacting the borrower or insurance carrier and taking action if repairs are not made. In addition, the document outlines the process for inspecting repairs and authorizing payments, including progress inspections, issuance of checks, and final payment requirements with the necessary inspection and contractor release forms.

nodes.title: INSPECTING REPAIRS AND AUTHORIZING PAYMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Agency handles insurance loss proceeds and repair-related payments for damaged property. It covers procedures for determining who receives insurance proceeds, when the Servicing Office should have checks made payable jointly to the borrower and Agency, and what happens if the borrower already has the funds. It also describes how repairs are inspected, how progress and final payments are authorized, and what documents are required before final payment is released. In addition, the document outlines what happens to any remaining insurance funds after repairs are completed, including applying them to liens or delinquencies and releasing excess funds if the debt is adequately secured. Finally, it gives special rules for presidentially declared disasters, allowing faster access to insurance proceeds for repairs under certain dollar limits and timeframes.

nodes.title: POST REPAIR ACTIVITIES
nodes.summary: The document section covers how insurance proceeds are handled after property repairs or damage, including the order in which leftover insurance funds must be applied, when remaining funds may be released to the borrower, and the requirement to restore full insurance coverage after a loss. It also explains special rules for presidentially declared disasters, where borrowers may receive insurance proceeds quickly for repairs if the work will be completed within 30 days, subject to limits and contractor estimates.

nodes.title: PRESIDENTIAL DECLARED DISASTERS
nodes.summary: This section explains how insurance proceeds are handled after property damage and what the Agency does in special situations. It covers the order in which remaining insurance funds are applied after repairs, the requirement to restore full insurance coverage after a loss, and how proceeds may be released quickly after a presidentially declared disaster depending on repair timelines and amounts. It also addresses losses that are not covered by insurance, instructing the borrower to make repairs and outlining Agency options if they cannot, such as considering additional loans, lien subordination, junior financing, protective advances, and possible loan acceleration.

nodes.title: LOSSES NOT COVERED BY INSURANCE
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines Rural Housing Service insurance-related servicing rules and policy requirements. It explains what happens after presidentially declared disasters, including how insurance proceeds are handled when repairs take more than 30 days. It then covers losses not insured at the time of damage, instructing the servicing office to notify the borrower, require repairs, and, if needed, prepare a problem case report recommending options such as a subsequent loan, lien subordination, junior financing, or a protective advance. The attachment also lists insurance policy requirements, including approved insurance providers, acceptable coverage for various hazards, and the required amount of coverage, noting that lender-placed insurance may be forced if the borrower does not maintain adequate insurance.

nodes.title: A. Authorized Insurance Providers
nodes.summary: This document outlines insurance policy requirements for borrowers. It explains that insurance must be purchased from approved, state-licensed insurers, with limited exceptions allowed if an out-of-state company is legally enforceable, reputable, and financially sound. It specifies the types of losses hazard insurance must cover, including fire, wind, hail, explosion, riot, civil commotion, aircraft, vehicles, and smoke, and notes that flood insurance must cover water or flooding damage when applicable. It also describes required coverage amounts, stating that the dwelling and essential buildings must generally be insured for their insurable value, though lower coverage is allowed if it is at least equal to the outstanding secured debt. If the borrower does not maintain required insurance, RHS may force-place insurance, usually at the last known insured value.

nodes.title: B. Loss or Damage Covered
nodes.summary: The document outlines insurance policy requirements for borrowers. It explains that insurance must be purchased from approved, state-licensed insurers unless an exception is approved by the Office of the General Counsel and the State Director. It specifies the types of losses covered by hazard insurance, including fire, wind, hail, explosion, civil disturbance, aircraft, vehicles, smoke, and flood damage where applicable. It also states that coverage must generally equal the insurable value of the dwelling and essential buildings, though borrowers may choose lower coverage if it is at least equal to the outstanding secured debt. If the borrower cannot maintain required insurance, RHS will force-place coverage, usually at the last known insured value.

nodes.title: C. Amount
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines insurance policy requirements for borrowers, likely in the context of housing or rural housing loans. It covers who may provide acceptable insurance, what types of hazards and losses must be covered, and the minimum amount of coverage required. It also explains special conditions such as leasehold property, deductible limits, policy term and effective date, construction and use requirements, required insured names and property location details, and the need for a standard mortgagee clause naming the Agency as the mortgagee so it is protected in the event of loss.

nodes.title: D. Borrower’s Deductible
nodes.summary: This document section explains the insurance policy requirements for properties securing a loan, including leasehold disclosure, borrower deductible limits, policy term, effective date, construction and use conditions, required names and property location details, and the mortgagee clause identifying the Agency as the secured party.

nodes.title: E. Term
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines insurance policy requirements for properties tied to a loan. It discusses leasehold disclosure and the role of State Supplements and Servicing Offices for guidance on state insurance rules. It then covers borrower deductible limits, required policy term and proof of premium payment, when coverage must become effective for existing or newly constructed dwellings, and acceptance conditions based on construction specifications and property use. Finally, it explains naming and location requirements in the policy and the need for a mortgagee clause identifying the Agency as the secured party.

nodes.title: F. Effective Date
nodes.summary: The document outlines insurance policy requirements for a property securing a loan. It explains rules for leasehold interests, borrower deductibles, minimum policy term, and when coverage must become effective, especially for existing buildings and new construction. It also covers construction specifications and permitted property uses, required names and property location details, and mortgagee clause requirements to ensure the Agency is named and protected in the event of a loss.

nodes.title: G. Construction Specifications and Use Conditions
nodes.summary: This document section outlines insurance policy requirements for properties securing a loan. It explains that the policy must indicate whether the building is on a leasehold, and notes that State Supplements may provide guidance on leasehold-related insurance rules. It then covers several key policy standards: the borrower’s deductible must stay within acceptable limits; the policy term must be at least one year with a full year’s premium paid; insurance must be in force at loan closing for existing buildings or by delivery of materials for new construction; any construction or use conditions in the policy must be met; the policy must name all insured parties and identify the property location; and a mortgagee clause must be included so the Agency is listed as the secured party and can be reimbursed in the event of a loss.

nodes.title: H. Names and Location
nodes.summary: The document outlines insurance policy requirements for a property loan, including whether the building is on a leasehold, borrower deductible limits, minimum policy term, and when coverage must become effective. It also covers construction and use restrictions, required names and property location details in the policy, and the need for a mortgagee clause identifying the Agency as the secured party.

nodes.title: I. Mortgagee Clause
nodes.summary: The document explains insurance policy requirements for properties securing a loan, including leasehold disclosure, acceptable borrower deductibles, minimum policy term, when coverage must be effective, and compliance with any construction or use conditions. It also outlines required information in the policy such as the insured parties, property location, and the mortgagee clause naming USDA/Rural Housing Service, along with the mailing address and rules for updated or alternative loss payable clauses. Finally, it defines master policies and explains that, where required by state law, individual policies are compared against the master policy to ensure they adequately protect the Agency’s interest.

nodes.title: Master Policies
nodes.summary: This partial document covers insurance and fee requirements related to USDA Rural Development loans. It explains how the Agency and other mortgagees must be listed on insurance policies, including the correct USDA address for the mortgagee clause. It also describes acceptable use of a loss payable clause when a standard mortgagee clause is not printed, and the closing agent’s responsibility to verify insurer compliance through a signed letter. In addition, it defines what a master policy is and when it is required. The final section provides the tax service fee schedule, including fees for new loans and new rates-and-terms assumptions for specified dates.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 3 -B
nodes.summary: This attachment provides a tax service fee schedule, listing the charges applicable to new loans approved during specific date ranges from September 30, 2024 through September 29, 2028. It notes an initial fee of $82 for loans approved between September 30, 2024 and September 29, 2025, with incremental increases in subsequent years. It also states that the tax service fee remains in effect until further notice, and that the charge for tax service on a new rates and terms assumption remains $10 unless otherwise notified.

structure.title: CHAPTER 4: PAYMENT SUBSIDIES AND INCOME DETERMINATIONS
