structure.summary: The partial document contains only an instruction stating that the response should directly return a description of the main points covered in the partial document, without including any additional text.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550 on payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It explains that payment subsidies are used to improve borrower repayment ability, with the Field Office handling initial subsidy determinations and the Servicing and Asset Management Office managing subsidy initiation and periodic reviews. The chapter is divided into two main sections: one covering approval and renewal of payment subsidies, and another explaining how annual and adjusted income are calculated for subsidy purposes. It also introduces the overview of payment subsidies, noting that they are only available for Section 502 loans, are based on borrower income, are calculated through LoanServ, and includes the first subsidy type, interest credit, which may continue as long as the borrower remains eligible.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF PAYMENT SUBSIDIES
nodes.summary: This partial document introduces Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550, which covers payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It explains that payment subsidies are used to improve borrowers’ repayment ability, with initial subsidy eligibility determined by the Field Office and ongoing subsidy reviews handled by the Servicing and Asset Management Office. The chapter is divided into two main sections: one on approval and renewal of payment subsidies, and another on calculating annual and adjusted income used to determine subsidy amounts. The excerpt begins Section 1 by outlining that payment subsidies apply only to Section 502 loans, are based on borrower income, and are calculated by LoanServ. It also starts describing the first type of subsidy, interest credit, and notes that borrowers may continue receiving it as long as they remain eligible.

nodes.title: A. Three Types of Subsidy
nodes.summary: This partial document introduces Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550, which covers payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It explains that payment subsidies are used to improve borrowers’ ability to repay loans, outlines the roles of the Field Office and the Servicing and Asset Management Office in determining and renewing subsidies, and notes that Section 1 covers subsidy approval and renewal while Section 2 covers annual and adjusted income calculations. The excerpt also begins an overview of payment subsidies, stating that they apply only to Section 502 loans, are based on borrower income, and are calculated by LoanServ, then starts describing the first subsidy type: interest credit.

nodes.title: Interest Credit
nodes.summary:
The partial document is from Chapter 4 of HB-2-3550 and explains payment subsidies and income determinations for Section 502 loans. It introduces the purpose of payment subsidies, which are used to improve borrowers’ repayment ability, and distinguishes responsibilities between the Field Office and the Servicing and Asset Management Office for initial determinations and ongoing reviews.

The main topics covered include:
- An overview of payment subsidies and how they are based on borrower income
- The three subsidy types:
  - Interest Credit
  - Payment Assistance Method 1
  - Payment Assistance Method 2
- Rules for continuing, renewing, or switching subsidy types
- Borrower eligibility requirements, including:
  - Income eligibility at origination and during the loan term
  - Occupancy requirements, requiring the borrower to personally occupy the property, with limited temporary absences allowed

It also notes that detailed calculation guidance for annual and adjusted income appears later in the chapter, since those figures are used to determine subsidy amounts.

nodes.title: Payment Assistance Method 1
nodes.summary: The document section explains the rules for payment subsidies in the HB-2-3550 program. It describes how interest credit and payment assistance are handled, including when payment assistance method 1 versus method 2 applies, such as after receiving subsequent loans or after a gap in subsidy receipt. It also outlines borrower eligibility requirements, focusing on income limits at origination and during the loan term, as well as occupancy rules requiring the borrower to personally occupy the property, with limited allowable temporary absences for approved reasons.

nodes.title: Payment Assistance Method 2
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains the USDA rural housing payment subsidy rules under HB-2-3550, focusing on how subsidy assistance is provided and who is eligible. It describes two payment assistance methods: Method 1, which can switch to Method 2 if a borrower gets a subsequent loan, and Method 2, which applies to most other eligible borrowers, including new loans, certain subsequent loans, and borrowers whose prior subsidy ended for 6 months or more. It also outlines borrower eligibility requirements, including income eligibility at origination and during the loan term, and the occupancy requirement that the borrower must personally occupy the dwelling, with limited temporary absences allowed for acceptable reasons.

nodes.title: B. Borrower Eligibility
nodes.summary: The document section explains the overview of payment subsidies, including how subsidy assistance continues or changes when interest credit agreements expire or loans are renewed. It distinguishes between Payment Assistance Method 1 and Method 2, describing which borrowers qualify for each and when Method 2 must be used. The section also outlines borrower eligibility requirements, focusing on income limits at loan origination and during the loan term, and states that borrowers receiving payment subsidies must personally occupy the dwelling, with limited exceptions for temporary absences such as military service, seasonal work, or hospitalization.

nodes.title: Income Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the USDA Rural Housing payment subsidy rules under HB-2-3550, Section 4.2. It covers the two payment assistance methods and when each applies, including transitions after interest credit ends, subsequent loans, and borrowers who re-enter subsidy after a break. It also outlines borrower eligibility requirements, focusing on income limits at origination and during the loan term, and the occupancy requirement that the borrower must personally occupy the home, with limited allowed temporary absences for acceptable reasons.

nodes.title: Occupancy Requirement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules and procedures for payment subsidies under HB-2-3550, including the two payment assistance methods and when each applies. It covers borrower eligibility requirements based on income, occupancy, and loan type, and notes that subsidies are not available for nonprogram, above-moderate, or pre-August 1, 1968 loans unless refinanced. It also outlines loan term requirements for subsidy eligibility and borrower reporting obligations, such as annual income and household updates and notifying the Agency of changes. The text begins to discuss special reporting arrangements for self-employed borrowers.

nodes.title: Nonprogram, Above-Moderate and Pre-August 1, 1968, Borrowers
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines rules for Rural Housing payment subsidies under HB-2-3550, focusing on:

- **Eligibility restrictions** for certain borrowers, including nonprogram, above-moderate, and pre-August 1, 1968 loans.
- **Loan term requirements** for subsidy eligibility, generally requiring initial or related loans to have at least a 25-year term.
- **Borrower reporting duties**, such as annual reporting of household income, expenses, and composition, plus required notifications when household or income circumstances change.
- **Servicer review flexibility**, allowing alternative review periods in special situations.
- A beginning discussion of **special rules for self-employed borrowers** regarding the timing of payment assistance agreements.

nodes.title: C. Loan Requirements
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules and conditions for USDA payment subsidies under HB-2-3550, including who is eligible and when subsidies cannot be provided. It covers limitations for nonprogram, above-moderate, and pre-August 1, 1968 borrowers, as well as the possibility of refinancing to obtain assistance. It also describes loan term requirements for subsidy eligibility, borrower reporting duties for annual income and household updates, and the obligation to notify the Agency of certain changes in employment, income, or household composition. Finally, it begins to discuss special review periods for specific cases, starting with self-employed borrowers.

nodes.title: D. Borrower Reporting Requirements
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains rules for USDA loan payment subsidies under HB-2-3550, focusing on who can receive assistance and what borrowers must do to keep it. It covers:

- Limits on subsidies for certain borrowers, including nonprogram, above-moderate, and pre-August 1, 1968 loans.
- Loan term requirements for eligibility, especially the need for a minimum 25-year term in many cases.
- Annual borrower reporting obligations about income, expenses, and household composition.
- Borrower notification requirements when household membership, employment, or income changes.
- The Servicer’s ability to use alternative review periods for special situations.
- An introduction to special rules for self-employed borrowers.

nodes.title: Self-Employed Borrowers
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines USDA/Rural Development rules for payment subsidies and payment assistance on loans. It explains who is eligible for subsidies, including restrictions for certain loan types and requirements that eligible loans generally have a term of at least 25 years. It also describes borrower reporting duties, such as annual income and household composition updates and notifying the agency of employment or income changes.

The document then covers special reporting/review periods for self-employed borrowers, unemployed borrowers, and borrowers with annual payment schedules. It includes recapture requirements, stating that subsidy recapture may be repaid if the borrower stops occupying the property or transfers title after the loan is paid off, and notes that Form RD 3550-12 must be signed when a borrower first qualifies.

Finally, the section begins explaining how payment assistance is calculated, introducing two methods of assistance and starting with Method 1, which is based on the difference between the full note payment and the borrower’s income-based payment amount.

nodes.title: Unemployed Borrowers
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses USDA/Rural Development payment subsidies and assistance rules for borrowers. It explains how agreement periods are set for different borrower situations, including business fiscal year income reviews, unemployed borrowers, and borrowers with annual installment payments. It also covers the recapture requirement, stating when subsidy recapture must be repaid and when it is not triggered, and notes the required form for first-time subsidy recipients. Finally, it introduces the calculation of payment assistance, describing two methods and beginning with Method 1, where assistance is the difference between the note-rate installment and the amount the borrower can pay based on income.

nodes.title: Annua l Payment Borrowers
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules and procedures for housing payment subsidies, including how payment assistance agreements are timed and renewed for different borrower situations such as self-employed borrowers, unemployed borrowers, and annual payment borrowers. It explains that agreement periods may align with a borrower’s fiscal year, includes limits on review periods and agreement terms, and describes how unemployment benefits and annual installment payments are treated in income and assistance calculations. The text also outlines subsidy recapture requirements, stating that recapture must be repaid when a borrower no longer occupies the property or transfers title, with exceptions for approved temporary absences, and notes that borrowers must sign Form RD 3550-12 when first qualifying for payment subsidy. Finally, it introduces the calculation of payment assistance, describing two methods and beginning with Method 1, which is based on the difference between the note-rate installment and the income-based amount the borrower is required to pay.

nodes.title: E. Recapture Requirement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payment subsidies and payment assistance are handled for borrowers. It covers special rules for different borrower situations, including borrowers with variable business income, unemployed borrowers, and borrowers making annual payments. It also discusses the subsidy recapture requirement, which applies when a borrower pays off the loan but stops occupying or transfers title to the property, along with the requirement to sign Form RD 3550-12 when first qualifying for payment subsidy. Finally, it introduces payment assistance calculations, describing two methods of assistance and outlining payment assistance method 1 as the difference between the regular note payment and the amount the borrower can pay based on income.

nodes.title: CALCULATING PAYMENT ASSISTANCE
nodes.summary: This excerpt covers key rules for USDA payment subsidies and payment assistance. It explains how subsidy agreements are timed for different borrower situations, including borrowers with seasonal or business income, unemployed borrowers, and borrowers making annual payments. It also outlines the recapture requirement, stating that subsidy may need to be repaid if the borrower stops occupying the property or transfers title after the loan is paid in full, with an exception for temporary absence approved by the Agency. Finally, it begins the section on calculating payment assistance, introducing two methods and describing Method 1 as the difference between the note-rate payment and the income-based amount the borrower is required to pay.

nodes.title: A. Payment Assistance Method 1
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA Rural Development payment subsidy rules and how payment assistance is calculated for borrowers. It covers special agreement terms for borrowers with different income situations, including borrowers whose income follows a business fiscal year, unemployed borrowers, and borrowers with annual payment schedules. It also describes the subsidy recapture requirement, which applies when a loan is paid in full and the borrower no longer occupies or transfers the property.

The main section focuses on calculating payment assistance using two methods. Method 1 is illustrated with a worked example and is based on the difference between the loan payment at the note rate and the amount the borrower can reasonably pay based on income. The document explains how to determine the payment at the equivalent interest rate (EIR), how to calculate the floor payment, and how to compare the two to find the required borrower payment and monthly assistance amount. It also provides a table of equivalent interest rates tied to income ranges and explains the minimum floor payment percentages for principal, interest, taxes, and insurance (PITI) based on borrower income level.

nodes.title: Calculating the Payment at the Equivalent Interest Rate
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate payment assistance in two main parts. First, it describes calculating the payment using an equivalent interest rate based on the borrower’s adjusted annual income and loan terms, including a table of interest rates tied to income ranges and noting that the rate cannot exceed the note rate or fall below 1 percent. Second, it explains how to calculate the floor payment, which is the minimum required PITI payment for most borrowers, with percentages set at 22%, 24%, or 26% of adjusted income depending on income category and whether the borrower is above or below 65% of the applicable median income.

nodes.title: Calculating the Floor Payment
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to calculate payment assistance under HB-2-3550. It covers two main calculation methods:

1. **Calculating the payment at the equivalent interest rate (EIR):**  
   The borrower’s loan amount, loan term, and an income-based equivalent interest rate are used. A table lists EIRs tied to adjusted annual income as a percentage of median income, with rates ranging from 1.0% up to 9.5%. It also states that the EIR cannot be higher than the note rate and cannot be less than 1%.

2. **Calculating the floor payment:**  
   Most borrowers must pay a minimum amount for principal, interest, taxes, and insurance (PITI), based on adjusted income:
   - 22% for very low-income borrowers
   - 24% for low-income borrowers at or below 65% of median income
   - 26% for borrowers above 65% of median income

The document also describes **Payment Assistance Method 2**, which determines assistance as the lesser of two differences: one based on the combined RHS loan and eligible leveraged loans minus 24% of adjusted income, and the other based on the RHS loan amortized at 1% interest. It explains that borrowers under Method 2 must pay the greater of a payment based on 24% of adjusted income after certain deductions, or a payment calculated at a 1% interest rate. Finally, it defines an **eligible leveraged loan** as one amortized over at least 30 years with an interest rate of no more than 3%, and notes that an example is provided in Exhibit 4-3.

nodes.title: B. Payment Assistance Method 2.
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how Rural Housing Service (RHS) payment assistance is calculated under Method 2. It defines the subsidy as the lesser of two comparison amounts: one based on the combined RHS loan and any eligible leveraged loans plus taxes and insurance minus 24% of adjusted income, and another based on the RHS loan payment reduced to a 1% amortized amount. It also states what the borrower must actually pay, including minimum payment rules and the definition of an eligible leveraged loan.

The document then gives a worked example showing how to calculate Method 2 payment assistance for a sample borrower, including the subsidy, borrower PITI, and borrower RHS payment.

Next, it briefly introduces interest credit calculations, explaining that interest credit is the difference between the note-rate payment and a minimum required borrower payment, with the minimum based on the greater of 20% of adjusted income minus taxes and insurance or a 1% loan payment.

Finally, it covers eligibility for borrowers not currently receiving payment subsidies. It notes that borrowers may become eligible if their income drops, and it outlines eligibility requirements such as income limits, owner occupancy, and restrictions on older or nonprogram loans, while also noting that some loans may be refinanced to obtain payment assistance.

nodes.title: CALCULATING INTEREST CREDIT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payment assistance is calculated and provided under the program. It first describes the calculation of interest credit, defining it as the difference between the note-rate payment and the minimum borrower payment, and specifies that borrowers must pay the greater of 20% of adjusted income minus taxes and insurance, or a payment based on a 1% interest amortization rate. It then outlines “Method 2” payment assistance for borrowers not currently receiving a subsidy, noting that assistance is typically triggered by a drop in income and may be requested by the borrower or identified by the servicer. The section also states the eligibility requirements, including income below the low-income limit and owner-occupancy, and notes restrictions on certain older or nonprogram loans, with refinancing sometimes available to make assistance possible.

nodes.title: PROVIDING PAYMENT ASSISTANCE METHOD 2 TO BORROWERS NOT CURRENTLY RECEIVING A PAYMENT SUBSIDY
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payment assistance is calculated and provided for certain USDA/HB-2-3550 borrowers. It covers interest credit calculation, including the difference between the note-rate payment and the minimum required borrower payment, and specifies that borrowers must pay the greater of 20% of adjusted income minus taxes and insurance or a payment based on a 1% interest rate amortization. It also describes “Payment Assistance Method 2” for borrowers not currently receiving a subsidy, noting that eligibility depends on low income, owner-occupancy, and program restrictions, and that borrowers may request assistance or be identified by the servicer when their income declines.

nodes.title: A. Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payment assistance and interest credit are calculated and administered for eligible borrowers under a loan servicing program. It covers: determining interest credit as the difference between the note-rate payment and the borrower’s minimum required payment; eligibility requirements for borrowers not already receiving a subsidy; a sample calculation showing how the monthly assistance amount is derived; the process for requesting and approving payment assistance using specific forms and LoanServ; the effective date when new subsidy assistance begins; and the annual review process used to verify continued eligibility and update subsidy agreements.

nodes.title: B. Processing Requests for Payment Assistance
nodes.summary: The document explains how payment assistance is processed for borrowers not already receiving a subsidy, including eligibility determination, form submission, calculation of assistance, borrower notification, and execution of required agreements. It also describes when the subsidy becomes effective after documents are received. In addition, it outlines annual and interim review procedures for payment subsidies, including when reviews are triggered, how borrowers are notified, how income changes are handled, and the timing needed to renew assistance before an existing agreement expires.

nodes.title: C. Effective Date of Payment Assistance
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payment assistance is processed for borrowers who are not already receiving a subsidy, including eligibility determination, required forms, borrower notification, and repayment agreement requirements. It also describes when the new payment assistance becomes effective after documents are received. In addition, it outlines the annual review process for subsidy agreements lasting more than 12 months, including borrower notification, income-change verification, and timing to ensure renewal before the current agreement expires.

nodes.title: ANNUAL AND INTERIM REVIEWS OF PAYMENT SUBSIDIES
nodes.summary: The document explains how the Servicer processes requests for payment assistance under Method 2 for borrowers not already receiving a subsidy. It covers sending Form RD 3550-21 to determine eligibility, calculating assistance through LoanServ, issuing Form RD 1944-14 and possibly Form RD 3550-12, and making the subsidy effective after executed documents are received. It also outlines annual review procedures for existing subsidy agreements, including when reviews are required, how borrowers are notified, how income changes are handled, and the timing needed to renew subsidy assistance before the current agreement expires.

nodes.title: A. Annual Reviews
nodes.summary:
This section explains how payment assistance subsidies are processed, renewed, and adjusted for borrowers not currently receiving a subsidy. It covers:

- How the servicer determines eligibility by sending Form RD 3550-21, using LoanServ to calculate assistance, and issuing Form RD 1944-14 along with Form RD 3550-12 when needed.
- The effective date of a new payment subsidy, which begins on the due date after the borrower’s signed documents are received and processed.
- Annual review requirements for subsidy agreements lasting more than 12 months, including borrower notification, income verification, and renewal timing so the new subsidy starts when the current one expires.
- Interim review procedures when a borrower reports changes in household income, employment, or household composition, or when income increases by more than 10 percent.
- Rules for recalculating subsidy amounts, notifying borrowers of payment changes, and setting the timing for increases and decreases in monthly payments.

nodes.title: B. Interim Reviews
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the procedures for reviewing payment subsidies for borrowers under LoanServ. It covers annual reviews, where borrowers are sent Form RD 3550-21 to update household income, expenses, and composition, and the servicer verifies the information before recalculating subsidy amounts. It also describes interim reviews, requiring borrowers to report household changes such as employment, composition, or significant income increases, and outlines how subsidy payments are adjusted and when changes take effect. In addition, it details in-depth quality control reviews using IRS Form 4506-T for random or questionable cases, and notes special handling for borrowers with Trust loans to ensure correct combined calculations and coordination between servicers.

nodes.title: C. In-Depth Reviews
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for annual and interim reviews of payment subsidies, focusing on two main areas. First, it describes in-depth income reviews conducted by the Servicing Office on a random quality-control basis for at least 1% of completed renewals, as well as additional reviews when borrower information appears inaccurate or conflicts with other information. These reviews use IRS Form 4506-T to verify tax information, require all adult household members to submit the form, and may also be triggered by reports of unauthorized assistance. Second, it addresses borrowers with Trust loans, stating that the Trust loan servicer must be notified when payment subsidies are processed so combined calculations are correct and duplication of effort is avoided.

nodes.title: D. Trust Loan Borrowers
nodes.summary:
The document covers procedures for reviewing and verifying borrower income for payment subsidy purposes under USDA Rural Housing guidelines. It explains that the Servicing Office conducts random in-depth quality control reviews of at least one percent of completed renewals, uses IRS Form 4506-T to verify tax information, and may review cases involving suspected inaccuracies or unauthorized assistance. It also notes that when a borrower has a Trust loan, the Trust loan servicer must be notified to ensure correct subsidy calculations and avoid duplication.

The second major topic introduces Section 2 on evaluating borrower income, outlining how income information is used to determine subsidy eligibility and amount. It defines key income concepts, especially the difference between repayment income and adjusted income, and explains that repayment income is used only for servicing decisions like moratorium or reamortization, while adjusted income is used to determine subsidy eligibility. The section also defines annual income broadly as income received by the borrower and household members from all relevant sources, unless specifically excluded.

nodes.title: Overview
nodes.summary:
This section explains how the Loan Servicer evaluates borrower income for USDA Rural Housing loan servicing purposes. It covers how income is used to determine eligibility for payment subsidy, and provides guidance on verifying and calculating income, with references to HUD Handbook 4350.3 examples.

Main points covered include:
- The role of income information in determining subsidy eligibility and subsidy amount.
- Key income concepts and the three income definitions used by the Agency.
- Repayment income, which is used only for servicing decisions such as moratorium or reamortization, and includes only the income of parties to the note.
- Adjusted income, which is used to determine eligibility for payment subsidies and is calculated by first determining annual income and then subtracting qualifying household deductions.
- Annual income, defined broadly as all monetary and non-monetary income received by household members, including anticipated income and certain asset-derived income, unless specifically excluded by regulation.

nodes.title: A. Key Concepts for Income Determinations
nodes.summary:
This section explains how the Loan Servicer evaluates borrower income for USDA Rural Housing purposes. It covers the overall purpose of using income information, which is to determine eligibility for payment subsidy and the amount of subsidy. It then introduces the key income concepts used in these determinations, especially the different income definitions.

The main points include:
- The need to use the correct income definition depending on the purpose:
  - Repayment income, used only for servicing decisions such as moratorium or reamortization, and based only on the income of parties to the note.
  - Adjusted income, used to determine eligibility for payment subsidies, which is calculated from annual income minus allowable deductions.
- A detailed definition of annual income, including income received by or on behalf of household members, anticipated income over a 12-month period, income from outside the family, amounts not specifically excluded by regulation, and income from assets.
- The section also notes that the guidance for verifying and calculating income draws on HUD Handbook 4350.3.

nodes.title: Income Definitions
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how a loan servicer evaluates borrower income for USDA Rural Housing purposes. It introduces the purpose of income verification in determining eligibility for payment subsidy and the amount of assistance, and it distinguishes among three income concepts: annual income, adjusted income, and repayment income. Annual income is the broad measure of household income used to establish eligibility, adjusted income is annual income minus allowed deductions and is used to determine subsidy eligibility, and repayment income is used only to assess ability to make payments and eligibility for moratorium or reamortization. The document also explains whose income must be counted under each definition, noting that repayment income includes only parties to the note while adjusted income includes all household members. It excludes live-in aides, foster children, and foster adults from household member calculations, and provides special guidance for individuals permanently confined to nursing homes or hospitals, allowing the family to choose whether to include that person’s income and related medical deductions or exclude both.

nodes.title: Whose Income To Count
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how income is counted for USDA Section 502 and 504 housing programs. It distinguishes between adjusted income, which is used to determine income eligibility and includes deductions for household circumstances, and repayment income, which is used to assess a borrower’s ability to make monthly loan payments and is based only on parties to the note. It also explains whose income must be counted for each type of income, including rules for borrowers, spouses, other adults, dependents, full-time students, foster members, live-in aides, and permanently confined family members. In addition, it outlines how income limits are used to classify households and introduces the different income categories, such as very low-income. The exhibit provides a detailed table showing which types of income are counted for each household member category.

nodes.title: Income Limits
Using LoanServ and the Income Worksheet to Compute Income
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how income is calculated and verified for loan servicing. It introduces the use of LoanServ and an Income Worksheet to compute income, noting that if the worksheet is used, a copy must be kept in the borrower’s file. It then begins Section 4.8 on sources and verification of income, describing that the servicer must determine both annual income and repayment income based on which income sources count. The excerpt specifically covers earned income, including gross wages, salaries, overtime, commissions, fees, tips, bonuses, housing allowances, and similar compensation, and explains that expected increases or cost-of-living adjustments may be included under certain timing rules. It also states that for annual income, only the first $480 of earned income from certain adult full-time students is counted.

nodes.title: SOURCES & VERIFICATION OF INCOME
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how income is calculated and verified for loan servicing. It describes using LoanServ and an income worksheet to compute borrower income, noting that servicers enter data and should keep a copy of the worksheet in the borrower’s file if used. It then outlines the sources of income that must be considered for annual income and repayment income, emphasizing that annual income includes income from household members and repayment income includes dependable income attributable to the borrowers on the note. The excerpt specifically lists wages and salary-related compensation such as overtime, commissions, fees, tips, bonuses, housing allowances, and similar earnings, and it notes how estimated cost-of-living increases and limited earned income from adult full-time students are treated.

nodes.title: A. For annual income , consider income from the following source s that are attributable to any household member.
nodes.summary: The partial document is a section of USDA loan servicing guidance that explains how to calculate and verify borrower income using LoanServ and an income worksheet. It outlines the main income sources that must be counted for annual income and repayment income, including wages, business/farm income, property income, benefits, public assistance, support payments, and military pay. It also lists specific exclusions and adjustments, such as treatment of fringe benefits, housing allowances, business losses, depreciation, and certain lump-sum or deferred benefit payments. The document further distinguishes additional sources that count only for repayment income, such as housing assistance payments, adoption assistance above a threshold, Holocaust reparation payments, some student financial assistance, property tax rebates, and other federally exempt income. Examples are included to show how to apply the rules in practice.

nodes.title: B. Consider these additional sources of income that are attributable to parties to the note and represent a source of dependable income for repayment income only.
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for determining and verifying income for housing repayment purposes. It lists specific income sources that must be counted as repayment income, including military pay and allowances, housing assistance payments, certain adoption assistance, Holocaust reparations, student financial assistance, property tax refunds, and several other federally exempt payments. It also identifies special cases such as SNAP benefits, which may be counted only up to a capped amount, and provides an example showing how to calculate that limit. Finally, it notes sources that are never counted as annual or repayment income, such as earnings from minors under 18 (with exceptions).

nodes.title: C. The following sources are never considered when calculating annual income or repayment income:
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for determining annual and repayment income under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 4.8, focusing on which income sources can be counted, partially counted, or excluded. It specifically covers certain allowable income items such as state agency payments for a developmentally disabled family member, special pay for military service in hostile fire areas, and how SNAP benefits may be included in repayment income up to a capped amount with a worked example. It also lists income sources that are never counted, including earnings of minors, income of foster children or live-in aides, special-purpose payments, temporary or nonrecurring income, lump-sum asset additions, medical reimbursements, and reverse amortization mortgage payments.

nodes.title: D. In addition, the following sources are never considered when calculating annual income :
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to determine and verify household income for USDA rural housing subsidy purposes. It lists several income sources that are excluded from annual income calculations, such as payments for foster care, special-purpose reimbursements, temporary or nonrecurring income, lump-sum asset additions, medical expense reimbursements, reverse mortgage payments, certain workforce and training program payments, earned income tax credits, and AmeriCorps-related payments. It also identifies income sources that are never counted, including foster care payments, deferred Social Security/SSI lump sums, certain crime victim compensation, specific veteran-related allowances, Indian Claims Commission payments, Section 8 homeownership voucher assistance, and excess adoption assistance.

The document then describes verification requirements for income and household composition. Borrowers must provide income information through the subsidy renewal certification form and submit supporting documents such as recent federal tax returns, pay stubs, authorization forms for third-party verification, and award letters for nonemployment income. It also explains that the loan servicer must review the submitted information, request additional verification when needed, and may use written or oral third-party verification, with oral verification requiring careful documentation.

nodes.title: E. Verifying Sources and Amounts
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how Rural Development loan servicers verify household income and related deductions for subsidy renewals. It covers required borrower documentation, such as tax returns, pay stubs, authorization forms, award letters, and third-party verifications, and notes when oral verification may be accepted. It then lists verification rules and acceptable alternatives for many income types, including wages, self-employment, seasonal employment, unemployment, disability, workers’ compensation, Social Security and pensions, alimony/child support, foster care payments, and asset income. The document also describes verification requirements for deductible expenses such as disability assistance, medical costs, and child-care expenses. Finally, it explains how servicers must project expected income for the next 12 months using approved calculation methods like straight-line, averaging, year-to-date, and historical income, and emphasizes careful analysis when choosing the most representative figure.

nodes.title: F. Projecting Expected Income for the Next 12 Months
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how a loan servicer should project a verified income source for the next 12 months, using applicable calculation methods such as straight-based, average, year-to-date, and historical income. It stresses that the chosen figure must reflect the most representative expected income, not simply the lowest amount, and that the decision may require additional verification and written documentation. An example demonstrates how different methods can produce different income projections and how the servicer should select the most appropriate one based on evidence of earnings trends, especially overtime.

The document also covers how to treat temporarily absent family members. It states that income from absent household members must still be counted if they remain part of the household or are parties to the note, while income should not be counted if the person is no longer considered a household member and is not on the note. Examples illustrate situations involving work away from home, disability-related absences, temporary employment, and military duty, showing when income should or should not be included in annual income calculations.

nodes.title: G. Income of Temporarily Absent Family Members
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to handle income verification and calculation for USDA Rural Housing loans. It covers income from temporarily absent family members, stating when their income should still be counted in annual income and repayment income, and when it should be excluded if they are not considered household members or parties to the note. It then gives examples involving workers away from home, a disabled household member in a rehabilitation center, temporary employment, and active military duty to illustrate these rules.

The document also introduces wage matching as a verification method, explaining that in states where it is authorized, the Loan Originator must use wage matching for a sample of households receiving a Certificate of Eligibility, include all adult household members in the request, and resolve any discrepancies with the applicant.

Finally, it begins Section 4.9 on calculating annual and adjusted income, explaining that annual income is the starting point for adjusted income, that all household members’ income is counted, and that adjusted income is used to determine payment subsidy eligibility. It outlines allowable deductions from annual income, including dependent deductions, child care expenses, elderly household medical expenses, and disability assistance, with a table showing which deductions apply to elderly and non-elderly households.

nodes.title: H. Wage Matching
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how USDA Rural Housing staff verify household income and calculate annual and adjusted income for program eligibility. It covers wage matching procedures in states with authority to use it, including checking income for a sample of households, comparing reported income with wage match results, and resolving discrepancies. It then describes how annual income is used to determine adjusted income, emphasizes that income from all household members must be counted, and introduces the deductions allowed when calculating adjusted income. These deductions include dependent deductions, child care expenses, elderly household medical expenses, and disability assistance, with applicability depending on whether the household is elderly or non-elderly.

nodes.title: CALCULATING ANNUAL AND ADJUSTED Income
nodes.summary: The partial document covers income verification and calculation rules for a housing program. It explains the use of wage matching to verify household income in states where the agency has authority, including a requirement to check a sample of households and to resolve discrepancies between reported and matched income. It then introduces how annual income and adjusted income are calculated, noting that all household members’ income must be counted and that adjusted income determines subsidy eligibility. Finally, it outlines the process for subtracting allowable deductions from annual income and mentions categories of deductions available to elderly and non-elderly households, such as dependent deductions, child care expenses, elderly household deductions, medical expenses, and disability assistance.

nodes.title: A. Calculating Deductions from Annual Income
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how income is verified and calculated for housing program eligibility. It covers wage matching requirements, including when agencies must use it, which household members must be included, and how discrepancies between reported and matched income should be handled. It then introduces the process for calculating annual and adjusted income, emphasizing that income from all household members is counted and that adjusted income determines payment subsidy eligibility. Finally, it outlines allowable deductions from annual income, noting that different deductions apply to elderly and non-elderly households and that further guidance is provided for verifying and computing these deductions.

nodes.title: B. Dependent Deduction
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how annual and adjusted income are calculated under HB-2-3550, focusing on two deduction categories. It describes the dependent deduction, which allows $480 for each eligible dependent household member, defines who can and cannot be counted as a dependent, and explains how custody disputes are handled. It also covers child care expenses, stating when unreimbursed child care costs for children age 12 and under may be deducted, including requirements tied to work, job search, or schooling, limits on expenses for full-time students, and rules preventing deductions in certain situations.

nodes.title: C. Child Care Expenses
nodes.summary:
This section explains key deductions used when calculating annual and adjusted income under HB-2-3550. It covers:

- **Dependent deduction**: A $480 deduction is allowed for each qualifying dependent household member. It defines who can and cannot be counted as a dependent, including rules for children, students, disabled individuals, foster children, unborn children, and live-in aides. It also explains how to handle custody disputes and what documentation may be needed.

- **Child care expenses deduction**: Reasonable unreimbursed child care costs for children age 12 and under may be deducted if the care allows a household member to work, seek employment, or attend school, provided no other adult is available and the expense rules are met. It limits deductions when the care is for a full-time student, excludes certain situations such as a provider who is a household member, and specifies what the borrower must document to qualify.

- **Verification of child care expenses**: The document lists required proof, such as identifying the children, the adult enabled to work or attend school, the provider, hours, cost, and proof that expenses are not reimbursed. It also notes acceptable ways to document child care.

- **Elderly household deduction**: A single $400 deduction is allowed for an elderly household, defined as one with a party to the note age 62 or older or a person with a disability. It also explains when a surviving household can continue to be treated as elderly after the death of a qualifying family member.

- **Examples**: The section includes a child care example showing how expenses are separated between work and school time and how the deduction is limited by earnings only for the work-related portion.

nodes.title: D. Elderly Household Deduction
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate adjusted income by applying specific household deductions. It focuses on the elderly household deduction, which allows a single $400 deduction for households with a member age 62 or older or a person with a disability, including rules for when a surviving household may still be treated as elderly. It also provides an example of child care deduction calculations, showing how expenses are treated differently for time spent working versus attending school, and how child care deductions are limited by earned income for work-related hours.

nodes.title: E. Deduction for Disability Assistance Expense
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how annual and adjusted income are calculated, focusing on two major deductions: disability assistance expenses and medical expenses. It describes the rules for qualifying disability assistance costs, including attendant care and auxiliary apparatus needed to enable a person with disabilities or another household member to work, and notes requirements such as documentation, verification, and limits based on income and reimbursements. It also gives examples of eligible and ineligible disability-related expenses and shows a sample calculation of the deduction. The document then covers the medical expense deduction for elderly households only, explaining that unreimbursed medical costs exceeding 3 percent of annual income may be deducted, that all family members’ expenses may count, and listing typical eligible and excluded medical expenses.

nodes.title: F. Deduction for Medical Expenses (for Elderly Households Only)
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains how to calculate income for loan and subsidy purposes under HB-2-3550. It focuses on:

- **Medical expense deductions for elderly households**, including the rule that unreimbursed medical costs can be deducted only when they exceed 3% of annual income, and that medical expenses for the entire family may be counted. It also lists typical deductible medical expenses and examples of excluded expenses.
- **How to estimate future medical expenses**, emphasizing the use of realistic historical information and partial inclusion of large bills based on expected payments in the coming year.
- **Examples of medical expense calculations**, showing how to apply the 3% threshold and how installment payments on past medical bills are treated over time.
- **Calculating repayment income**, including counting only income of persons on the note and “grossing up” certain nontaxable income sources by 120% to account for tax savings.
- **Calculating income from assets**, describing when asset income is considered, what forms/documents may be used for verification, and noting that all household members’ assets may be included for annual income purposes.

nodes.title: Calculating Repayment Income
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains how to calculate repayment income and income from assets for loan eligibility. It states that repayment income includes only the income of persons who will be on the note, and that nontaxable income may be “grossed up” by 120 percent to account for tax savings when determining Standard PITI and TD ratios. It also covers how income from assets is evaluated, noting that asset income is considered for all household members, with different rules applying during origination versus later subsidy reviews, and references forms used to verify deposits, pensions, annuities, and asset disposition. An example demonstrates grossing up nontaxable income by converting $5,000 into $6,000 and adding it to taxable income to produce revised repayment income.

nodes.title: Calculating Income from Assets
nodes.summary: This partial document explains how to calculate repayment income and income from assets for a household loan application. It states that repayment income should include only the income of persons who will be on the note, and that certain nontaxable income sources may be “grossed up” by 120 percent to account for tax savings. It also describes how income from assets is used, including when asset information is reviewed, and provides examples of acceptable documentation. In addition, it lists the types of assets that must be counted in annual income, such as savings, investments, real property equity, retirement funds, lump-sum receipts, foreign assets, and investment personal property, as well as types that are excluded, such as necessary personal property, business assets, irrevocable trusts, restricted land interests, health and college savings accounts, and assets used to reduce the loan amount.

nodes.title: Types of Assets
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to calculate income from assets under HB-2-3550, including which assets must be counted, which are excluded, and how certain retirement accounts are treated. It lists examples of countable non-retirement assets such as savings, checking, investments, real property equity, trusts, lump-sum receipts, foreign assets, and investment personal property. It also covers retirement assets that may be counted for applicants, like withdrawable IRA, 401(k), and Keogh funds. The document then identifies assets that are not counted, such as necessary personal property, assets used in an active business or farm, irrevocable trusts, restricted Indian land, certain health and college savings accounts, and loan-reduction funds. Finally, it provides examples showing that non-periodic IRA withdrawals are not counted as income, and that a Keogh account continues to be counted as an asset even when periodic withdrawals begin.

nodes.title: Withdrawals from IRAs or 401K Accounts
nodes.summary: This document section explains how to calculate income from assets, focusing on retirement accounts such as IRAs, 401(k)s, and Keogh accounts. It gives examples showing that one-time withdrawals from retirement accounts are not counted as income if they are not periodic payments. It also illustrates that when a person begins receiving regular withdrawals from a Keogh account, the account is still treated as an asset, and its cash value and imputed income must be determined, while the withdrawn amount itself is not counted as income.

nodes.title: Withdrawals from a Keogh Account
nodes.summary: The partial document covers two main topics from USDA housing program guidance. First, it explains how to calculate income from assets, including examples showing that non-periodic withdrawals from retirement accounts like IRAs, 401(k)s, or Keogh accounts are not counted as income, while the account itself remains an asset for determining cash value and imputed income. It also references a worksheet for computing income. Second, it introduces Chapter 5 on special servicing, describing how the agency helps borrowers who are unable or unwilling to keep up with loan terms, the role of the Servicing and Asset Management Office, how leveraged/participation loans are identified, and the time frames for servicing action, including when accounts are considered past due, when late fees apply, and when loans may be liquidated or accelerated without further servicing.

structure.title: CHAPTER 5: SPECIAL SERVICING
structure.summary: The partial document introduces the USDA Agency’s special servicing process for borrowers who have trouble repaying their loans. It explains that most borrowers repay normally, but when financial or personal difficulties arise, the Agency uses special servicing tools to help borrowers become current and avoid liquidation. If those efforts fail, the Agency may liquidate the loan in the most cost-effective way. The text also notes that the Servicing and Asset Management Office reviews new loans to correctly classify leveraged/participation loans and ensure lender information is accurate. Finally, it outlines key servicing time frames, including when an account is considered past due, when late fees apply, and when monthly-pay or annual-pay loans may be accelerated or liquidated without further servicing.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF SPECIAL SERVICING [7 CFR 3550.201]
nodes.summary: This section provides an overview of special servicing for loans, explaining that most borrowers repay normally but some need assistance due to financial, personal, or unwillingness issues. It outlines the Agency’s special servicing approach, which aims to help borrowers become current and avoid default, and notes that if assistance fails, the Agency will liquidate the loan in the most cost-effective way. The text also describes servicing review procedures for new loans, especially leveraged/participation loans, including proper identification of the lender and related account information. Finally, it explains the time frame for servicing action, defining when an account is past due, when late fees apply, and when monthly-pay or annual-pay loans may be liquidated or accelerated without further servicing.

nodes.title: A. Time Frame for Servicing Action [ 7 CFR 3550.202]
nodes.summary:
The partial document is a section of a USDA Rural Development servicing manual focused on “Special Servicing” for delinquent loans. It explains that most borrowers repay normally, but when borrowers face financial or personal hardship—or are unwilling to pay—the Agency uses special servicing tools to help them become current before moving toward liquidation if necessary.

Main points covered include:

- **Purpose of special servicing:** to help distressed borrowers repay their loans through counseling and other assistance, and only liquidate the loan if repayment is not possible.
- **Loan review and identification:** the Servicing Office reviews new loans to correctly identify leveraged/participation loans and ensure lender information is accurate.
- **Time frames for action:** defines when an account is past due, when late fees are charged, and when liquidation or acceleration may occur for monthly-pay and annual-pay accounts.
- **Examples of delinquency timing:** illustrations show how three missed payments or two scheduled payments past due for three consecutive months can trigger liquidation eligibility.
- **Identifying troubled accounts early:** borrowers may be referred for special servicing before reaching formal delinquency thresholds, especially when problems are reported early or on leveraged/participation loans.
- **Developing a servicing strategy:** the Servicing Office must work with the borrower to create a tailored plan, document contacts, and consider the status of any other lender on leveraged/participation loans.
- **Available servicing tools:** the exhibit lists options such as regular follow-up and counseling, delinquency workout agreements, protective advances, payment assistance, and moratoriums.
- **Combining tools:** examples show how multiple servicing tools may be used together, such as payment subsidies plus a moratorium or a workout agreement after job loss or illness.

nodes.title: B. Identifying Special Servicing Accounts
nodes.summary: The document section explains special servicing procedures for delinquent or at-risk borrowers. It focuses on how to identify accounts that need special servicing early, including cases where the borrower reports hardship before missing payments or when past-due amounts reach about two scheduled payments. It also describes how the Servicing Office should work with each borrower to develop an individualized servicing strategy, document all contacts and actions, and consider the status of any leveraged/participation loan with other lenders. The text outlines possible servicing tools such as payment assistance, moratoriums, delinquency workout agreements, promises to pay, and protective advances, and includes examples showing how multiple tools may be combined depending on the borrower’s situation.

nodes.title: C. Developing a Servicing Strategy
nodes.summary: The document section explains how special servicing accounts should be identified and managed. It notes that borrowers may be referred for special servicing before or as soon as payment problems begin, including cases of borrower notification, lender contact on leveraged/participation loans, or when past due amounts reach about two scheduled payments. It then describes the need to develop an individualized servicing strategy for each borrower, with the Servicing Office working directly with the borrower, documenting all contacts, and considering the status of any other lender in leveraged/participation loans. The servicing response may include tools such as payment assistance, moratoriums, delinquency workout agreements, promises to pay, or protective advances. The section ends with examples showing how multiple special servicing tools can be combined, such as using subsidies or moratoriums after job loss or reamortizing a loan after a moratorium due to illness.

nodes.title: D. Borrowers Who Have Filed for Bankruptcy
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA Rural Development special servicing procedures for borrowers facing bankruptcy and delinquency. It covers how servicing actions must be handled case by case with guidance from the Office of General Counsel, including sending bankruptcy petitions to OGC, obtaining bankruptcy notices for the file, stopping collection actions, and continuing monthly statements unless told otherwise. It outlines proof of claim requirements and the different treatment of Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 bankruptcy cases, including payment plans, acceleration restrictions, reaffirmation agreements, and when normal servicing can resume.

The document also describes the development of a problem case report, an automated record used by counselors to track loan status, borrower information, liens, servicing actions, and recommendations for foreclosure or liquidation. Finally, it introduces delinquency workout agreements, explaining that they allow borrowers to avoid liquidation by making extra payments to cure delinquency within a set period, and notes basic eligibility and approval conditions.

nodes.title: E. Developing a Problem Case Report
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing special servicing procedures, focusing on two main topics:

1. **Problem Case Reports**
   - Describes the purpose of the problem case report as an automated summary of the loan and borrower’s situation.
   - Explains that it should include loan status, servicing actions taken or considered, delinquent taxes, liens, borrower income/assets, property value, and recommendations about foreclosure and handling other lienholders.
   - Emphasizes that the report is a “living document” updated throughout servicing.
   - States that when the counselor determines the borrower cannot succeed or will not cooperate, the report is completed and submitted with a liquidation recommendation for supervisor review and approval.

2. **Delinquency Workout Agreements**
   - Explains that these agreements allow borrowers to avoid liquidation by making extra payments in addition to regular payments so the account becomes current within two years or the remaining loan term, whichever is shorter.
   - Covers basic eligibility, noting that both program and nonprogram loan borrowers may qualify.
   - Mentions limits on approving more than one agreement within a two-year period without supervisory approval.
   - Begins describing conditions for approval, including requirements for monthly payment borrowers.

nodes.title: DELINQUENCY WORKOUT AGREEMENTS [7 CFR 3550.205]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains special servicing procedures for delinquent loans under USDA Rural Development rules. It covers the “problem case report,” an automated record used to summarize loan status, servicing actions, borrower and property information, other liens, and recommendations for foreclosure or liquidation. The report is described as a living document that must be updated throughout servicing and reviewed by a supervisor before liquidation is recommended.

The document also introduces delinquency workout agreements, which allow borrowers to avoid liquidation by making extra payments in addition to their regular installment so the loan can return to current status within two years or the remaining loan term, whichever is shorter. It notes eligibility considerations, including that both program and nonprogram borrowers may qualify, but multiple agreements with the same borrower within two years require supervisor approval.

nodes.title: A. Eligibility
nodes.summary: The partial document explains special servicing procedures for delinquent loans, focusing on two main topics: the problem case report and delinquency workout agreements. It describes the problem case report as a living, automated document used to summarize the loan’s status, servicing actions, borrower and property information, liens, and foreclosure recommendations, and notes that it must be completed and approved when liquidation is being considered. It also outlines delinquency workout agreements, including who may be eligible, related servicing options such as moratoriums or subsidies, and how to process them by determining a reasonable repayment amount based on the borrower’s finances. The document emphasizes that repayment plans must be realistic, generally not exceed 24 months, and be properly documented and confirmed.

nodes.title: B. Related Servicing Actions
nodes.summary: The partial document explains delinquency workout agreements for borrowers who are behind on payments, including when they are appropriate, how they differ from other servicing actions like moratoriums or subsidies, and how to process them. It covers calculating a reasonable repayment amount based on the borrower’s finances, using a 41 percent income ratio and allowable expense deductions, setting a repayment term of up to 24 months, and situations where an agreement should not be established. It also describes confirming agreements, documentation requirements for short repayment periods, and the importance of making sure any promise to repay is realistic and supported by the borrower’s financial situation.

nodes.title: C. Processing Delinquency Workout Agreements
nodes.summary: The partial document explains delinquency workout agreements for borrowers who are behind on payments, including when they are appropriate and how they are processed. It emphasizes first understanding the cause of delinquency, noting that if the problem is loss of income or unexpected expenses, a moratorium or additional subsidy may be more suitable than a workout agreement. It then describes how the Servicing Office determines a reasonable repayment amount and term, using the borrower’s financial information, necessary family expenses, and a 41 percent of annual income calculation, with repayment generally limited to 24 months. The document also covers confirmation requirements, such as documenting reaffirmation agreements and obtaining financial information before a moratorium expires, and stresses avoiding unrealistic repayment promises that are not supported by the borrower’s circumstances.

nodes.title: 1. Determining a Reasonable Payment
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains USDA Rural Development servicing guidance for delinquency workout agreements. It covers when such agreements are appropriate, emphasizing that the Servicing Office should first understand the cause of delinquency and consider alternatives like moratoriums or additional payment subsidy if the borrower has lost income or faced unexpected expenses.

It then outlines how delinquency workout agreements are processed, including how to determine a reasonable payment amount and repayment term. The document describes using the borrower’s financial information, necessary family expenses, and a formula based on 41 percent of annual income, with deductions for housing-related costs and certain debts, to decide whether a workout agreement can be established, generally with a maximum repayment period of 24 months.

Finally, it discusses confirming the agreement, including documentation requirements for borrowers who can repay within 30 days and the need for financial information before moratorium expiration. It also stresses that servicers should counsel borrowers to avoid unrealistic repayment promises and should not approve workout agreements unless the borrower’s ability to repay is supported by available information.

nodes.title: 2. Confirming the Agreement
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains policies and procedures for handling delinquent USDA Rural Housing loans through delinquency workout agreements and related servicing actions. It covers:

- When a delinquency workout agreement is appropriate versus other options like a moratorium or payment subsidy.
- How servicing offices determine a reasonable repayment amount and term based on the borrower’s financial situation, with a repayment period generally not exceeding 24 months.
- Rules for confirming repayment agreements, including documentation, borrower financial information, and billing statements for formal agreements.
- Treatment of late fees during an active workout agreement.
- What happens if a borrower fails to comply, including cancellation of the agreement and possible liquidation if the loan cannot be brought current.
- Conditions under which a successful borrower may reamortize the remaining balance, including eligibility requirements and escrow/monthly payment conversion rules for certain annual-pay borrowers.
- An introduction to payment assistance as a special servicing action to reduce future delinquencies by lowering monthly payments to an affordable level.

nodes.title: 3. Late Fees on Delinquency Workout Agreements
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing servicing procedures for delinquency workout agreements and payment assistance. It explains when borrowers who are delinquent must receive written repayment confirmations or formal adjusted billing statements, how late fees are waived during compliant workout periods, and what happens if a borrower fails to follow the agreement, including automatic cancellation and possible liquidation. It also describes reamortization as a way to bring an account current, including eligibility requirements and conditions such as converting to monthly payments and establishing escrow. Finally, it introduces payment assistance as a special servicing action used to reduce monthly payments to an affordable level and help prevent future delinquencies.

nodes.title: 4. Borrower Failure to Pay
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for handling loan delinquencies and related servicing actions. It covers delinquency workout agreements, including when written confirmation or adjusted billing statements are required, how late fees are treated during the agreement, and what happens if the borrower fails to comply. It also describes the option to bring an account current through reamortization, including eligibility rules and required changes such as moving to monthly payments and establishing escrow. Finally, it introduces payment assistance as a special servicing action used to reduce monthly payments and help prevent future delinquencies.

nodes.title: 5. Bringing the Account Current Through Reamortization
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing delinquency servicing procedures. It covers delinquency workout agreements, including when borrowers must receive written confirmation, when formal agreements and adjusted billing statements are required, and how late fees are waived during compliant payment periods. It also describes what happens if a borrower fails to follow the agreement, including automatic cancellation after being more than 30 days past due and possible liquidation if the account cannot be brought current. In addition, it explains when a delinquent account may be reamortized, the eligibility requirements for reamortization, and related conditions such as converting to monthly payments and establishing escrow accounts. Finally, it introduces payment assistance as a special servicing action used to lower monthly payments and help prevent future delinquencies.

nodes.title: USING PAYMENT ASSISTANCE AS A SPECIAL SERVICING ACTION [7 CFR 3550.204]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Development servicing procedures, focusing on how payment assistance is used, when refinancing may be allowed to enable payment assistance, what steps servicers must take while a payment subsidy request is being processed, and the rules for protective advances. It explains that payment assistance alone does not cure delinquency and must be combined with a delinquency workout agreement or moratorium, then outlines eligibility checks and refinancing considerations. It also describes that late fees continue to accrue during subsidy processing and notes that protective advances may be made to pay expenses needed to protect the government’s interest when a subsequent loan is not possible, with cases referred from the Servicing Office to the Field Office.

nodes.title: A. Refinancing Loans to Permit Payment Assistance
nodes.summary: The document explains two servicing topics for delinquent or at-risk borrowers. First, it describes how payment assistance can be used as a special servicing action, emphasizing that assistance alone does not cure delinquency and must be combined with a delinquency workout agreement or moratorium. It also covers when refinancing may be allowed to enable payment assistance, what the servicer must check before proposing refinancing, and the process of referring the case to the Field Office. Second, it introduces protective advances, which are Agency funds used to pay fees or services necessary to protect the Government’s interest in property when a subsequent loan is not possible, and notes that such cases are referred from the Servicing Office to the Field Office.

nodes.title: B. Servicing Actions While the Request for Payment Subsidies Is Processed
nodes.summary: The partial document covers servicing actions for delinquent loans, focusing on how payment assistance can be used only together with a delinquency workout agreement or moratorium, not by itself. It explains when loans may be refinanced to allow payment assistance, including eligibility checks, using LoanServ to estimate new payments, and involving the Field Office to originate a new loan if feasible. It also notes that delinquency can continue to accrue late fees while income eligibility for payment subsidies is being verified. In addition, the document introduces protective advances, describing when the Agency may advance funds to protect its interest in property, and how Servicing Offices should refer such cases to the Field Office.

nodes.title: PROTECTIVE ADVANCES [7 CFR 3550.206]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains two main servicing topics for Rural Development loans:

1. **Using payment assistance as a special servicing action**
   - Payment assistance alone does not cure delinquency and must be combined with a delinquency workout agreement or a moratorium.
   - It describes when **loan refinancing** may be used to enable payment assistance, including eligibility checks and estimating the borrower’s new monthly payment.
   - It also notes that while income is being verified and payment assistance is being processed, late fees continue to accrue.

2. **Protective advances**
   - The Agency may advance funds to pay necessary costs that protect its interest in the property, especially when a later loan cannot be made.
   - It explains how advances are recovered, how repayment may be handled, and how advances for **property repairs** are determined and approved.
   - The document also covers checking for alternative funding sources, documenting urgency or ineligibility for a subsequent loan, IRS reporting requirements, and other uses of protective advances such as paying off a prior lienholder to avoid foreclosure.

nodes.title: A. Advances for Property Repairs
nodes.summary:
This section explains USDA Rural Development’s policy for “protective advances,” which are loan funds advanced to protect the government’s security interest in the property. It covers how advances are charged to the borrower’s account, when repayment is due, and options if the borrower cannot repay immediately.

The document then focuses on two main categories:

- **Advances for property repairs:** These are only used when repairs are necessary to protect the government’s interest and cannot be financed another way. It describes how the need for repairs is identified, the Field Office’s responsibilities for inspection, environmental review, cost estimates, and approval, and the requirement to first consider whether the borrower qualifies for a subsequent loan. It also explains documentation requirements when alternatives are not feasible and notes tax reporting rules for vendors.

- **Advances for other purposes:** These are also allowed when needed to protect the government’s interest, and may be approved by the Field Office or Servicing Office. An example given is paying off a prior lien holder to prevent foreclosure when the agency holds a junior lien.

Overall, the passage outlines when protective advances may be made, who handles them, and the procedures and safeguards involved.

nodes.title: 1. Determining the Need for Repairs
nodes.summary:
The document explains USDA Rural Development’s policy on protective advances under HB-2-3550, Section 5.4. It describes how advances are charged to the borrower’s account, when repayment is due, and options if the borrower cannot repay immediately, including scheduling repayment or reamortizing the loan.

It then focuses on protective advances for property repairs, stating that these are used only to protect the Government’s interest in the security property and only when repairs cannot be funded through a later loan. It outlines how interest is applied, how advances are handled when a borrower has multiple loans, and the responsibilities of Field Offices in identifying repair needs, inspecting the property, completing environmental review, preparing work descriptions and cost estimates, and approving the advance.

The document also covers how Field Offices must first determine whether the borrower may qualify for a subsequent loan before approving a protective advance, and it explains when an advance may be processed immediately due to urgency or lack of loan eligibility. It notes documentation requirements when alternatives are not feasible and mentions IRS reporting rules for vendor payments.

Finally, it introduces protective advances for other purposes, such as paying off a prior lien holder to prevent foreclosure when the Agency holds a junior lien, and explains that these advances are handled by Field Offices or the Servicing Office depending on loan jurisdiction.

nodes.title: 2. Alternative Sources of Funding
nodes.summary:
The document section explains USDA/Rural Development “protective advances,” which are amounts advanced to protect the government’s interest in a borrower’s secured property and charged to the borrower’s account, usually due with the next payment. It covers how repayments may be handled if the borrower cannot pay immediately.

Main points include:
- **Protective advances for property repairs**: used only when needed to protect the security property and generally only if repairs cannot be financed through a subsequent loan.
- **Interest and charging rules**: advances accrue interest at the promissory note rate of the loan they are charged against, and if there are multiple loans on the same property, they are charged to the largest loan.
- **Determining repair needs**: repairs may be identified by the borrower, Field Office, or Servicing Office; the Field Office must inspect the property, complete environmental review, define the work, estimate costs, and approve the advance.
- **Checking alternative funding**: before approving an advance, the Field Office must determine whether a subsequent loan is feasible; if not, it must document why a protective advance is necessary.
- **Tax reporting**: protective advances paid to vendors are reported to the IRS on Form 1099, and neither the borrower nor an RD employee may be listed as the vendor.
- **Other uses of protective advances**: they may also be used for other government-protection purposes, such as paying off a prior lienholder in foreclosure situations where the agency is a junior lienholder.
- **Office responsibility**: approvals and processing depend on whether the loan is under Field Office or Servicing Office jurisdiction, with coordination required when appropriate.
