nodes.title: B. Advances for Other Purposes
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Rural Development servicing rules on protective advances and moratoriums for loans. It explains that protective advances are amounts the agency may charge to a borrower’s account to protect the government’s interest, with repayment due at the next scheduled payment or through repayment arrangements if the borrower cannot pay in full. It describes protective advances for property repairs, including how the need for repairs is identified, the Field Office’s responsibilities, the requirement to consider alternative funding such as a subsequent loan, and that advances bear interest and may be reported to the IRS. It also lists other purposes for protective advances, such as paying off a prior lienholder, taxes, insurance premiums, and local assessments.

The document then introduces moratoriums, which allow suspension of borrower payments for up to two years to help avoid foreclosure during temporary financial hardship. It notes that borrowers must first be considered for payment subsidy, that nonprogram borrowers are not eligible, and that borrowers with leveraged/participation loans remain responsible for those payments. Finally, it outlines eligibility requirements for a moratorium, including a substantial decrease in repayment income or unexpected unreimbursed expenses due to illness, injury, death, or damage to the security property, and mentions that multiple moratoriums may be granted over the life of the loan if new qualifying circumstances arise.

nodes.title: MORATORIUMS [7 CFR 3550.207]
nodes.summary: The document section explains USDA loan moratoriums under HB-2-3550, including what a moratorium is, who is eligible, and the conditions for approval. It states that a moratorium temporarily suspends required payments for up to two years to help borrowers facing temporary financial hardship avoid foreclosure. Before a moratorium is granted, borrowers must first be considered for payment subsidy assistance, and nonprogram borrowers are not eligible. It also notes that borrowers with leveraged/participation loans must still make those loan payments unless special consideration is given. The eligibility rules require that the borrower’s inability to pay be due to circumstances beyond their control, such as at least a 20 percent drop in repayment income, unexpected unreimbursed medical or death-related expenses, or unreimbursed damage to the security property. The section also explains that borrowers may receive more than one moratorium over the life of the loan without a waiting period if they qualify again.

nodes.title: A. Eligibility
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the USDA Rural Housing Service moratorium policy for program borrowers who are temporarily unable to make mortgage payments due to circumstances beyond their control. It defines a moratorium as a temporary suspension of payment requirements for up to two years to help avoid foreclosure, and notes that borrowers must first be considered for payment subsidy assistance before a moratorium is approved. It also clarifies that nonprogram borrowers are not eligible.

The document outlines eligibility requirements, including a significant drop in repayment income, unexpected unreimbursed expenses from illness, injury, death, or property damage, occupancy of the dwelling, no current acceleration of the loan, and that the loan was originally made on program terms. It also explains how borrowers request a moratorium, how the Servicing Office evaluates the request, and stresses careful documentation.

In addition, the text discusses cases involving leveraged/participation loans, multiple moratoriums over the life of the loan, and when a moratorium may not be appropriate if recovery within the maximum period is unlikely. Finally, it lists key evaluation steps such as confirming facts, determining whether moratorium is the right remedy, checking for SCRA protections, and setting a monitoring schedule.

nodes.title: B. Initiating the Request for a Moratorium
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses USDA Rural Housing Service loan servicing rules for moratoriums under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 5.5. It explains the conditions a borrower must meet to be eligible, including occupying the dwelling, having a loan not currently accelerated, and having a loan written on program terms. It then covers how a moratorium request is initiated, noting that either the borrower or servicing office may start the discussion, but the borrower must formally request it and provide financial information, and the request must be carefully documented. The document also outlines how the servicing office evaluates the request, emphasizing that a moratorium is not suitable if recovery within the maximum period is unrealistic, and that the servicer should assess eligibility for payment assistance, whether the borrower’s inability to pay is beyond their control, and the borrower’s financial situation. Finally, it lists four key steps in evaluating a moratorium request: confirming the facts, determining whether a moratorium is the right remedy, checking whether the borrower is protected by the SCRA, and setting an appropriate monitoring schedule.

nodes.title: C. Evaluating and Acting on the Request
nodes.summary: This partial document explains the USDA Rural Housing Service moratorium process for borrowers under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 5.5. It covers: eligibility conditions for a moratorium, how a borrower request is initiated and documented, and how the Servicing Office evaluates whether a moratorium is appropriate based on financial hardship, temporary circumstances, payment assistance, and related loan factors. It also summarizes the four key evaluation steps, including checking for SCRA protection and setting a monitoring schedule. In addition, it describes how borrowers are monitored during the moratorium, including periodic reviews, required documentation, and verification of other loan payments. Finally, it begins the section on cancellation of a moratorium, noting that it may be canceled if the borrower fails to provide requested financial information.

nodes.title: D. Monitoring Borrower Accounts During the Moratorium
nodes.summary: This section explains the rules for granting, monitoring, canceling, and ending moratoriums on loan payments. It covers how to decide whether a borrower’s financial hardship is temporary and whether a moratorium is appropriate, including special considerations for leveraged/participation loans. It also describes ongoing account monitoring during the moratorium, such as periodic review of the borrower’s situation, required documentation, and verification of other loan payments. The document then outlines when a moratorium must be canceled and how the borrower is notified. Finally, it explains how payments resume after the moratorium, including reamortization of the loan and possible forgiveness of accrued interest if needed to keep payments affordable.

nodes.title: E. Cancellation of a Moratorium
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing moratorium rules, including when a moratorium can be cancelled or continued, how borrowers are notified, and what must happen as payments resume. It covers the Servicing Office’s review before expiration, the need for updated financial information, loan re-amortization after the moratorium ends, and the possible forgiveness of accrued interest if repayment would otherwise be unaffordable. It also notes special interest-rate restoration and reduction requirements for borrowers protected under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act.

nodes.title: F. Resumption of Payments
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses moratoriums in USDA Rural Housing servicing, including when a moratorium may be cancelled, how borrowers are notified, and when continuation can be justified. It also covers the resumption of payments before a moratorium expires, requiring the Servicing Office to review the borrower’s ability to resume payments and obtain updated financial information if needed. Finally, it explains what happens after the moratorium ends or is cancelled, including loan re-amortization, possible escrow requirements, and circumstances under which accrued interest may be forgiven to make the new payment affordable, with special rules for borrowers protected under the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act.

nodes.title: 1. Reamortization
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses USDA servicing rules for moratoriums on loan payments. It explains when a moratorium may be cancelled, including when the reason for it no longer exists or when the borrower cannot verify that deferred payments were used for unexpected unreimbursed expenses. It also covers borrower notification and the chance to provide supporting information within 30 days to continue the moratorium. The document then outlines how the Servicing Office reviews the borrower’s ability to resume payments before the moratorium ends, including obtaining updated financial information if needed. Finally, it describes two post-moratorium options: reamortization of the loan to include deferred amounts and required escrow, and possible forgiveness of accrued interest if needed to make the new payment affordable, while also noting special interest rate rules for borrowers protected under the SCRA.

nodes.title: 2. Forgiving Interest
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing servicing procedures for moratoriums and liquidation recommendations. It explains when a moratorium may be cancelled, how borrowers must be notified, and the steps for reviewing a borrower’s ability to resume payments before the moratorium ends. It also describes reamortization of the loan, possible forgiveness of accrued interest to make payments affordable, and the requirement to liquidate the account if the borrower still cannot resume payments. The document then begins a section on liquidation recommendations, outlining how the servicer should discuss voluntary liquidation with the borrower, document servicing actions, and submit a problem case report for supervisory approval.

nodes.title: 3. Borrowers Unable to Resume Payments
nodes.summary: The partial document explains what happens when borrowers cannot resume payments after a moratorium and outlines the liquidation recommendation process. It states that if the borrower still cannot make payments after the approved moratorium period, the account must be liquidated under Chapter 6. It also describes how, during special servicing, the Servicer may determine that the borrower is unlikely to succeed and liquidation is appropriate. In that case, the Servicer should discuss voluntary liquidation with the borrower, prepare a problem case report recommending liquidation, submit it for supervisory approval, and document all servicing actions and the reasons for the recommendation.

nodes.title: THE LIQUIDATION RECOMMENDATION
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing servicing procedures related to moratoriums, liquidation recommendations, and liquidation options. It explains that if a borrower cannot resume payments after an approved moratorium, the account must be liquidated. It then outlines how servicers should recommend liquidation when it becomes clear the borrower cannot succeed, including discussing voluntary liquidation with the borrower, documenting servicing actions and reasons in a problem case report, and obtaining supervisory approval. The document emphasizes that the servicer should first try all appropriate special servicing tools, but if liquidation is in the government’s best interest, prompt action should follow. It also begins Chapter 6 on liquidation and acquisition, focusing on voluntary liquidation through sale of the property, why sale is usually the preferred option, how sales are handled when debt may not be fully satisfied, and the role of authorized selling expenses in the transaction.

structure.title: CHAPTER 6: LIQUIDATION AND ACQUISITION [7 CFR 3550.211]
structure.summary:
The partial document explains the agency’s approach to loan liquidation when a borrower cannot continue with the loan. It emphasizes that the servicer should first use all appropriate servicing tools to help the borrower become current, but if liquidation is necessary, the servicer must evaluate the available options and recommend the one that best serves the Government’s interest by maximizing net recovery. It also states that once a liquidation recommendation is approved, the Servicing and Asset Management Office should act quickly.

The main liquidation topic introduced is voluntary liquidation, especially encouraging the borrower to sell the property. This is described as the preferred option because it can avoid liquidation and property ownership costs for the Agency while giving the borrower the best chance of being released from the debt without major credit damage. The text explains that a borrower may sell even after loan acceleration, and handling of the sale depends on whether the proceeds will fully satisfy the debt. If the sale will not fully cover the debt, the Field Office may handle or approve the sale in the Agency’s best interest, with different procedures for centralized and non-centralized states.

The excerpt also begins defining authorized selling expenses, listing typical costs that may be allowed in a property sale, such as real estate commissions, certain buyer credit costs, taxes, liens, deed preparation, title-related fees, inspections, insurance, surveys, and stamps.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF LIQUIDATION OPTIONS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how a servicer should handle borrowers who are unable to continue making loan payments, emphasizing that liquidation options should be reviewed when continued repayment is unlikely and the option chosen must maximize the government’s net recovery. It introduces the overall liquidation process and then focuses on voluntary liquidation, especially encouraging the borrower to sell the property as the preferred solution because it can avoid foreclosure costs and reduce harm to the borrower’s credit. The text also describes how a sale is handled depending on whether the proceeds will fully satisfy the debt, including when the Servicing Office or Field Office takes responsibility. In addition, it discusses agency approval for sales that may not fully cover the debt and outlines the types of authorized selling expenses that may be paid during a property sale.

nodes.title: A. Voluntary Liquidation
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures for loan liquidation when a borrower cannot continue making payments. It explains that the servicer should first try special servicing tools to help bring the account current, but if that is not possible, the servicer must evaluate liquidation options and recommend the one that gives the Government the greatest net recovery. It then introduces the chapter on liquidation and acquisition and begins an overview of voluntary liquidation, focusing on encouraging the borrower to sell the property. The text explains why a property sale is often the best option for both the Agency and the borrower, outlines how the account is handled depending on whether sale proceeds will fully satisfy the debt, and identifies when the Field Office or Servicing Office may approve a sale. It also defines authorized selling expenses, listing common costs that may be paid in connection with a property sale.

nodes.title: 1. Encouraging the Borrower to Sell
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines liquidation and acquisition procedures for delinquent loans, focusing on how the Servicer should choose the option that provides the greatest net recovery for the Government. It explains that special servicing tools should first be used to help borrowers cure financial problems, but if continued repayment is not feasible, liquidation options must be evaluated and acted on promptly.

Main topics covered include:

- **Overview of liquidation options**
  - The Agency’s goal is to minimize losses to both the Government and the borrower.
  - Decisions are based on net recovery and best interest of the Agency.

- **Voluntary liquidation**
  - **Encouraging the borrower to sell the property** is presented as the preferred option.
  - It explains when sales may be handled by the Servicing Office versus the Field Office.
  - It describes **short sale** approval, including required documents such as the sales contract, appraisal, and estimated expenses.
  - The text details approval and denial letter requirements, including informing the borrower that remaining debt may still be owed.
  - It addresses **authorized selling expenses** and when the Agency may advance them.
  - It covers handling of **junior liens**, including possible settlement if needed to complete the sale.
  - It explains the **debt settlement process** when sale proceeds are less than the debt, including the Debt Settlement Package, required forms, and how funds and records are transmitted.
  - An example is provided showing how a sale below debt is evaluated using net recovery.

- **Deed in lieu of foreclosure**
  - The borrower may convey the property to the Agency after acceleration.
  - Acceptance is allowed only if it yields greater net recovery than foreclosure.
  - Borrowers are not automatically released from remaining debt unless debt settlement procedures are followed.

- **Borrowers in bankruptcy**
  - Acceptance of property conveyed through Bankruptcy Court requires supervisory and OGC approval.
  - The Agency generally accepts if it can obtain title free of other liens and substantially recover the debt.

- **Foreclosure**
  - Explains that loans are accelerated when liquidation is appropriate.
  - Borrowers may still attempt voluntary liquidation before foreclosure occurs.
  - If another lien holder initiates foreclosure, the Servicing Office must determine the best strategy to maximize Government recovery, including joining the action, bidding at sale, or settling for assignment.
  - If redemption rights exist, the Servicing Office Supervisor decides whether to redeem based on estimated net recovery.

nodes.title: 2. Deed in Lieu of Foreclosure
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation options available to the Agency when a borrower defaults on a loan secured by property. It covers accepting a deed in lieu of foreclosure, including the requirement that the Agency approve it only if it provides a better net recovery than foreclosure and that borrower debt may remain unless settled separately. It also describes handling cases where a borrower is in bankruptcy, where supervisor and legal approval are needed before accepting title from a Bankruptcy Court. Finally, it outlines foreclosure procedures, including Agency-initiated foreclosure after loan acceleration and strategies when another lien holder begins foreclosure, such as joining the action, bidding at sale, settling for assignment, or redeeming the property if that would maximize recovery.

nodes.title: 3. Borrowers in Bankruptcy
nodes.summary: The partial document describes liquidation options available to the Agency when a loan has been accelerated and the borrower’s security property must be resolved. It covers deed in lieu of foreclosure, where the borrower voluntarily conveys the property and the Agency accepts only if it results in greater net recovery than foreclosure, with supervisor approval and possible remaining debt liability unless debt settlement procedures are followed. It also covers borrower bankruptcy cases, where acceptance of property from a Bankruptcy Court requires supervisor and OGC approval, generally only if title will be clear of other liens and the Agency can substantially recover its debt. The document then explains foreclosure procedures, including Agency-initiated foreclosure after acceleration and the possibility of voluntary liquidation before sale, as well as foreclosure by another lien holder, where the Servicing Office must choose the strategy that maximizes government recovery, such as joining the action, bidding at sale, settling with the other lien holder, or redeeming the property if the government has redemption rights.

nodes.title: B. Foreclosure
nodes.summary: The excerpt describes the Agency’s options for liquidating a defaulted loan, focusing on deed in lieu of foreclosure, borrowers in bankruptcy, and foreclosure procedures. It explains that the Agency may accept a deed in lieu only when it yields greater net recovery than foreclosure and when approved by a supervisor, while the borrower remains liable for any remaining debt unless debt settlement procedures are completed. For bankruptcy cases, acceptance of a court-offered conveyance requires supervisor and OGC approval, and is generally allowed when the Agency can obtain clear title and substantially recover the debt. The document then outlines foreclosure actions, including Agency-initiated foreclosure after acceleration and strategies when another lien holder begins foreclosure, such as joining the action, bidding at sale, settling for assignment, or redeeming the property if doing so is expected to improve net recovery.

nodes.title: 1. Agency Foreclosure
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes liquidation options available to the Agency when a loan is in default, focusing on how the Agency may recover the most value from the security property. It explains:

- **Deed in lieu of foreclosure**: After acceleration, a borrower may voluntarily convey the property to the Agency, but the Agency will accept only if it expects a better net recovery than through foreclosure. This decision requires supervisory approval, and the borrower may still remain liable for any remaining debt unless debt settlement procedures are followed.

- **Borrowers in bankruptcy**: If a Bankruptcy Court offers title to the Agency, acceptance must be approved by a supervisor and the Office of General Counsel. The Agency generally accepts if it can obtain clear title and substantially recover its debt, and it may pay approved court-related fees.

- **Foreclosure**: The section explains that once liquidation is appropriate, the loan is accelerated and the borrower may still try to liquidate voluntarily before foreclosure occurs.

- **Foreclosure by another lien holder**: If another lien holder initiates foreclosure, the Servicing Office should choose the action that maximizes government recovery, such as joining the action, bidding at sale, or settling for assignment of the note and mortgage. If the Government has redemption rights and did not protect its interest, a supervisor decides whether to redeem based on estimated net recovery value.

nodes.title: 2. Foreclosure by Another Lien Holder
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation options and how the Agency decides whether to accept different forms of property recovery. It covers deed in lieu of foreclosure, conveyance from borrowers in bankruptcy, standard foreclosure actions, foreclosure by another lien holder, and when a valueless lien may be released. It also introduces net recovery value, defining estimated versus actual recovery, and explains that market value is the starting point for calculations. Finally, it notes that environmental hazards and responsibilities must be considered when estimating market value and net recovery value.

nodes.title: C. Release of a Valueless Lien
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation procedures, focusing on when a valueless lien may be released and how that decision is handled by the Field Office and Servicing Office. It then defines net recovery value as the expected amount the Agency could recover from a property after liquidation costs, and distinguishes between estimated and actual net recovery value. The section also describes how to establish market value as the basis for the calculation and notes that environmental hazards and environmental responsibilities must be considered when estimating value.

nodes.title: NET RECOVERY VALUE
nodes.summary: The partial document explains liquidation options, focusing on when a valueless lien may be released if the Agency’s net recovery analysis shows no expected recovery. It notes that this usually happens at the Field Office level and triggers notifications so taxes and insurance stop being paid by the Agency and a debt settlement package is sent to the borrower. The document then defines net recovery value as the estimated amount the Agency could recover from a property after all liquidation, holding, and sale costs, and distinguishes it from actual net recovery value. It also describes how market value is the basis for calculating net recovery value, noting that it may come from an estimate, appraisal, or actual sale price depending on the stage of liquidation. Finally, it emphasizes environmental considerations that may affect market value and liability, and shows the basic calculation structure: market value minus costs plus income equals net recovery value.

nodes.title: A. Establishing Market Value
nodes.summary: The passage explains liquidation options in USDA servicing, focusing on when a valueless lien may be released and how the Field Office and Servicing Office coordinate notifications and debt settlement. It also defines estimated versus actual net recovery value, describes how market value is used as the basis for net recovery calculations, and notes that environmental hazards and responsibilities must be considered when estimating market value and recovery.

nodes.title: B. Environmental Considerations
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Agency handles liquidation cases by estimating net recovery value and deciding whether a lien should be released if it appears valueless. It defines net recovery value as the expected amount recoverable after liquidation costs, explains that market value is the basis for the calculation, and notes that market value may come from estimates, appraisals, or actual sale prices. The section also emphasizes environmental due diligence, including checking for hazardous substances, wetlands, floodplains, historic properties, and other sensitive resources before acquiring property, with special coordination requirements for tribal lands and Section 106 review. Finally, it describes when appraisals should be ordered, especially when a lien may be valueless or when a borrower offers a deed in lieu of foreclosure.

nodes.title: C. Ordering An Appraisal
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers net recovery value procedures and related appraisal guidance in loan servicing. It explains that projects on Tribal land or dependent Indian communities may require coordination with the Tribe for environmental and cultural resource review, including Section 106 consultation and the role of the SHPO or THPO. It then discusses when to order an appraisal, noting that appraisal information is in another chapter, and gives two cases where an appraisal should be obtained immediately: when a lien appears valueless, and when a borrower offers a deed in lieu of foreclosure so the government can decide whether accepting it is in its interest.

nodes.title: 1. Valueless Lien
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses several related servicing and appraisal topics. It first explains that development on Tribal lands or in dependent Indian communities may require additional coordination with the Tribe, especially to assess effects on the human environment, cultural resources, and the Section 106 review process under NHPA and related RD instructions. It describes how the State Historic Preservation Officer (SHPO) and Tribal Historic Preservation Officer (THPO) participate in Section 106 review depending on whether the tribe has designated a THPO.

The document then begins a section on ordering appraisals, referring readers to HB-1-3550 for general appraisal procedures. It outlines when a formal appraisal should be obtained, including two key situations: when the servicer’s estimate suggests a lien may be valueless, and when a borrower offers a deed in lieu of foreclosure after acceleration. In both cases, the appraisal is used to determine whether the government should release the lien or accept the deed in lieu.

nodes.title: 2. Deed in Lieu of Foreclosure
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses how to determine net recovery value in property liquidation situations, with special attention to Tribal land and Section 106 review requirements. It explains when additional coordination with tribes, SHPO, or a THPO is needed for appraisal and environmental/cultural resource review. The document then outlines when appraisals should be ordered for different cases: immediately if a lien may be valueless, immediately after a borrower offers a deed in lieu of foreclosure, and shortly before a foreclosure sale. It also describes how to estimate the holding period based on the expected disposition method and notes that marketing/disposition time should be included. Finally, it begins listing deductions from market value used in the net recovery calculation, such as prior liens and junior liens that may need to be addressed by the Agency.

nodes.title: 3. Foreclosure
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to determine net recovery value in foreclosure-related situations. It covers:

- When to obtain an appraisal if a property is heading to foreclosure, emphasizing that it should be done shortly before the sale because property value may change over time.
- How to estimate the holding period, depending on the method of disposition such as foreclosure, deed-in-lieu, release of a valueless lien, or debt settlement after acceleration.
- The importance of including marketing and disposition time in the holding period estimate.
- Deductions from market value that must be considered in liquidation, especially prior liens and junior liens, including when the Agency may need to pay them and the need for a title search to identify liens and amounts.

nodes.title: D. Holding Period
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how to estimate net recovery value in foreclosure-related situations. It emphasizes that appraisals should be timed close to a foreclosure sale because property value can change during a long foreclosure process. It then discusses how to estimate the holding period based on the expected disposition method, such as foreclosure, deed-in-lieu, release of lien, or debt settlement, and notes that marketing and disposition time should be included when relevant. Finally, it outlines deductions from market value that affect net recovery value, including prior liens and junior liens that may need to be paid by the Agency, and stresses the importance of a title search to identify liens and their amounts.

nodes.title: E. Deductions from Market Value
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how to calculate Net Recovery Value under HB-2-3550, focusing on property liquidation scenarios such as foreclosure, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, release of a valueless lien, and debt settlement after acceleration. It emphasizes estimating the holding period based on the expected disposition method and the time needed for marketing and sale.

It then outlines the main deductions from market value that must be included, such as prior liens, junior liens, selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation, administrative costs, and management costs. Finally, it identifies possible additions to market value, including appreciation during the holding period and limited rental income from REO properties under special circumstances.

nodes.title: F. Additions to Market Value
nodes.summary: The document section explains how to calculate net recovery value by accounting for costs and value changes related to selling and holding a property. It covers selling expenses paid by the Agency, holding costs such as accrued interest, depreciation during the holding period, administrative costs associated with liquidation, and management costs for maintaining the property. It also notes possible additions to market value, including appreciation during the holding period and limited income from leasing REO properties in certain situations.

nodes.title: BASIC SECURITY LOSS
nodes.summary: The document explains the concept of basic security loss, defined as the difference between a property’s market value and the outstanding Agency debt, including principal, subsidy subject to recapture, and recoverable costs. It notes that this measure helps Servicers decide how to work with borrowers and can inform more flexible debt settlement when property value declines were beyond the borrower’s control, as well as support future portfolio analysis and loan origination decisions. The document also covers deed in lieu of foreclosure, describing it as a liquidation method where the borrower conveys title to the Agency. It outlines when this option may be considered, emphasizing that it is generally only after acceleration of the account and when it is in the Government’s best interest based on estimated net recovery value. It lists the required documents for processing such a conveyance, including a warranty deed, Form RD 1955-1, and copies of leases, junior liens, or other title-related documents, and explains that the borrower should be told the deed will not be recorded unless accepted. It further states that liens and real estate taxes or assessments usually must be satisfied before acceptance, though the Agency may choose to settle them if that better serves the Government’s interests.

nodes.title: DEED IN LIEU OF FORECLOSURE
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains key Agency procedures for handling distressed loans and security property. It first defines “basic security loss” as the difference between a property’s market value and the outstanding Agency debt, including principal, subsidy subject to recapture, and recoverable costs, and notes that this measure helps guide borrower settlement decisions and portfolio analysis.

It then covers “deed in lieu of foreclosure,” describing it as a liquidation method where the borrower conveys title to the Agency. The section explains when such an offer may be considered, what documents are required, how it is processed, and how liens, taxes, and assessments may be handled. It also emphasizes that a deed in lieu does not automatically release the borrower from liability, that the account is credited with the actual net recovery value, and that any remaining debt must still be settled.

Finally, the document begins the foreclosure section, noting that state laws affect foreclosure procedures and that OGC consultation may be required. It outlines the servicing office’s role in deciding whether to accelerate an account and begin foreclosure, and highlights circumstances requiring OGC concurrence, including nonmonetary defaults and certain military service-related situations.

nodes.title: FORECLOSURE
nodes.summary:
The document covers USDA Rural Development loan servicing procedures for deed in lieu of foreclosure and foreclosure. It explains that a deed in lieu does not automatically eliminate borrower liability, that the account is credited only with the actual net recovery value, and that any remaining debt must be handled through debt settlement procedures. It also lists borrower advisories, such as foreclosure continuing during consideration of the offer, conveyance costs being added to the debt, and the need for title insurance or a final title opinion.

The foreclosure section describes how state law affects acceleration and foreclosure actions, and emphasizes consulting OGC to ensure proper procedures. It outlines the Servicing Office’s responsibility to decide whether to accelerate an account and begin foreclosure, noting that this decision depends on case-specific factors. It also identifies situations requiring OGC concurrence before foreclosure, including nonmonetary defaults and certain military service-related cases.

nodes.title: A. Making the Acceleration Decision
nodes.summary:
The document section explains USDA loan servicing procedures for deed in lieu of foreclosure and the start of foreclosure action. It notes that a deed in lieu does not automatically eliminate the borrower’s remaining debt; the account is credited with the actual net recovery value, and any unpaid balance must be handled through debt settlement procedures. It also lists borrower reminders, including that foreclosure continues while the deed in lieu offer is reviewed, Agency-paid conveyance costs are added to the debt, and the borrower may need to provide title insurance or a title opinion.

The foreclosure section describes how state law affects acceleration and foreclosure procedures and emphasizes consulting OGC to ensure proper steps are followed. It explains that the Servicing Office must decide whether to accelerate the loan and begin foreclosure based on case-specific factors, and that OGC concurrence is required in certain situations, such as nonmonetary defaults or when Servicemembers Civil Relief Act protections may apply.

nodes.title: 1. OGC Concurrence
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing foreclosure-related procedures. It first covers deed in lieu of foreclosure, emphasizing that it does not automatically cancel borrower debt, that the account receives credit for the actual net recovery value, and that any remaining debt must be handled through debt settlement. It also lists borrower advisements, such as foreclosure not stopping during review, conveyance costs being added to the debt, and the need for title insurance or a title opinion.

It then moves into the foreclosure process itself, noting that state law and OGC guidance affect required procedures. The document discusses how the Servicing Office decides whether to accelerate a loan, including situations requiring OGC concurrence, such as nonmonetary defaults, certain military service cases, or when the property secures another USDA loan. It also addresses special issues for tribal land and the role of other lien holders in foreclosure actions.

Finally, it describes the acceleration notice: what it demands, what information it must include, the borrower’s 30-day response period, and the required method of delivery to borrowers, cosigners, and sometimes the property address.

nodes.title: 2. Tribal Land
nodes.summary: The partial document covers foreclosure procedures under USDA Rural Housing regulations, focusing on when foreclosure/liquidation may be affected by other USDA loans, tribal land requirements, and the role of other lien holders. It then explains the acceleration process, including when an acceleration notice is issued, what the notice must contain, who must receive it, and the borrower’s 30-day period to pay in full or request a hearing.

nodes.title: 3. Role of Other Lien Holders
nodes.summary: This excerpt from HB-2-3550, Paragraph 6.5 on foreclosure, explains how USDA loan servicing handles liquidation through foreclosure in special situations and the start of the acceleration process. It covers how foreclosure may affect other USDA loans, procedures for properties on tribal allotted or trust land, and the involvement of other lien holders in foreclosure actions. It then describes the acceleration notice: its purpose, the information it must include, the borrower’s 30-day response period, and the required methods of delivery to borrowers, cosigners, and property addresses.

nodes.title: B. Acceleration
nodes.summary: The partial document covers foreclosure procedures under USDA Rural Housing servicing, focusing on conditions affecting liquidation and the acceleration process. It explains special rules when the property secures another USDA loan, when the property is on tribal allotted or trust land, and how other lien holders may participate in foreclosure. It also describes the acceleration notice: what it demands, what information it must include for the borrower, the 30-day response period, and the required methods and recipients for mailing the notice.

nodes.title: 1. The Acceleration Notice
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines USDA foreclosure procedures under HB-2-3550, focusing on when foreclosure is allowed and how the acceleration process works. It discusses special cases such as tribal allotted or trust land, where liquidation is delayed until an offer to transfer the account to an eligible tribal party is made, and the role of other lien holders in joining foreclosure actions. It explains the acceleration notice, including what it must tell the borrower, how it must be delivered, and the borrower’s 30-day response window. The text also covers the treatment of payment subsidies after acceleration, loss of eligibility for special servicing, assignment of promissory notes and security instruments, and conditions under which borrowers may cure the default and avoid foreclosure, including possible waivers of the two-year limitation.

nodes.title: 2. Treatment of Payment Subsidy
nodes.summary: The partial document covers foreclosure procedures under HB-2-3550, focusing on what happens after a borrower’s loan is accelerated. It explains that payment subsidy agreements are not canceled immediately but will not be renewed unless the account is reinstated, and that borrowers lose eligibility for special servicing actions once acceleration occurs. It also describes when promissory notes and security instruments may be assigned for foreclosure, and how offers to cure default are handled. The text outlines the conditions under which a borrower may reinstate the loan, including state law requirements, NAD decisions, or a two-year limit on prior cures, and notes that a waiver of this two-year limit may be granted if it is in the government’s best interest, supported by financial documentation showing repayment ability.

nodes.title: 3. Special Servicing
nodes.summary: The partial document covers foreclosure procedures under HB-2-3550, specifically how a borrower’s account is handled after acceleration of the debt. It explains: the treatment of payment subsidy agreements after acceleration; loss of eligibility for special servicing actions once the account is accelerated; conditions under which promissory notes and security instruments may be assigned for foreclosure; and how offers to cure the default are handled. It also describes when a borrower may be allowed to reinstate the loan, including state law requirements, NAD decisions, or a two-year cure rule, and notes that waivers of the two-year limitation require a financial review showing repayment ability.

nodes.title: 4. Assignment of Security Instruments
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses foreclosure procedures under HB-2-3550, focusing on what happens after a borrower’s account is accelerated. It explains how payment subsidy agreements are handled, limits on special servicing after acceleration, and the conditions under which promissory notes and security instruments may be assigned for foreclosure. It also outlines how offers to cure a default are processed, including when borrowers may reinstate the loan, the two-year limitation on curing defaults to avoid foreclosure, possible waivers of that limitation, and the financial documentation required to support such a waiver.

nodes.title: 5. Offers to Pay
nodes.summary: The partial document explains key foreclosure servicing procedures after a borrower’s loan has been accelerated. It covers how payment subsidies are handled, when special servicing ends, and the conditions for assigning promissory notes and security instruments for foreclosure. It also details how offers to cure default are processed, including eligibility to reinstate the loan, possible waivers of the two-year limitation, required financial documentation, escrow and repayment conditions, and procedures for accepting less than the full amount due. In addition, it outlines borrower review and appeal options after acceleration, and explains when the Agency will proceed with legal foreclosure actions and seek deficiency judgments, including the circumstances in which deficiency judgments will not be pursued.

nodes.title: C. Review of the Acceleration Decision
nodes.summary: The passage covers foreclosure and related servicing procedures for Rural Development loans, including rules for accepting partial cures of default, requirements for cure and financial statements, and reamortization if delinquency cannot be paid within 30 days. It also outlines how borrowers can challenge an acceleration decision through informal review, mediation, dispute resolution, or formal appeal, and explains when foreclosure proceedings should begin. In addition, it discusses state-specific legal guidance, and the conditions under which the Agency may or may not seek a deficiency judgment during foreclosure.

nodes.title: D. Initiating Legal Proceedings
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures related to foreclosure under HB-2-3550, including how borrowers may cure a default, request waiver of certain limits, and have funds submitted. It explains borrower review options after acceleration, such as informal review, mediation, dispute resolution, and formal appeal. It also outlines when legal foreclosure proceedings begin, when deficiency judgments may or may not be sought, and encourages voluntary liquidation during the legal process. Finally, it describes the foreclosure sale process, how the Agency determines its bid amount, how bidding is handled locally, what happens if RHS is the highest bidder, and how debt settlement is pursued if sale proceeds do not cover the full debt.

nodes.title: E. The Foreclosure Sale
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the foreclosure process under HB-2-3550, focusing on what happens after legal proceedings end. It says the Servicer should encourage voluntary liquidation during the legal process and may use debt settlement options. Once foreclosure proceeds to sale, the Field Office must decide how much to bid, with the Agency’s bid set as the lesser of its gross investment or the estimated net recovery value of the property. It defines gross investment as the unpaid balance, outstanding advances and fees, and total subsidy received. The document also notes that bidding procedures vary by locality and that if the Agency wins the bid, the property becomes REO and is managed by the Field Office. If the sale does not cover the borrower’s debt, debt settlement procedures are followed. It also includes a note about RHS requirements to lease or sell certain inventory properties to public agencies and nonprofits for transitional and turnkey housing.

nodes.title: 1. Establishing a Bid Amount
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the foreclosure stage in the loan servicing process. It states that while legal proceedings are pending, the Servicer should encourage voluntary liquidation and may consider debt settlement options. Once foreclosure proceedings are complete, the property is sold at a foreclosure sale, and Field Offices must determine and place the Agency’s bid. The bid amount is based on the lesser of the Agency’s gross investment or the estimated net recovery value of the property. Gross investment includes the unpaid balance, outstanding advances and fees, and total subsidy received, with different definitions of unpaid balance depending on whether the state uses judicial or nonjudicial foreclosure. The document also describes bidding procedures, noting that they vary by locality, that the Agency may become the owner of REO property if it is the highest bidder, and that if sale proceeds do not cover the debt, debt settlement procedures are followed. It also includes a note about RHS requirements to lease or sell inventory properties to public agencies and nonprofits for transitional and turnkey housing purposes.

nodes.title: 2. Bidding at the Foreclosure Sale
nodes.summary: The partial document explains foreclosure procedures and how the Agency determines a bid at a foreclosure sale. It states that while legal proceedings are underway, the Servicer should encourage the borrower to voluntarily liquidate and may consider debt settlement options. Once foreclosure proceedings conclude, the property is sold, and Field Offices must establish a bid based on the lesser of the Agency’s gross investment or the estimated net recovery value of the property. It defines gross investment as the unpaid balance, outstanding advances and fees, and total subsidy received. It also explains that if the Agency is the highest bidder, the property becomes REO property to be managed and disposed of by the Field Office, and if sale proceeds do not cover the debt, the Servicing Office follows debt settlement procedures. The document also includes a Net Recovery Value Worksheet, outlining how to calculate net recovery value by starting with market value, subtracting various deductions such as liens, expenses, holding and administrative costs, and adding expected appreciation and income during the holding period.

nodes.title: ATTACHMENT 6-A
nodes.summary: This document is a worksheet for calculating the Net Recovery Value of a property or loan-related asset. It begins with background fields for case number, borrower name/ID, proposed liquidation option, calculation date, and estimated holding period. The main section outlines how to compute net recovery value by starting with the current market value and then subtracting various deductions such as prior liens, junior liens, selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation, administrative costs, and management costs. It also includes possible additions to value, such as appreciation and income during the holding period. The final result is the net recovery value, calculated as market value minus total reductions plus total additions. A note explains how to determine the holding period for different liquidation scenarios, including lien release, refinancing, sales, deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, and debt settlement.

nodes.title: NET RECOVERY VALUE WORKSHEET
nodes.summary:
This partial document is a USDA/Farmers Home Administration worksheet titled “Net Recovery Value Worksheet” (Attachment 6-A, HB-2-3550). It provides a structured form for calculating the expected net recovery value of a property or loan asset in a liquidation scenario.

Main points covered:
- Background information fields for case number, borrower name/ID, proposed liquidation option, calculation date, and estimated holding period.
- A calculation section that starts with current market value from an appraisal.
- Deductions from market value, including prior liens, junior liens, selling expenses, holding costs, depreciation during the holding period, administrative costs, and management costs.
- Additions to present market value, including appreciation and income during the holding period.
- A final formula to compute net recovery value by subtracting total reductions and adding total additions.
- Notes explaining how to determine the holding period for different liquidation options such as release of valueless lien, refinancing, sale, deed-in-lieu, debt settlement, foreclosure, and acquired-property disposition.
- Definitions of expense categories, including what counts as selling, holding, administrative, and management costs.

structure.title: CHAPTER 7: SPECIAL COLLECTIONS
structure.summary: The partial document appears to be an instruction rather than substantive content. It mainly states that the response should directly provide a description of the partial document’s main points, without including any additional text.

nodes.title: INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary: The partial document introduces Chapter 7 on Special Collections, explaining that the Agency may need to recover unauthorized assistance, collect debts through administrative, salary, and IRS offsets, and settle debts when other collection efforts fail. It emphasizes that unauthorized assistance must generally be repaid even if caused by Agency error, and outlines the chapter’s three sections: identifying and correcting unauthorized assistance, obtaining offsets from federal payments, and debt settlement. The excerpt begins Section 1, which distinguishes unauthorized assistance caused by inaccurate information or error from cases involving false information, and notes that the statute of limitations for collecting assistance due to inaccurate information is six years.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF UNAUTHORIZED ASSISTANCE
nodes.summary:
The partial document is from Chapter 7, “Special Collections,” and explains how the Agency handles debt recovery when borrowers owe money due to unauthorized assistance or other debts. It introduces three main collection areas: recovering unauthorized assistance, collecting debts through administrative, salary, and IRS offsets, and settling debts when other collection efforts fail. The chapter emphasizes that borrowers are generally responsible for repaying unauthorized assistance even if the error was caused by Agency staff, though the Agency may help correct such cases.

The section on unauthorized assistance defines when assistance is considered unauthorized and distinguishes between “inaccurate information” and “false information,” noting different statute of limitations periods for collection: 6 years for inaccurate information and 10 years for false information. It also explains that false information includes knowingly incorrect information or information provided to obtain benefits the borrower was not eligible for, and that borrowers may be accountable for false information supplied by third parties if they knew or should have known it was incorrect.

The document then describes three types of unauthorized assistance: unauthorized loans, unauthorized subsidies, and related collection rules. Unauthorized loans include loans made to unqualified borrowers, loans written at the wrong interest rate, or loans made for ineligible purposes. Unauthorized subsidy applies when a borrower receives subsidy they were not eligible for, with collection pursued only if the amount is $550 or more, in which case the full amount must be repaid. It also discusses a special exception involving same rates and terms assumptions: if an eligible occupant is in the property and signs a same rates and terms assumption within 90 days after notice, collection of the unauthorized assistance may not be pursued.

nodes.title: A. Three Types of Unauthorized Assistance
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for “Unauthorized Assistance” under HB-2-3550, focusing on how false information is defined, when borrowers are held responsible for incorrect information, and the 10-year statute of limitations for collecting assistance obtained through false information. It then outlines three types of unauthorized assistance, with emphasis on unauthorized loans and unauthorized subsidies. For unauthorized subsidies, it states that collection is pursued only when the amount is $550 or more, and the borrower must repay the full subsidy received. It also describes an exception involving eligible same rates and terms assumptions: if a qualifying individual is already occupying the property and receiving subsidy, unauthorized assistance is not pursued unless they fail to execute the assumption within 90 days after written notice and field office follow-up.

nodes.title: 1. Unauthorized Loan
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the policy for unauthorized assistance under HB-2-3550, focusing on how false information is defined and handled, including borrower responsibility, third-party misinformation, and a 10-year statute of limitations for collection. It then outlines three types of unauthorized assistance: unauthorized loans, unauthorized subsidies, and related collection rules. The text also explains when unauthorized subsidy collection is pursued, including a $550 threshold, and describes an exception for eligible occupants who complete a same rates and terms assumption within 90 days after notification.

nodes.title: 2. Unauthorized Subsidy
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing Service rules on unauthorized assistance under HB-2-3550. It defines false and inaccurate information, notes borrower responsibility for misleading information from third parties, and states the statute of limitations for collecting assistance caused by false information is 10 years. It then outlines three types of unauthorized assistance: unauthorized loans, unauthorized subsidies, and unauthorized grants, including when each occurs and when collection will or will not be pursued. The document also describes borrower consequences, such as repayment requirements, appeal-related deadlines, repayment of subsidies in lump sum, and corrective actions for loans based on inaccurate information, including refinancing, conversion of loan type, or full repayment for ineligible borrowers.

nodes.title: 3. Unauthorized Grant
nodes.summary: This section explains unauthorized assistance under HB-2-3550, focusing on grants and the consequences for borrowers. It defines unauthorized grants as those made to unqualified borrowers or for ineligible purposes. It then distinguishes between assistance based on false information and assistance based on inaccurate information. Borrowers who received assistance through false information generally must repay the loan and any subsidy within 30 days after appeal rights expire, with unauthorized subsidies repaid in a lump sum. Borrowers who received assistance due to inaccurate information must also correct the issue within 30 days, but the Agency may allow loan adjustments or repayment flexibility. The section includes specific rules for over-income borrowers and ineligible borrowers, including refinancing, conversion to other loan types, or full repayment.

nodes.title: B. Consequences for borrowers
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the rules for unauthorized assistance under HB-2-3550, focusing on grants and loans made to ineligible borrowers or for ineligible purposes. It outlines borrower consequences when assistance was obtained through false or inaccurate information, including repayment requirements, deadlines after appeal rights expire, and whether loans or subsidies may be continued or must be repaid. It also describes specific remedies for over-income borrowers and ineligible borrowers, such as refinancing, converting loans to nonprogram or Section 502 loans, or requiring repayment within 30 days.

nodes.title: 1. Assistance Based on False Information
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains the rules for unauthorized assistance under HB-2-3550, focusing on unauthorized grants and the borrower consequences when assistance is based on false or inaccurate information. It states that grants to unqualified borrowers or for ineligible purposes are unauthorized and must be corrected or repaid. Borrowers who received assistance through false information must repay the loan and any subsidy within 30 days after appeal rights expire, with unauthorized subsidies repaid in a lump sum. For cases involving inaccurate information, the Agency may allow loan adjustments or repayment assistance, but borrowers above income limits may need to refinance, convert the loan type, or repay if they were not eligible.

nodes.title: 2. Assistance Based on Inaccurate Information
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing rules for unauthorized assistance under HB-2-3550, focusing on loans and grants made to ineligible borrowers or for improper purposes. It distinguishes between assistance based on false information and inaccurate information, and outlines borrower obligations such as repaying loans, repaying unauthorized subsidy in a lump sum, refinancing or converting loans when income eligibility was incorrect, and correcting wrong interest rates. It also covers unauthorized Section 504 grants, including options to repay in full or convert the grant to a loan, and describes the Servicing Office’s role in evaluating repayment ability, preparing promissory notes, or pursuing judgment if repayment is refused.

nodes.title: 3. Unauthorized Grants
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA Rural Housing rules for handling unauthorized assistance in Section 504 loans and grants. It covers situations such as loans approved for unauthorized purposes, incorrect borrower income calculations, loans made at the wrong interest rate, and unauthorized subsidy, explaining when the loan can continue, when account adjustments are required, and when borrowers must repay unauthorized amounts. It also describes procedures for unauthorized Section 504 grants, including the 30-day correction requirement after appeal rights expire, repayment in a lump sum or conversion to a promissory note, and actions the Servicing and Asset Management Office should take if the borrower cannot or will not repay.

nodes.title: IDENTIFYING AND INVESTIGATING CASES
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Agency identifies and investigates cases of unauthorized assistance, including sources such as file reviews, referrals, and OIG audits. It outlines when the Servicing Office handles an investigation versus when cases must be referred to the Office of Inspector General, especially if false information may be involved. The text also covers determining borrower awareness of false third-party information, the treatment of cases where the borrower was unaware, reporting requirements for OIG audit cases, and when cases remain open. Finally, it introduces suspension or debarment recommendations when false information is submitted by an applicant, noting that the National Office reviews the recommendation and an Administrative Law Judge makes the final decision.

nodes.title: NOTIFICATION AND APPEALS AND ADMINISTRATIVE COST
nodes.summary: The partial document only contains an instruction asking for a direct description of the document’s main points, without providing any actual substantive content to summarize.

nodes.title: A. Notify Borrower
nodes.summary: The document section explains the procedures for handling unauthorized assistance in a loan servicing context. It covers how the Servicing Office must notify the borrower, explain the reasons and repayment amount, and allow time for consultation or dispute. It also describes the borrower consultation process, possible extension for gathering documentation, and the final notice of determination including appeal rights and recovery actions. In addition, it states that borrowers who are contractually obligated may be charged a $300 administrative cost plus third-party costs if they do not repay or enter an approved repayment plan in time. The section then begins guidance on determining the amount due, starting with identifying the correct interest rate if the loan was made at the wrong rate.

nodes.title: B. Borrower Consultation
nodes.summary: This section explains the procedures for notifying a borrower when unauthorized assistance has been received, including the reasons for the determination, the repayment amount, and the borrower’s 30-day period to consult or dispute the claim. It describes the consultation process, possible extensions for gathering documentation, and the final notice of determination with appeal rights and recovery actions. It also covers administrative costs that may be charged if repayment or an approved repayment plan is not made in time. Finally, it begins the process for determining the amount due by stating that if the loan was made at the wrong interest rate, the servicing office must identify the correct rate that should have been used.

nodes.title: C. Notice of Determination
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the procedures for handling unauthorized assistance under HB-2-3550, including how the Servicing Office must notify the borrower, allow time for consultation or dispute, issue a final notice with appeal rights, and assess administrative costs if repayment is not made. It also begins the section on determining the amount due, specifically noting that if a loan was made at the wrong interest rate, the Servicing Office must identify the correct rate that should have been used.

nodes.title: D. Administrative Cost
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the procedures for handling unauthorized assistance in a loan servicing context. It covers how the Servicing Office must notify the borrower, including the reasons the assistance is considered unauthorized and the amount to be repaid. It describes the borrower consultation process, including the right to review the claim, provide responses, request extra time for documentation, and receive documented extensions. It also outlines the final notice of determination, which includes the repayment details, mailing instructions, servicing actions, and appeal rights, and states that recovery actions cannot begin until appeal rights are exhausted. In addition, it addresses administrative costs, noting that certain borrowers may be charged a $300 fee plus third-party costs if they do not repay or arrange an approved repayment plan. The next section begins the process for determining the amount due, starting with identifying the correct interest rate if the loan was made at the wrong rate.

nodes.title: DETERMINING THE AMOUNT DUE
nodes.summary: The document section explains the procedure for handling unauthorized assistance in a loan servicing context. It covers how the Servicing Office must notify the borrower, including the reasons the assistance was considered unauthorized and the amount owed. It describes the borrower consultation period, the possibility of an extension to gather documentation, and the final notice of determination with appeal rights and repayment instructions. It also states that recovery actions cannot begin until the appeal period has passed and any appeal is resolved. In addition, it outlines a $300 administrative cost plus third-party costs for borrowers who do not repay or enter an approved repayment plan on time. The next section begins the process for determining the amount due, starting with identifying the correct interest rate if the loan was made at the wrong rate.

nodes.title: A. Determine Correct Interest Rate
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for handling unauthorized assistance in a loan servicing context. It covers how the Servicing Office must notify borrowers, allow a 30-day consultation and appeal period, and issue a final determination with repayment instructions and appeal rights. It also describes administrative costs that may be charged if repayment is not made or an approved repayment plan is not arranged.  

The document then outlines how to determine the amount due, including identifying the correct interest rate based on borrower eligibility, calculating the correct subsidy amount, and using reverse-and-reapply procedures to retroactively adjust accounts. It notes that these adjustments can create delinquency and describes possible consequences, such as lump-sum repayment or loan acceleration, especially in cases involving false or inaccurate information.

nodes.title: B. Determine Correct Subsidy Amount
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how the Servicing Office determines the amount due when unauthorized assistance or incorrect loan servicing has occurred. It covers which interest rate should be used based on borrower eligibility at the time of loan approval or closing, including differences for Section 502 and Section 504 loans or grants. It also describes how to determine the correct subsidy amount, how to reverse and reapply payments to calculate unauthorized assistance, and how borrower accounts are adjusted retroactively. Finally, it outlines the consequences if the account adjustment creates a delinquency, including repayment in a lump sum or loan acceleration, depending on whether the issue involved false or inaccurate information.

nodes.title: C. Reverse and Reapply Procedures
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how the Agency determines the amount due when a borrower has received unauthorized assistance, including which interest rate to apply based on whether the borrower was income-eligible at loan approval or closing. It distinguishes between Section 502 and Section 504 loans or grants, and describes how subsidy amounts are calculated based on eligibility at the time the unauthorized assistance began.

It also covers “reverse and reapply” procedures, where borrower accounts are adjusted retroactively to identify the amount of unauthorized assistance by recalculating payments at the correct rate. This may create a delinquency that must be repaid, either through a lump sum or by reamortizing the loan in some cases.

The document further outlines servicing actions and consequences depending on whether the unauthorized assistance resulted from false information or inaccurate information. False information generally leads to no assistance and possible loan acceleration if repayment is not made, while inaccurate information may allow reamortization if the borrower cannot repay in a lump sum. An example exhibit illustrates how incorrect and correct payment amounts are applied to a borrower’s account.

nodes.title: SERVICING ACTIONS
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains a servicing example showing how loan payments are recalculated and reapplied when an interest-rate error is discovered. It illustrates the “reverse and reapply” process with Mr. Smith’s account, comparing incorrect and correct monthly payments and showing the resulting past-due amount after adjustments.

It also covers Section 7.6, “Servicing Actions,” focusing on assistance to borrowers who received unauthorized assistance based on false or inaccurate information. The document states that the Agency generally provides no assistance in cases involving false information, and if the borrower does not repay the required amount or authorize reamortization within the allowed time, the loan may be accelerated. If the borrower cannot repay in a lump sum and the case involves inaccurate information, the Agency may assist by reamortizing the loan to include the delinquent amount.

nodes.title: A. Assistance to Borrowers
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers procedures for handling unauthorized assistance or subsidy errors in loan accounts, especially when incorrect information or interest rates were used. It gives an example of the “reverse and reapply” method to show how payments are recalculated at the correct rate and how past-due balances can result from the adjustment.

It then outlines servicing actions, including:
- how the Agency handles borrowers who received unauthorized assistance,
- when loans must be accelerated if required payments or documents are not provided,
- how to process changes in interest rate, subsidy, and reamortization,
- the role of required forms such as RD 1940-16, RD 3550-21, and RD 3550-18,
- and what to do if a borrower’s account has already been satisfied but unauthorized subsidy is later discovered.

Overall, the section explains the correction, repayment, and enforcement steps the Servicing Office must follow when account errors or unauthorized assistance are identified.

nodes.title: B. Processing Changes to the Account
nodes.summary: This section explains servicing actions for changes to a borrower’s account, including how to handle interest rate changes, subsidy corrections, and reamortization when unauthorized assistance has been received. It also describes procedures for reopening satisfied accounts if unauthorized subsidy is discovered later, including borrower notification, repayment arrangements, document updates, and possible consultation with the Office of General Counsel.

nodes.title: 1. Change in Interest Rate
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines servicing actions for handling changes to a borrower’s account in a rural housing loan program. It covers procedures for changing the interest rate, including amending Form RD 1940-16, obtaining borrower initials, and consulting OGC if required documents are not signed within 30 days. It also explains how to handle incorrect subsidy agreements by reverifying income, issuing Form RD 3550-21, and converting unpaid unauthorized assistance into a fee on the account. Another section describes reamortizing a loan when unauthorized assistance cannot be repaid, using Form RD 3550-18 and requiring escrow if taxes, insurance, or coverage are delinquent. Finally, it addresses reopening satisfied accounts when unauthorized subsidy is discovered after payoff, including contacting the former borrower, requesting repayment, and preparing a new repayment agreement at the original interest rate, with possible mortgage/deed of trust changes advised by OGC.

nodes.title: 2. Change in Subsidy
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines servicing actions for loan account changes and unauthorized assistance issues. It explains how to process changes in interest rate, including amending Form RD 1940-16, borrower initials, possible mortgage/deed of trust changes, and acceleration if required documents are not signed within 30 days. It covers correcting subsidy errors by reverifying income, using Form RD 3550-21, and charging unauthorized assistance as a fee if repayment information is not provided. It also describes reamortization of loans when unauthorized assistance cannot be repaid, including use of Form RD 3550-18, acceleration if not signed, and escrow requirements. Finally, it addresses reactivating satisfied accounts when unauthorized subsidy is discovered after payoff, including reopening the account, contacting the former borrower, establishing a repayment agreement, and consulting OGC about securing repayment.

nodes.title: 3. Reamortization
nodes.summary: This partial document describes servicing actions for changes to a borrower’s account in a USDA housing loan context. It covers procedures for changing the interest rate, correcting subsidy amounts, and reamortizing a loan when unauthorized assistance has occurred. It explains required forms, borrower signatures, time limits for compliance, and when the account must be accelerated if documents are not signed in time. The document also outlines how to reactivate satisfied accounts if unauthorized subsidy is discovered later, including contacting the former borrower, seeking repayment, and consulting the Office of General Counsel about securing the debt.

nodes.title: C. Reactivating Satisfied Accounts
nodes.summary: The partial document covers servicing actions for loan accounts and the use of offsets to collect debts owed to the Agency. It explains how to process changes to an account, including changes in interest rate, subsidy, and reamortization when unauthorized assistance has occurred, along with required forms, borrower signatures, deadlines, and when acceleration or escrow requirements apply. It also describes how to reactivate satisfied accounts if unauthorized subsidy is later discovered, including contacting the former borrower, restructuring repayment, seeking judgments, or debt settling if repayment cannot be obtained. The document then introduces offset procedures, explaining that Agency debts may be collected through administrative offsets, salary offsets, and IRS tax refund offsets, and outlines the annual review of delinquent accounts to determine offset eligibility and basic conditions under which offsets may or may not be used.

nodes.title: OVERVIEW OF OFFSETS
nodes.summary: The document section explains the Agency’s use of offsets to collect debts owed by borrowers or debtors. It covers three types of offsets: administrative offsets, salary offsets, and IRS tax refund offsets, including the rules and regulations that govern each type. It notes that administrative and salary offsets can be used together, while IRS offsets are only used when the debt cannot be collected through the other methods. The section also describes the annual review of delinquent accounts, stating that accounts should be screened at least once a year to identify borrowers eligible for offset, with specific delinquency thresholds required before offsets are considered. Finally, it clarifies that offsets may apply to both current and former borrowers, though the term “borrower” is used broadly for simplicity.

nodes.title: ANNUAL REVIEW OF DELINQUENT ACCOUNTS
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the Agency’s debt collection procedures through offsets. It covers three types of offsets: administrative offset, salary offset, and IRS tax refund offset, including the rules and legal authorities that govern each. It also describes the annual review process for delinquent accounts to determine whether a borrower is eligible for offset, along with conditions that make offsets unavailable, such as bankruptcy, active military duty in certain cases, or current workout agreements.

The document further outlines how the Servicing Office should decide whether to use administrative or salary offsets, the required borrower notification, and how collected funds are applied. Administrative offsets are taken as lump-sum payments, while salary offsets generally withhold 15 percent of disposable pay each pay period, with the option for borrowers to voluntarily authorize a larger deduction.

nodes.title: SALARY AND ADMINISTRATIVE OFFSETS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the annual review process for delinquent accounts and identifies situations in which debt collection through offset should not be pursued, such as bankruptcy, active military duty, foreclosure complications, approved repayment arrangements, or an expired statute of limitations. It then outlines rules for salary and administrative offsets, including when the Servicing Office must decide whether offset is appropriate, document its decision, and notify the borrower within 15 days if offset will be used. The section also describes how offset amounts are calculated: administrative offsets are collected in a lump sum, while salary offsets generally take 15 percent of disposable pay each pay period, with provisions for changes in pay and for borrowers to voluntarily increase the amount deducted.

nodes.title: A. Amount of Offsets
nodes.summary: This passage covers the annual review of delinquent accounts and the rules for using salary and administrative offsets to collect debts. It explains situations where offset collection should not be pursued, such as bankruptcy, military active duty status, approved workouts, or expired statutes of limitations. It then describes how the Agency determines whether to use offsets, how much can be collected, and how multiple loans are handled. The section also outlines borrower notification requirements, timing rules before an offset can begin, exceptions when prior notice may be delayed, and the borrower’s rights to respond, pay the debt, request records, present evidence, and appeal.

nodes.title: B. Notifying the Borrower
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for salary and administrative offsets under HB-2-3550. It covers how borrowers must be notified using specific handbook letters, the required mailing methods and timing before an offset can take effect, and when notice may be delayed if it would hinder debt collection.
