It also describes borrower rights and responses, including how borrowers can avoid offset by paying delinquency within 30 days, request records in writing, present evidence, consult with the Servicing Office, and appeal decisions. The section also notes that requests and decisions should be documented, and that copying costs may be charged in some cases.

nodes.title: C. Borrower Response and Appeals
nodes.summary: The partial document explains the procedures for salary and administrative offsets on borrower accounts. It covers how borrowers must be notified, including required use of specific handbook letters, mailing methods, timing, and what information the notice must contain. It describes borrower rights and response options, such as paying the delinquency to avoid offset, requesting records, submitting evidence, and appealing decisions. It also outlines consultation with the Servicing Office, possible review of additional documentation, and appeals to the National Appeals Division. In addition, it discusses alternatives to offset such as delinquency workout agreements and debt settlement, and gives rules for petitioning for a hearing or administrative appeal within the required time frame.

nodes.title: 1. Consultation with the Servicing Office and NAD Appeals
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for handling salary and administrative offsets for borrowers. It covers consultation with the Servicing Office, review of offset determinations based on written evidence, possible extensions for submitting documentation, and the suspension of offset while an appeal to the National Appeals Division is pending. It also describes how delinquency workout agreements may be accepted instead of offset under certain conditions, including considerations of hardship and financial analysis. In addition, it outlines when debt settlement may be considered as an alternative, and gives instructions for petitioning for a salary offset hearing or administrative offset appeal, including the 30-day filing deadline and requirements for late petitions.

nodes.title: 2. Delinquency Workout Agreement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for handling salary and administrative offsets for borrowers. It covers how borrowers can consult with the Servicing Office, submit additional documentation, and appeal adverse decisions to the National Appeals Division, with offsets suspended during appeal. It also describes alternatives to offset such as delinquency workout agreements and debt settlement offers, including conditions under which they may be accepted or considered. Finally, it outlines the process and deadline for requesting a salary offset hearing or administrative offset appeal, including petition requirements and when late filings may be accepted.

nodes.title: 3. Debt Settlement
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for salary and administrative offsets on borrower debts. It covers how borrowers can consult the Servicing Office, request a review of offset determinations, and appeal adverse decisions to the National Appeals Division, with collection action suspended during appeal. It also describes alternatives to offset, including delinquency workout agreements and debt settlements, and states when each may be considered. Finally, it outlines the process and deadline for petitioning for a salary offset hearing or administrative offset appeal, including the need to submit a signed petition within 30 days and the conditions for accepting late filings.

nodes.title: 4. Petitioning for a Salary Offset Hearing or Administrative Offset Appeal
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules and procedures for salary and administrative offsets in debt collection cases. It covers how borrowers can consult the Servicing Office, submit documentation to challenge an offset, and appeal adverse decisions to the National Appeals Division. It also describes alternatives to offset such as delinquency workout agreements and debt settlement, including when these options may be considered and when offset action should continue. In addition, it outlines the process for requesting hearings or appeals, filing deadlines, how petitions are handled, and the timelines for decisions. Finally, it details administrative offset processing, including the certification required for paying agencies, cancellation of offset requests, account crediting after collection, and circumstances under which a repayment plan may be offered instead of offset.

nodes.title: D. Processing Offsets
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains procedures for salary and administrative offsets used to collect debts owed to the Agency. It covers the borrower’s rights during a requested salary offset, including the option for a review of file documents instead of a hearing when that is more efficient, notification requirements, and how accepted petitions stop collection. It also states that final decisions must include the case facts and any repayment schedule, and gives deadlines for issuing decisions.

The document then describes how offsets are processed, including the need to provide the paying agency with written certification of the debt, the basis and amount of the offset, the due date, and confirmation that regulations were followed. It notes that if certification is incomplete, the request is returned, and that the borrower’s account must be credited once the offset is completed.

Finally, it outlines the specific process for administrative offsets: sending the required handbook letter to the appropriate Federal agency, canceling requests when the debt is satisfied, possible refusal by an agency if the offset would interfere with a program, early notification to help with collection, and the option for a repayment plan if at least one year has passed since the original request.

nodes.title: 1. Administrative Offset
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for collecting delinquent debts through salary and administrative offsets, including borrower hearing/review rights, notice requirements, decision timelines, and how offsets are processed and credited. It describes how the Servicing Office requests administrative offsets from federal agencies and salary offsets from paying agencies, when offsets may be canceled or replaced by repayment plans, and what to do if a borrower resigns, retires, or changes employers. The document also introduces IRS tax refund offset procedures, emphasizing strict deadlines, 60-day notice requirements, and how offset amounts are determined and applied to multiple debts.

nodes.title: 2. Salary Offset
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for recovering delinquent debt through salary offset, administrative salary-related actions, and IRS tax refund offset. It explains how the Servicing Office requests a salary offset from a borrower’s paying agency, handles cancellations once the debt is paid, and takes action if a borrower resigns or retires, including requesting final salary, lump-sum leave, or retirement fund holds. It also outlines IRS offset as a common collection method, emphasizing the need to follow IRS deadlines and provide borrowers at least 60 days’ notice. Finally, it describes the amount to collect through IRS offset, prioritizing full debt recovery when possible and applying payments to multiple debts in the order they were incurred.

nodes.title: IRS OFFSET
nodes.summary: The partial document covers procedures for collecting delinquent debt through salary offsets and IRS tax refund offsets. It explains how the Servicing Office requests salary offset through the borrower’s paying agency, how to handle cases where a borrower resigns, retires, changes employers, or is otherwise no longer receiving salary payments, and how to coordinate repayment from final salary, leave payouts, retirement funds, or a new agency. It also describes IRS offset as a common collection tool, emphasizing the need to follow IRS deadlines and provide proper notice, and states that the agency should try to collect the full debt amount or, if not possible, the maximum available refund and apply payments to multiple debts in the order they were incurred.

nodes.title: A. Amount of the Offset
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for collecting delinquent borrower debts through salary offsets, IRS tax refund offsets, and refunds of overpayments. It covers how the Servicing Office requests salary offsets from a borrower’s paying agency, what happens if the borrower resigns, retires, or changes employers, and how remaining debt may be collected from final salary, retirement funds, or a new agency. It then describes IRS offset procedures, including eligibility, notice requirements, deadlines, list submission to the IRS, borrower rights to dispute the offset, and how the Servicing Office updates accounts after IRS collection. Finally, it explains that any offset amounts collected in excess of the debt must be refunded, and for administrative offsets, interest may also be included.

nodes.title: B. Processing
nodes.summary: This section explains how IRS tax refund offsets are processed for delinquent borrowers and how related refunds are handled. It covers the annual IRS schedule, the requirement to report borrower delinquencies to a credit bureau before requesting an offset, and the steps for notifying borrowers with Handbook Letters 108 and 109. It also describes the borrower’s 60-day opportunity to contest the offset or pay the debt, how the Servicing Office must revise the IRS list if needed, and the rule that final offsets cannot be canceled once submitted. Finally, it outlines that offsets received in excess of the debt must be refunded, including interest for administrative offsets under specified conditions.

nodes.title: REFUNDS
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers two main topics related to debt collection and settlement in the USDA servicing process.

First, it explains the IRS offset process for delinquent borrowers. It describes how the Servicing Office receives IRS schedules, screens borrower accounts, reports delinquent borrowers to credit bureaus, notifies borrowers using specific handbook letters, allows borrowers 60 days to dispute or pay the debt, and then submits final offset lists to the IRS. It also notes that IRS offsets cannot be canceled after final submission, that any mistaken offsets must be refunded, and that overpayments received by the Agency must be returned with interest in some cases.

Second, it introduces debt settlement procedures. It states that debt settlement is used only after other collection efforts fail, with different rules depending on whether the debt is secured or unsecured. It outlines four settlement types: compromise, adjustment, charge-off, and cancellation. It also explains the use of Form RD 3550-20 to process these settlements, when verbal compromise agreements are allowed, and that no payment should be accepted before an agreement is approved.

nodes.title: COMPROMISE AND ADJUSTMENT OF DEBT [7 CFR 3550.253]
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the USDA/RD debt settlement process, including when debt settlement may be pursued and how it affects a debtor’s account. It outlines the four settlement types: compromise, adjustment, charge-off, and cancellation, and notes the required forms and documentation procedures, including use of Form RD 3550-20 and LoanServ.

It then focuses on compromise and adjustment under 7 CFR 3550.253. A compromise allows a debtor to settle for a lump sum less than the full debt, sometimes while retaining the security property if it is in the government’s best interest. An adjustment allows a debtor to pay an initial lump sum plus periodic payments over up to five years, generally without retaining the security property. The section emphasizes that the agency must confirm the offer is the maximum the debtor can afford before approving it.

The document also describes servicing procedures after approval of an adjustment: establishing an adjustment account, sending billing statements, archiving the remaining balance, handling default by reinstating the debt, referring past-due accounts for offset, and documenting any modification to the agreement.

nodes.title: A. Compromise
nodes.summary:
This section explains the rules for compromising or adjusting a debtor’s debt when full repayment is not possible. It states that the Agency must first verify, using financial information, that any compromise or adjustment reflects the maximum amount the debtor can afford. 

It defines two repayment options:
- Compromise: a lump-sum payment for less than the full debt, with the remaining balance cancelled. The debtor may keep the security property only if the compromise amount is at least the property’s net recovery value and it benefits the Government.
- Adjustment: an initial lump-sum payment followed by a short-term payment plan, for no longer than five years. Any remaining debt is cancelled after the plan is completed, but the debtor generally cannot keep the security property except in the case of a valueless lien.

The section also outlines servicing requirements for approved adjustments, including setting up an adjustment account, sending billing statements, and archiving any balance above the agreed amount. If the debtor defaults, the full debt is reinstated, and past-due adjustment accounts must be contacted quickly and referred for offset. Finally, it explains that an adjustment agreement may be modified based on the debtor’s circumstances, but only one amendment may be approved without higher-level authorization, and any change must be documented and confirmed in writing.

nodes.title: B. Adjustment
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how the Agency handles delinquent debts under USDA Rural Housing servicing rules. It covers three main actions:

- **Compromise and adjustment of debt:**  
  The Agency may accept less than the full amount owed only if the debtor can pay the maximum amount they can afford based on financial information. A **compromise** is a lump-sum settlement for less than the debt, with the remaining balance canceled; the debtor may keep the security property only if the compromise amount meets the net recovery value and it benefits the Government. An **adjustment** allows an initial lump sum plus a short repayment plan of up to 5 years, after which any remaining balance is canceled, but the debtor generally cannot keep the security property. The document also explains how adjustment accounts are managed, how defaults are handled, how past-due accounts are pursued, and how agreement amendments must be approved and documented.

- **Cancellation of an adjustment agreement:**  
  If the debtor cannot or will not continue repayment, the Servicing Office must cancel the adjustment agreement in writing and explain appeal rights. Payments made before cancellation are kept by the Agency.

- **Recall from Treasury and charge-off of debt:**  
  The Agency may recall a debt from Treasury when further collection is no longer in the Government’s best interest, based on collection history and thresholds over a three-year period. Recalled debts are canceled and may result in IRS Form 1099-C issuance. If compromise or adjustment cannot be reached but the debtor can still repay some portion now or later, the debt may be **charged off** using Form RD 3550-20.

nodes.title: RECALL TREASURY DEBT
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains USDA debt servicing procedures for Single Family Housing loans. It covers:  
- **Compromise and adjustment of debt:** If the debtor cannot or will not repay, the Servicing Office must cancel the adjustment agreement in writing and inform the debtor of appeal rights; any payments made before cancellation are kept by the Agency.  
- **Recall of Treasury debt:** The Agency may recall debt from Treasury when it decides further collection is not in the government’s best interest, based on legal authority and collection history. It reviews whether Treasury collected at least 30% of the debt or more than $3,600 over three years before deciding to recall and cancel the debt, with IRS Form 1099-C issued when applicable.  
- **Charge-off of debt:** If compromise or adjustment cannot be reached, but the debtor may still be able to repay some amount now or later, the debt may be charged off using Form RD 3550-20.

nodes.title: CHARGE-OFF OF DEBT
nodes.summary: The partial document explains several debt resolution actions for USDA single-family housing loans. It covers: when an adjustment or compromise agreement is canceled and how the debtor is notified; the process for recalling debt from Treasury when further collection is not worthwhile, including review criteria based on collection history and issuing IRS Form 1099-C after cancellation; the rules for charging off debt, which does not remove the debtor’s liability and may still lead to Treasury collection; and the conditions for canceling debt, including when no other settlement options remain, no security is left, or the debtor cannot repay now or in the future. It also notes the forms and documentation used for these actions and special rules for debts tied to secured property under a certain dollar threshold.

nodes.title: CANCELLATION OF DEBT
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains rules for debt charge-off and cancellation. It states that charging off a debt does not eliminate the debtor’s liability, and charged-off debts are referred to the Treasury for collection. If Treasury determines the debt is uncollectible and returns it to the agency, the agency may cancel the debt under paragraph 7.15. It also notes that charged-off accounts may still be settled through compromise, adjustment, or cancellation, but the debtor generally cannot keep the security property except in limited cases, such as a valueless lien or when the secured debt is $7,500 or less.

The document then describes debt cancellation requirements. Debt can be canceled only when other settlement methods are unavailable, no security remains, and the debtor is unable to repay any part of the debt now or in the future. If security remains, cancellation may still be approved when the secured debt is under $7,500 and the account has been accelerated. It identifies Form RD 3550-20 as the standard form for cancellation, while also allowing documentation directly in LoanServ if the decision is made without the form. Finally, it explains that a debtor may apply for cancellation when there is no deficiency judgment or when the debtor cannot pay and has no reasonable prospects of future repayment.

nodes.title: A. Application from Debtor
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for debt charge-off and debt cancellation. It states that a charge-off does not remove the debtor’s liability, that charged-off debts are referred to Treasury for collection, and that such debts may later be compromised, adjusted, or canceled. It also notes restrictions on retaining security property after charge-off. The cancellation section describes when debt may be canceled, including cases where no other settlement options exist, the debtor cannot pay, there is no remaining security, or the debt is below a certain threshold with secured property. It outlines procedures and forms used for cancellation, and lists circumstances allowing cancellation with or without a debtor’s application, such as legal unenforceability, Treasury returning the debt as uncollectible, death of the debtor, bankruptcy discharge, uncollectible judgments, and foreclosure deficiencies where further collection is not allowed.

nodes.title: B. No Application from Debtor
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for canceling debts and negotiating debt settlements under HB-2-3550. It explains when a debt may be canceled without an application from the debtor, such as when the debt is legally unenforceable, uncollectible after Treasury collection efforts, the debtor is deceased with no recoverable assets, the debt was discharged in bankruptcy, certain judgments are uncollectible, or a foreclosure deficiency cannot be further pursued. It then begins the section on negotiating settlements, emphasizing that settlements must be handled consistently and thoroughly documented, and outlines the need to assess the debtor’s financial condition by reviewing income, assets, liens, equity, future earning potential, and other debts, including use of Form RD 3550-20 and supporting verification documents.

nodes.title: NEGOTIATING A SETTLEMENT
nodes.summary: The partial document describes procedures for negotiating a debt settlement, including when a debtor is considered unable to pay and how that determination is documented in LoanServ. It explains that settlements must be handled consistently and may involve a legal representative if the debtor cannot act. The section outlines how the Servicing Office should assess the debtor’s financial condition before making a settlement offer, focusing on signatories and cosigners, and considering factors such as prior liens, current security value, equity in other assets, income prospects over the next five years, and other debts. It also references Form RD 3550-20 as the tool for gathering financial and household expense information, and says the Servicing Office should request additional documentation within 30 days to verify the debtor’s financial situation, starting with income verification such as paystubs or Social Security checks.

nodes.title: A. Assessing the Debtor’s Financial Condition
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains the USDA Rural Housing Servicing rules for negotiating a debt settlement under HB-2-3550, Paragraph 7.16. It covers when a settlement may be considered, emphasizing that settlements must be handled consistently to avoid unequal treatment and that a legal representative may act for a debtor who cannot.

A major section focuses on assessing the debtor’s financial condition before settlement. It lists the factors the Servicing Office should review, such as prior liens, the value of the security, other assets, total income and future earning prospects, and other debts. It also describes use of Form RD 3550-20 to document household finances and explains that the debtor may need to provide additional verification, such as pay stubs, tax returns, bank statements, expense records, property records, and wage information. It notes that verification costs may be charged to the debtor and that credit reports are used only when financial information appears incomplete or inaccurate.

The document also addresses joint debtors and requires documentation when some joint debtors are excluded from a compromise, adjustment, or cancellation agreement. Finally, it outlines special cases, including deceased debtors, where an executor or administrator may negotiate settlement based on estate value, and disappeared debtors, where the agency must make and document reasonable efforts to locate the debtor before charging off or cancelling the debt.

nodes.title: B. Special Case Debtors
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes procedures for negotiating debt settlements and documenting debtor information. It explains what financial verification may be needed, such as medical expenses, tax returns, bank statements, county records, and wage data, and notes that verification costs are charged to the debtor’s account. It also states that credit reports should be obtained only when there is reason to suspect incomplete or inaccurate financial information.

The document then addresses settlement issues involving joint debtors, requiring the servicer to explain why some joint parties are not included in the application, such as when they are deceased, in bankruptcy, or missing. It also covers special cases: settlements for deceased debtors, which may be handled by an executor or administrator and based on estate value after higher-priority expenses; and debtors who have disappeared, where the servicer must make and document reasonable efforts to locate them before charging off or cancelling the debt.

nodes.title: 1. Deceased Debtors
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers procedures for negotiating a settlement on a debt, including what financial and property information should be verified, how verification costs are handled, and when credit reports may be obtained. It also explains special rules for joint debtors, including when missing parties may be excluded and the need to document those reasons. In addition, it addresses special case debtors: settlements for deceased debtors through an executor or administrator, how claims against estates should be evaluated, and the required efforts to locate debtors who have disappeared before a debt can be charged off or cancelled.

nodes.title: 2. Debtors Who Have Disappeared
nodes.summary: The partial document explains USDA debt settlement procedures under HB-2-3550, focusing on how settlements are negotiated and processed. It covers the types of financial verifications that may be used to assess a debtor’s situation, when credit reports can be obtained, and how to document cases involving joint debtors who cannot all participate. It also describes special handling for deceased debtors and debtors who have disappeared, including the need for reasonable search efforts and documentation. In addition, it notes that loans should generally not be settled while other Agency loans on the same property remain active unless it benefits the government. Finally, it outlines post-approval processing steps such as applying escrow/unapplied funds to the debt, executing Form RD 3550-20, notifying the debtor in writing, and canceling any offset actions when a compromise, adjustment, or cancellation is approved.

nodes.title: 3. Settlement Where Debt or Owes More Than One Type of Agency Loan
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Development debt settlement procedures, focusing on when a property loan should not be settled if other agency loans on the same security remain active unless it benefits the Government. It then explains how debt settlements are processed, including applying escrow and unapplied funds to the debt once settlement is complete. Finally, it lists required actions after a settlement is approved, such as executing Form RD 3550-20, notifying the debtor in writing of the approved terms or cancellation, attaching the form, and canceling any offset requests by notifying the paying agencies involved.

nodes.title: PROCESSING DEBT SETTLEMENTS
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules and procedures for negotiating and processing USDA debt settlements. It states that a loan should generally not be settled if other USDA agency loans on the same property are still active unless doing so benefits the government. It then outlines how escrow and unapplied funds must be handled during settlement, noting that any remaining funds are applied to the debt once settlement is complete. Finally, it lists the actions the Servicing Office must take after a settlement is approved, including executing Form RD 3550-20, notifying the debtor in writing, informing the debtor of any cancellation if applicable, and canceling any offsets with paying agencies for compromises, adjustments, or cancellations.

nodes.title: A. Escrow and Unapplied Funds [7 CFR 3550.252(h)]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules and procedures for negotiating and processing debt settlements under USDA loan servicing. It explains that a property loan should generally not be settled while other USDA agency loans tied to the same property remain active, unless doing so benefits the government. It also outlines how escrow and unapplied funds must be handled during settlement, requiring remaining funds to be applied to the debt once settlement is complete. Finally, it describes the required steps after a settlement is approved, including executing Form RD 3550-20, notifying the debtor in writing of the approved terms, informing the debtor of cancellations when applicable, and canceling any offset actions for compromises, adjustments, or cancellations.

nodes.title: B. Approved Settlements
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA debt settlement procedures for Rural Housing loans. It explains when a property loan should not be settled if other agency loans on the same collateral are still active, unless doing so benefits the government. It then outlines how debt settlements are processed, including handling escrow and unapplied funds, actions required after a settlement is approved, steps for documenting and notifying borrowers when a settlement is rejected, and requirements to report settled debts to the IRS and credit agencies. Finally, it describes what happens to promissory notes and security instruments after a debt is satisfied, including when notes are returned to the debtor or retained in the case file.

nodes.title: C. Rejected Settlements
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for processing debt settlements. It covers how to handle rejected settlement offers, including documenting the reason for rejection, notifying the debtor in writing, and informing them of appeal rights. It also describes reporting settled debts to the IRS using Form 1099-C and notifying credit reporting agencies. Finally, it outlines the disposition of promissory notes and security instruments after a debt is satisfied, including when notes and release letters are returned to the debtor or retained in the case file.

nodes.title: D. Notify the IRS and Credit Agencies [7 CFR 3550.252(e)]
nodes.summary: The partial document explains procedures for handling debt settlements. It covers how rejected settlement offers must be documented on Form RD 3550-20, how the debtor must be notified in writing with appeal rights, and how the original form and copy are filed. It also describes required reporting of settled debts to the IRS using Form 1099-C and notification to credit agencies. Finally, it outlines what happens to promissory notes and security instruments after a debt is satisfied, including when satisfaction/release letters are issued, when notes are returned to the debtor, and when they are retained in the case file.

nodes.title: DISPOSITION OF PROMISSORY NOTES AND RELEASE OF SECURITY INSTRUMENTS
nodes.summary:
The document covers procedures for processing debt settlements, including how to handle rejected settlement offers, document the rejection on Form RD 3550-20, notify the debtor in writing with appeal rights, and file records appropriately. It also explains requirements for notifying the IRS and credit agencies after a debt has been settled, including use of IRS Form 1099-C and stopping further collection efforts.

In addition, it describes how to dispose of promissory notes and release security instruments after debt satisfaction, depending on whether the debtor requested the settlement or the Agency cancelled the debt. Finally, the excerpt begins a glossary of key housing and loan-related terms, providing definitions for items such as acceleration, actual net recovery value, adequate housing, adjusted income, adjustment, administrator, adverse decision, affordable housing product, agency, allowable excess costs, and amortized payment.

structure.title: GLOSSARY
structure.summary: The partial document is a glossary and table of contents for USDA Rural Housing Service’s HB-2-3550 direct single-family housing loan and grant program. It mainly defines key terms used in the program, including loan and housing concepts, income eligibility categories, subsidy types, security and foreclosure terms, property and appraisal terms, servicing and repayment terms, tribal and veteran-related definitions, and agency roles and systems. The document also begins the regulatory table of contents for 7 CFR Part 3550, covering general provisions such as applicability, purpose, civil rights, appeals, environmental requirements, state law, conflict of interest, definitions, and the start of Section 502 loan origination rules, including program objectives, loan purposes, eligible costs, and loan restrictions.

structure.title: APPENDIX 1
structure.summary:
This partial document is the table of contents and opening sections of 7 CFR Part 3550, which governs USDA Rural Housing Service direct single-family housing loans and grants. It outlines the structure of the rule and the major topics covered, including general program provisions, section 502 loan origination, section 504 repair loans and grants, regular servicing, special servicing, and post-servicing actions.

Key points covered include:
- Program scope, purpose, civil rights protections, appeals, environmental compliance, state law considerations, exception authority, and conflict-of-interest rules.
- Detailed definitions used throughout the regulation, such as borrower, applicant, repayment income, adjusted income, payment assistance, interest credit, manufactured home, modest housing, and REO property.
- Section 502 loan origination requirements, including eligibility, income and asset calculations, application procedures, site and dwelling standards, ownership and security requirements, insurance, appraisals, loan limits, interest rates, repayment terms, subsidies, deferred payments, conditional commitments, and manufactured home rules.
- Special provisions for nonprogram loans and certified loan application packaging.
- Section 504 loan and grant origination for very low-income homeowners, including repair/rehabilitation purposes, eligibility, application processing, property requirements, loan/grant limits, and related WWD grant provisions.
- Regular servicing topics such as payment processing, fees, inspections, escrow, borrower obligations, subsidy changes, military duty protections, refinancing, final payoff, recapture, transfer and assumption, and unauthorized assistance.
- Special servicing measures for delinquent or distressed accounts, including late fees, liquidation, moratoriums, reamortization, offsets, and protective advances.
- Post-servicing rules for property management, disposition, debt settlement, compromise or adjustment, and handling REO or custodial property.

Overall, the document provides the regulatory framework for how USDA administers direct rural housing loans and grants from application through servicing, delinquency resolution, and final disposition.

nodes.title: 7 CFR Part 3550
nodes.summary: The partial document is a glossary from 7 CFR Part 3550 defining key terms used in USDA Rural Development housing programs. It covers definitions related to lending and program administration, including Rural Development agencies, scheduled payments, secured and unsecured loans, security property, subsidy types, total debt ratio, unauthorized assistance, U.S. citizen status, and USDA.

nodes.title: § 3550.10 (Con.)
nodes.summary: This excerpt from 7 CFR Part 3550 provides regulatory definitions and guidance related to USDA Rural Development housing programs. It defines key terms such as Rural Development, scheduled payment, secured and unsecured loans, security property, subsidy, total debt ratio, unauthorized assistance, U.S. citizen, USDA, value appreciation, very low income, and veterans preference. It also explains the circumstances under which veterans or their families may receive preference, including qualifying active-duty service periods. The section then begins § 3550.11, which requires State Directors to assess the availability of certified homeowner education in their states, keep an updated list of providers and costs, and follow a preferred order of education formats, favoring classroom, one-on-one counseling, or interactive video conference, with home-study or telephone counseling allowed if those are not reasonably available.

nodes.title: § 3550.11 State Director Assessment of Homeowner Education.
nodes.summary: The partial document defines key terms and requirements under 7 CFR Part 3550 related to rural housing programs. It explains how “value appreciation” is calculated, defines “very low income,” and describes “veterans preference” eligibility based on military service periods. It also outlines the State Director’s role in assessing available homeowner education providers, sets the preferred formats for homeowner education, and specifies acceptable certification sources for counselors. Finally, it lists the required homeownership education topics borrowers must complete, including readiness, budgeting, credit, home shopping, mortgages, closing, foreclosure prevention, and homeowner maintenance.

nodes.title: § 3550.11 (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service regulations for homeownership education and Section 502 loan origination. It explains that if standard in-person or other required education formats are not reasonably available, applicants may satisfy the homeownership education requirement through online counseling. It specifies that completion must be documented by a letter or certificate from an approved certified counselor or organization, such as HUD, NeighborWorks America, NFHC, NAIHC, or a state-approved housing finance agency.

The document also lists the minimum topics that homeownership education must cover, including readiness for homeownership, budgeting, credit counseling, shopping for a home, predatory lending and lender differences, obtaining a mortgage, loan closing, post-occupancy counseling for delinquency and foreclosure prevention, and homeownership responsibilities like maintenance and repairs. It notes that the provider may tailor the education to the borrower’s needs as long as these topics are satisfactorily addressed.

In addition, the text includes administrative information about an OMB control number for the information collection requirements, then begins Subpart B on Section 502 Origination. This section states the program’s objective: to help low- and very low-income rural applicants obtain adequate housing when they cannot get credit elsewhere, often requiring other funding sources when feasible. It also outlines the basic purposes of Section 502 loans, which may be used to buy, build, rehabilitate, improve, relocate, or in limited cases refinance an eligible dwelling, including specific rules for purchasing homes already financed by RHS loans.

nodes.title: § 3550.50 OMB control number.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service regulations for Section 502 homeownership loans. It includes a note that homeownership education may be tailored to the borrower’s needs, an OMB control number and reporting burden statement, and then begins Subpart B on Section 502 origination. The main substantive points explain the program’s objective: helping low- and very low-income rural applicants obtain adequate housing when they cannot get credit elsewhere, often requiring use of other financing sources if feasible. It also outlines eligible loan purposes, such as buying, building, rehabilitating, improving, relocating, or in limited cases refinancing a dwelling, and provides rules for purchases involving existing RHS borrowers, including assumption of existing debt and use of funds for additional financing, repairs, or buying out a co-borrower’s equity.

nodes.title: Subpart B - Section 502 Origination
nodes.summary: This portion of 7 CFR Part 3550 covers the tail end of the homeownership education requirements, noting that providers may tailor training to the borrower’s needs, followed by a reserved section and an OMB control number notice for the regulation’s information collection requirements. It then begins Subpart B on Section 502 Origination, explaining the program’s purpose: helping low- and very low-income rural applicants obtain adequate housing when they cannot get credit elsewhere, often requiring use of other funding sources as well. The section also states that §§ 3550.52 through 3550.73 govern loan origination on program terms, while § 3550.74 covers nonprogram terms. Finally, it introduces loan purposes, specifying that Section 502 funds may be used to buy, build, rehabilitate, improve, or relocate an eligible dwelling and related facilities for a permanent residence, and in limited cases refinance existing debt. It also includes a rule for purchases from existing RHS borrowers, requiring assumption of the existing RHS loan and allowing additional financing, repairs, or buyout of a departing co-borrower’s equity.

nodes.title: § 3550.51 Program objectives.
nodes.summary: This section of 7 CFR Part 3550 covers requirements related to rural housing loans under Section 502. It explains that homeownership education may be tailored to a borrower’s needs, notes the OMB control number and reporting burden for the information collection, and begins Subpart B on Section 502 loan origination. The main program objective is to provide financing for low- and very low-income persons in rural areas who cannot obtain credit elsewhere, with applicants generally expected to combine Section 502 funds with other financing when possible. It also outlines allowable loan purposes, such as buying, building, rehabilitating, improving, or relocating a dwelling and related facilities, and mentions limited refinancing. For properties already financed by RHS, the borrower must assume the existing loan, though additional financing, repairs, or buying out a departing co-borrower’s equity may also be allowed.

nodes.title: § 3550.52 Loan purposes.
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers requirements and objectives for the USDA Rural Housing Service’s Section 502 home loan program. It explains that homeownership education may be tailored to borrower needs, notes an OMB approval/control number for the information collection, and introduces Subpart B on Section 502 loan origination. The main program objective is to help low- and very low-income rural applicants obtain adequate housing when they cannot get credit elsewhere, often by combining Section 502 funds with other financing. It also outlines allowable loan purposes, including buying, building, rehabilitating, improving, relocating, or in limited cases refinancing a dwelling, and describes special rules for purchasing properties already financed by RHS, including assumption of existing debt and additional financing for repairs or equity buyout of a departing co-borrower.

nodes.title: § 3550.52 (Con.)
nodes.summary: This section of 7 CFR Part 3550 explains when existing debt may be refinanced with an RHS loan and what costs may be included in the loan. It covers refinancing of non-RHS loans for existing dwellings and vacant building sites, refinancing certain RHS debt in limited circumstances, and refinancing debts incurred after application but before closing if they meet program rules. It also lists eligible loan costs, including loan-related fees, special accessibility features, utility connections, lender charges in leveraged loans, taxes and escrow accounts, packaging fees, essential household equipment, energy-saving measures, site preparation, and homeownership education fees.

nodes.title: § 3550.52 (d) (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The document excerpt outlines eligible uses and restrictions for Rural Housing Service loan funds under 7 CFR Part 3550, followed by borrower eligibility requirements.

Main points covered include:
- Eligible loan costs such as:
  - special design features or equipment needed due to disability,
  - utility connection fees and related assessments,
  - certain lender charges in leveraged loans,
  - real estate taxes and escrow setup costs,
  - packaging fees under certified loan application packaging rules,
  - essential household equipment like ranges or refrigerators,
  - approved energy-saving measures and heating equipment,
  - site preparation costs,
  - homeownership education fees.
- Loan restrictions stating funds cannot be used to:
  - buy an existing manufactured home,
  - purchase or improve income-producing property mainly for business use,
  - pay fees or commissions to for-profit entities related to packaging or referrals.
- Eligibility requirements for RHS loans, including:
  - income limits at approval and closing,
  - U.S. citizenship or legal alien status,
  - intent and ability to occupy the dwelling as a primary residence,
  - special conditions for active military members and full-time students,
  - requirements for someone in the household to oversee inspections and progress payments during construction or renovation.

nodes.title: § 3550.52 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines restrictions on how loan funds may be used and the eligibility requirements for a loan program under 7 CFR Part 3550. It states that loan funds cannot be used to buy an existing manufactured home, purchase or improve property mainly used to generate income, or pay certain fees and commissions to for-profit loan packagers or referral entities. It then explains borrower eligibility rules, including income limits at approval and closing, citizenship or legal alien status, and the requirement that the dwelling be used as a permanent primary residence. The document also notes special restrictions for active-duty military personnel and full-time students, and requires an adult household member to be available during construction or renovation to inspect work and authorize payments.

nodes.title: § 3550.53 Eligibility requirements.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA RHS loan restrictions and borrower eligibility requirements under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains what loan funds cannot be used for, including purchasing existing manufactured homes, buying income-producing property, and paying certain fees to for-profit entities. It then outlines eligibility rules such as income limits at approval and closing, citizenship or eligible alien status, and the requirement that the home be the applicant’s primary permanent residence. The document also notes special rules for military personnel, full-time students, and construction or renovation cases. In addition, it describes when current homeowners may qualify for loans, legal capacity requirements, treatment of suspended or debarred applicants, and repayment ability standards based on income and debt ratios.

nodes.title: § 3550.53 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines eligibility requirements for Rural Housing Service (RHS) loans under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains when current homeowners may qualify for loans, including refinancing, buying a new dwelling if the current home is deficient, making repairs to property financed with an affordable non-RHS loan, or receiving a subsequent RHS loan. It also covers legal capacity requirements, rules for handling suspended or debarred applicants, and repayment ability standards based on income, debt, and housing costs, including allowable ratios and the use of cosigners or additional household members if needed. Finally, it describes credit qualification standards, requiring applicants to be unable to obtain credit elsewhere on reasonable terms and to have acceptable credit history, and lists examples of unacceptable credit such as recent delinquencies, recent foreclosure, unpaid tax liens, and certain outstanding court judgments.

nodes.title: § 3550.53 ( g) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document describes Rural Housing Service loan eligibility requirements, focusing on repayment ability, credit qualifications, and homeownership education. It explains when repayment ratios may be allowed to exceed standard limits, and notes that applicants who do not meet repayment requirements may qualify with a cosigner or by combining household income. It also outlines credit standards, including unacceptable credit indicators such as recent delinquencies, foreclosures, unpaid tax liens, outstanding judgments, rent payment problems, collection accounts, and certain debt settlements, while identifying some situations that are not considered unacceptable credit, such as older bankruptcies or satisfied judgments. The section further states that applicants rejected for credit reasons must be told why. Finally, it requires first-time homebuyers to complete a certified homeownership education course before loan closing, while allowing case-by-case exceptions where the training is not reasonably available or where a special need would make completion difficult.

nodes.title: § 3550.53 (h) (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Rural Housing Service loan eligibility requirements related to credit history, homeownership education, and income calculation. It lists specific credit issues that indicate unacceptable credit, such as repeated late rent payments, unresolved collection accounts, recent debt write-offs or settlements, and delinquency on federal debt. It also notes exceptions for older bankruptcies and satisfied judgments, and requires applicants rejected for credit reasons to be told why. The document then explains the homeownership education requirement for first-time homebuyers, including certification, possible exceptions when education is not reasonably available, and case-by-case waivers for special needs.

The second major topic is how to calculate repayment income and annual income. Repayment income includes expected annual income of household members on the promissory note, excluding certain student financial aid used for education-related costs. Annual income includes income from all household members except for several specified exclusions, such as income of minors, foster care payments, medical reimbursements, certain student earnings, temporary or sporadic income, lump-sum additions, earned income tax credit, adoption assistance above certain limits, property tax rebates, disability-related state payments, student financial aid, and other federally exempt revenue.

nodes.title: § 3550.54 Calculation of income and assets.
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains how income and assets are calculated for USDA Rural Development housing programs under 7 CFR Part 3550. It covers four main areas:

- **Repayment income:** the annual income expected from household members who are on the promissory note, excluding certain student financial aid used for education-related expenses. This is used to assess the household’s ability to repay a loan.
- **Annual income:** the income of all household members, with a detailed list of income types that are excluded, such as income of minors, foster care payments, certain medical reimbursements, sporadic income, lump-sum payments, earned income tax credits, some adoption assistance, property tax rebates, disability-related state payments, student aid, and other federally exempt income.
- **Adjusted income:** annual income after specific deductions, used to determine eligibility for Sections 502 and 504 and the amount of payment subsidy. Deductions include allowances for certain household members, child care costs, disability-related care expenses, elderly household deductions, and elderly medical expenses above a set threshold.
- **Net family assets:** income from assets must be counted in annual income, and the value of these assets may also affect whether a down payment is required.

nodes.title: § 3550.54 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how Rural Housing Service program rules calculate household financial eligibility. It defines “adjusted income” as annual income minus certain allowable deductions, including deductions for dependents, childcare needed for work or education, disability-related care expenses, and, for elderly households, additional deductions for elderly status and unreimbursed medical expenses above a set threshold. It also describes “net family assets,” noting that income from these assets is counted in annual income and can affect whether a down payment is required. The document lists what counts as net family assets, such as real property equity, cash, savings, trust funds, stocks, bonds, lump-sum receipts, and investment property, and what does not count, including restricted Indian land interests, necessary personal property, business or farm assets, certain retirement accounts, irrevocable trusts, life insurance cash value, tax-advantaged education and health accounts, and other excluded amounts designated by the agency.

nodes.title: § 3550.54(d)(Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service regulations in 7 CFR Part 3550. It first defines what counts as “net family assets” for loan eligibility purposes, including items such as real estate equity, cash, bank accounts, trust funds available to the household, stocks and bonds, lump-sum receipts, and investment property. It also lists exclusions from net family assets, such as restricted Indian land interests, cash used to reduce the loan amount, necessary personal property, assets tied to an active business or farming operation, certain retirement accounts, irrevocable trusts beyond household control, life insurance cash value, tax-advantaged education and health savings accounts, and other amounts the agency excludes.

The document then describes the loan application process under § 3550.55. Applicants must submit a complete written application in the required RHS format, and applications are accepted even when funds are unavailable. Incomplete applications are returned for correction, applicants may withdraw voluntarily, and RHS may ask applicants to confirm continued interest. Applicants are notified in writing of eligibility or ineligibility, with reasons provided for denials. Finally, when funds are limited, applications are selected for processing based on funding priorities, processed in order of completed receipt within those priorities, with veterans preference applied when appropriate, and loans are funded first-come, first-served after selection.

nodes.title: § 3550.55 Applications.
nodes.summary: The document outlines the application and site requirements for RHS loans under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains how applicants must submit complete written applications, how incomplete or withdrawn applications are handled, and how RHS may request reconfirmation of interest or reevaluate eligibility if circumstances change. It also describes the order in which applications are selected for processing when funding is limited, establishing priority categories such as existing customers needing health and safety repairs, REO-related loans, hardship cases, and self-help or leveraging projects, with veterans preference applied in certain ties. Finally, it states that eligible applicants must provide underwriting information within a set timeframe and that loans are only available in rural areas designated by RHS, with special rules if an area later becomes non-rural.

nodes.title: § 3550.55 (c) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines the priority order for processing RHS housing loan applications and the related timeframe for applicants to submit underwriting information. It also begins the site requirements section, stating that loans may only be made in rural areas designated by RHS and explaining how changed area designations affect new and existing conditional commitments.

nodes.title: § 3550.56 Site requirements.
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers the priority order for processing RHS Section 502 loan applications and the site requirements for making loans. It explains that first priority goes to existing customers seeking loans to correct health and safety hazards, followed by loans tied to REO property sales or transfers of existing RHS-financed property, then applicants facing housing-related hardships, and finally applicants for Mutual Self-Help construction or those leveraging other financing. Applications without priority status are processed after all priority applications, and the Administrator may temporarily reclassify certain packaged applications as fourth priority. It also states that RHS will set a reasonable timeframe for eligible applicants to submit underwriting information. In addition, the site requirements section says loans may only be made in rural areas designated by RHS, and if an area changes to non-rural, only certain existing conditional commitments tied to prior applicants will be honored.

nodes.title: § 3550.56 (a)(Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines Rural Housing Service loan property requirements. It covers: approval of REO property sales, transfers with assumption, and subsequent loans for repairs or related transactions; site standards requiring compliance with federal, state, and local development rules, limits on subdividing, restrictions on included structures, and a cap on site value relative to the property’s improved value; and dwelling requirements, including that homes must be modest, not intended for income-producing use, generally without in-ground pools or excessive market value unless an exception is approved. It also distinguishes rules for new dwellings, which must meet construction standards, and existing dwellings, which must be structurally sound, in good repair, properly equipped with utilities and systems, and free of pests and wood-damaging organisms.

nodes.title: § 3550.57 Dwelling requirements.
nodes.summary:
This excerpt from 7 CFR Part 3550 covers property eligibility requirements for RHS housing loans. It explains when REO property sales, transfers with assumption, and subsequent loans may be processed. It then outlines site standards, including compliance with development standards, zoning limits, restrictions on farm service buildings, and the rule that the site value generally cannot exceed 30 percent of the property’s “as improved” market value, with possible waivers in high-cost areas.

The document also details dwelling requirements, stating that homes must be modest, not intended for income-producing use, and generally free of in-ground pools or excessive market value unless an exception is approved for special household needs. It distinguishes between new dwellings, which must meet construction standards, and existing dwellings, which must be structurally sound, in good repair, and equipped with adequate utilities and pest-free conditions.

Finally, it sets ownership requirements after loan closing, describing acceptable interests in the property: fee-simple ownership, secure leasehold interests with minimum lease terms, life estate interests, undivided interests with rules for co-owners, and possessory rights on tribal or restricted lands.

nodes.title: § 3550.58 Ownership requirements.
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules on borrower ownership and loan security requirements. It explains the acceptable forms of property interest a borrower may hold after loan closing, including fee-simple ownership, leasehold interests with minimum remaining terms, life estates, undivided interests with co-owner signature rules, and certain American Indian possessory rights. It then outlines security standards for loans, such as requiring a mortgage on all ownership interests or acceptable alternative ownership forms, prohibiting problematic liens unless specific exceptions apply, ensuring title clearance and proper legal services, and confirming that property improvements do not encroach on adjoining land. Finally, it notes that mortgage insurance or government/tribal guarantees can satisfy security requirements, and that RHS may require escrow accounts for taxes, insurance, and related housing costs.

nodes.title: § 3550.59 Security requirements.
nodes.summary: This excerpt covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) loan security requirements and escrow rules. It explains when a loan is considered adequately secured, including requirements for RHS to obtain a mortgage on the property, restrictions on prior and junior liens, compliance with title clearance and legal services rules, and ensuring property improvements do not encroach on adjoining land. It also notes that government or tribal mortgage insurance can serve as adequate security. In addition, it describes RHS authority to require escrow accounts for taxes, hazard and flood insurance, and related costs in line with RESPA and the Housing Act of 1949.

nodes.title: § 3550.60 Escrow account.
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines Rural Housing Service (RHS) loan security and property protection requirements under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains what counts as adequate security for a loan, including securing a mortgage interest, avoiding problematic liens, following title clearance/legal service rules, and ensuring property improvements are on-site. It also notes that certain government or tribal mortgage insurance can satisfy security requirements.

The document then covers escrow accounts, stating RHS may require borrowers to deposit funds to cover real estate taxes, hazard and flood insurance, and related costs. It further details insurance obligations: borrowers with secured indebtedness over $15,000 must maintain hazard insurance, coverage must be sufficient for the dwelling and essential buildings, and RHS may force-place insurance if the borrower fails to do so. Flood insurance is mandatory for properties in Special Flood Hazard Areas, and if NFIP coverage is unavailable, the property is ineligible for federal financial assistance.

Finally, the document addresses handling insurance losses, including limits on deductibles, borrower notification responsibilities, possible supervision of loss payments by RHS, compliance with repair and replacement standards, and the order in which leftover insurance proceeds must be applied, such as to prior liens and past-due amounts.

nodes.title: § 3550.61 Insurance.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) loan requirements related to property insurance, appraisals, and maximum loan limits. It explains that borrowers with secured debt over $15,000 must maintain hazard insurance, with required coverage levels, loss payable clauses, and rules for force-placed insurance if the borrower does not insure the property. It also requires flood insurance for properties in Special Flood Hazard Areas and describes what happens after losses occur, including deductible limits, reporting obligations, supervision of repairs, distribution of insurance proceeds, and RHS options if the borrower cannot fund repairs. The document then outlines when appraisals are required, standards for conducting them, appraisal fees, and exceptions. Finally, it states that total secured indebtedness may not exceed applicable loan limit or market value restrictions, with some fees excluded from those limits.

nodes.title: § 3550.61 (d) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document describes USDA Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules on insurance losses, repairs, appraisals, and maximum loan limits. It explains what happens when a property loss occurs without insurance, including the borrower’s responsibility to repair the property and options RHS may use if the borrower cannot pay, such as making a repair loan, subordinating the lien, allowing a junior lien, making a protective advance, or accelerating the account. It then sets requirements for appraisals, including when they are needed, how they must be conducted, and related fee rules. Finally, it outlines the maximum loan limit, stating that total secured debt cannot exceed certain area or market-value limits, with some fees excluded from those limits.

nodes.title: § 3550.62 Appraisals.
nodes.summary: The document section outlines rules for RHS housing loans regarding insurance losses, appraisals, and loan limits. It explains what happens when a property loss occurs and insurance is missing, including the borrower’s responsibility for repairs and possible RHS actions if the borrower cannot afford them, such as making a repair loan, subordinating the lien, allowing junior financing, making a protective advance, or accelerating the account. It then describes appraisal requirements, stating when an appraisal is needed, how it must be performed, and when appraisal fees apply or are waived. Finally, it sets the maximum loan limit, requiring total secured debt to stay within the lower of the area loan limit or market value limits, while excluding certain fees from those limits and noting that the rule does not apply to loans on NP terms.

nodes.title: § 3550.63 Maximum loan limit.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers USDA Rural Housing Service loan servicing rules on property loss and insurance, appraisal requirements, and maximum loan limits.

Main points include:
- If a loss occurs and insurance is still in force, any insurance proceeds may be used for protective advances or released to the borrower if the RHS debt is adequately secured.
- If a loss occurs without insurance coverage, the borrower must repair or replace the damaged property and restore insurance.
- If the borrower cannot afford repairs, RHS may help by making a repair loan, subordinating its lien, allowing a junior lien from another source, making a protective advance, or accelerating the account.
- Appraisals are required when the loan exceeds $15,000 or when RHS needs to verify adequate security, and they must follow Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practices.
- RHS charges appraisal fees in most cases, with some exceptions for certain subsequent repair loans or acceptable third-party appraisals.
- The total secured indebtedness cannot exceed the lower of the area loan limit or market value limits.
- Area loan limits are based on a percentage of local HUD section 203(b) limits, are reviewed annually, and are published by the agency.
- Loan limits are reduced when the applicant already owns the site, buys below market value, or receives certain housing grants/assistance.
- For self-help housing, the loan limit is based on the lot value, construction costs, and sweat equity, subject to the overall area limit.

nodes.title: § 3550.63 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document describes key loan and financing rules under 7 CFR Part 3550 for Rural Housing Service programs. It covers how the maximum area loan limit is determined based on local HUD section 203(b) limits and adjusted for factors like land ownership, low-cost land purchases, grants, and self-help housing contributions. It also explains market value limitations for existing and new dwellings, including possible increases for certain closing-cost, repair, or refinance situations. In addition, the section outlines down payment requirements for elderly and non-elderly families based on net family assets, specifies that loans use the applicable RHS interest rate in effect at approval or closing, and states that repayment periods cannot exceed the useful life of the property, with a general maximum of 33 years.

nodes.title: § 3550.63 (Con.)
nodes.summary: This section covers key loan and financing requirements for RHS housing loans. It explains market value limitations for existing and new dwellings, including when the limit is 100% or 90% of market value and how it can be increased for certain subsequent loans or refinancing. It also sets down payment rules requiring elderly and non-elderly families to apply net family assets above specified thresholds toward the purchase. In addition, it states that loans use the lower of the interest rate at approval or closing, and it describes the repayment period, generally limiting it to no more than 33 years and tying it to the expected useful life of the property.

nodes.title: § 3550.64 Down payment.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers key loan and housing assistance requirements under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains the market value limitation for Rural Housing Service loans, including different percentages for existing housing and new dwellings depending on the availability of construction quality documentation, as well as situations where the limit can be increased for closing costs, repair loans, or refinancing. It also describes down payment rules, requiring elderly and non-elderly families to apply excess net family assets above specified thresholds toward the down payment. In addition, it states that loans use the applicable RHS interest rate in effect at approval or closing, whichever is lower, and introduces the repayment period rules, indicating that loans must be repaid over a period not exceeding the property’s expected useful life, with a general maximum of 33 years unless otherwise provided.

nodes.title: § 3550.65 [Reserved]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers key loan terms and requirements under 7 CFR Part 3550, including: the market value limitation for existing and new housing and when that limit may be increased for certain subsequent loans or refinancing; down payment rules requiring elderly and non-elderly families to apply excess net family assets above specified thresholds; the interest rate rule stating loans use the lower of the RHS rate at approval or closing; and the repayment period, which must not exceed the dwelling’s expected useful life and is generally capped at 33 years except for specified exceptions.

nodes.title: § 3550.66 Interest rate.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers loan and property financing rules under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains the market value limitation for loans on existing and new dwellings, including when higher limits may apply for closing costs, repairs, or refinancing. It also describes down payment requirements, requiring elderly and non-elderly families to apply net family assets above certain thresholds toward the purchase. In addition, it states that loans use the lower of the interest rate at approval or closing, and it introduces repayment period rules, noting that loan terms cannot exceed the expected useful life of the dwelling and are generally limited to 33 years unless otherwise provided.

nodes.title: § 3550.67 Repayment period.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers Rural Housing Service loan rules under 7 CFR Part 3550, focusing on loan terms and borrower assistance. It explains market value limits for existing and new dwellings, including when those limits can be increased for closing costs, repairs, or refinancing. It also describes down payment requirements, stating that elderly and non-elderly families must apply net family assets above certain thresholds toward the purchase. The document then outlines interest rate rules, using the lower of the rate at approval or closing, and repayment period limits, including standard 33-year terms and longer terms in specific cases such as low-income borrowers, small loans, and manufactured homes. Finally, it discusses payment subsidies, including the types available, eligibility conditions, when subsidies may be granted for initial or subsequent loans, and how subsidy type is determined for borrowers who already receive assistance.

nodes.title: § 3550.67 (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers loan term limits and payment subsidies under USDA/RHS housing regulations. It explains that 38-year loan terms are allowed for certain borrowers with incomes at or below 60% of area median income when a longer term is needed to demonstrate repayment ability, with special rules for subsequent loans and assumptions. It also states that 10-year terms apply to loans not exceeding $2,500 and 30-year terms apply to manufactured homes.  

The section on payment subsidies describes three subsidy types: interest credit, payment assistance method 1, and payment assistance method 2. It explains when borrowers are eligible for subsidies, including occupancy and income requirements, and notes that subsidies can be recaptured if the borrower transfers title or no longer occupies the property. It also outlines how the subsidy type is determined for continuing borrowers and for borrowers receiving subsequent loans.

nodes.title: § 3550.68 Payment subsidies.
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains loan term limits and payment subsidy rules under 7 CFR Part 3550. It covers:

- When 38-year loan terms are allowed, including for very low-income applicants and certain subsequent loans.
- Shorter fixed terms for small loans and manufactured homes.
- RHS payment subsidy programs, including interest credit, payment assistance method 1, and payment assistance method 2.
- Eligibility requirements for receiving subsidies, such as occupancy of the property, income limits, and minimum loan term lengths.
- Rules for which subsidy type a borrower receives when a loan is assumed or refinanced.
- How payment assistance is calculated, including limits based on 1 percent amortization.
- Detailed formulas and factors used in payment assistance method 1 and method 2, such as adjusted income, floor payments, taxes, insurance, and equivalent interest rates.

nodes.title: § 3550.68 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains how payment assistance is determined for borrowers under 7 CFR Part 3550, § 3550.68. It states when a borrower must receive Payment Assistance Method 2, including cases where the borrower has never received subsidy or resumes eligibility after a break, and notes that borrowers cannot choose between assistance methods. It then describes how payment assistance is calculated under both Method 2 and Method 1, emphasizing that assistance cannot exceed the amount needed to amortize the loan at a 1 percent interest rate. The section gives formulas for Method 2 based on borrower income, loan installments, taxes, insurance, and adjusted income, and for Method 1 based on the difference between the promissory note payment and either an equivalent interest-rate payment or a required floor payment. It also defines floor payment percentages tied to income categories and includes a chart showing how equivalent interest rates are assigned according to the borrower’s adjusted income as a percentage of area median income.

nodes.title: § 3550.68 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules in 7 CFR Part 3550 about payment assistance and deferred mortgage payments. It includes a table that links a borrower’s adjusted income as a percentage of area median income to an equivalent interest rate, with a note that the rate cannot be below 1 percent or below the note rate, whichever is less. It then explains how interest credit is calculated as the difference between the note-rate payment and either 20 percent of adjusted income minus taxes and insurance, or the payment at 1 percent amortization, whichever is greater. The section also requires annual income review and borrower reporting of changes in employment, household composition, or income increases of 10 percent or more. Finally, it describes deferred mortgage payments, including that eligible borrowers may defer up to 25 percent of monthly principal and interest at 1 percent for up to 15 years, subject to annual review and recapture, and lists the main eligibility requirements related to income limits, loan term, and payment burden thresholds.

nodes.title: § 3550.68 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers USDA Rural Housing Service payment assistance rules, focusing on how interest credit is calculated, how borrowers’ income must be reviewed annually, and how borrowers must report changes in household income or composition. It also explains deferred mortgage payments, including that RHS may defer up to 25% of monthly principal and interest at 1% for up to 15 years, with annual review and recapture conditions. Finally, it lists eligibility requirements for deferred mortgage payments, such as very low-income limits, loan term limits, and payment thresholds based on repayment income or adjusted income.

nodes.title: § 3550.69 Deferred mortgage payments.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules on borrower payment assistance and property commitments. It explains how interest credit is calculated, how borrowers’ income and household changes must be reviewed annually, and how deferred mortgage payments work, including eligibility, amount, duration, and recapture conditions. It also describes conditional commitments, which are RHS determinations that a dwelling will be acceptable for purchase if built or rehabilitated to approved standards and priced within limits, and outlines basic eligibility requirements for builders, dealers, contractors, or sellers requesting such commitments.

nodes.title: § 3550.69 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for deferred mortgage assistance and conditional commitments under 7 CFR Part 3550. It describes how deferred mortgage payments are calculated, how long deferral agreements remain in effect, and the annual review process for a borrower’s income, taxes, and insurance. It also outlines conditional commitments, explaining that RHS may determine a dwelling will be acceptable for purchase if it meets approved plans, specifications, regulations, and price limits, while clarifying that such commitments do not reserve funds or guarantee financing. Finally, it begins listing eligibility requirements for builders, dealers, contractors, or sellers seeking a conditional commitment.

nodes.title: § 3550.70 Conditional commitments.
nodes.summary:
The partial document explains rules for deferred mortgage assistance and conditional commitments under 7 CFR Part 3550. It covers how the deferred payment amount is calculated, how long deferred assistance can last, and the requirement for annual income and expense reviews, including borrower reporting duties for changes in household income or employment.

It also defines what a conditional commitment is and outlines the eligibility requirements for builders, dealers, contractors, or sellers to request one. The text describes limitations on when commitments may be issued, how long they remain valid, possible extensions or cancellations, and special rules when applications are packaged for a specific purchaser, including conditions tied to loan approval, construction timing, and final loan closing.

nodes.title: § 3550.70 (a) (Con.)
nodes.summary:
This excerpt from 7 CFR Part 3550 outlines rules for conditional commitments and condominium eligibility under the RHS loan program. It explains the qualifications a commitment applicant must meet, including competence, legal capacity, financial responsibility, and compliance with equal opportunity requirements. It also describes limitations on issuing commitments, the period of validity, possible extensions, and when commitments may be canceled.

The text covers special rules for commitments involving packaged applications, including conditions for sales agreements, construction start timing, and loan closing requirements. It details fee requirements, denial or cancellation when properties do not qualify, procedures for approving changes in plans or costs, and builder warranty obligations.

The second section, § 3550.71, specifies special requirements for condominium units, requiring that the project be approved by HUD, Fannie Mae, or Freddie Mac and that the condominium complies with applicable laws. It also protects RHS rights in cases of foreclosure, deed in lieu of foreclosure, and resale or leasing of acquired units.

nodes.title: § 3550.70 (Con.)
nodes.summary: This section explains requirements related to conditional commitments and condominium eligibility under 7 CFR Part 3550. It covers: payment and refund rules for conditional commitment fees; denial or cancellation of commitments when property or applicant requirements are not met; procedures for requesting approval of changes in plans, specifications, or commitment price; and builder warranty requirements.
