It also outlines special conditions for RHS loans on condominium units, including approval by HUD, Fannie Mae, or Freddie Mac, compliance with applicable condominium laws, and protections ensuring RHS can foreclose, accept deed in lieu, and sell or lease acquired condominium units.

nodes.title: § 3550.71 Special requirements for condominiums.
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for conditional commitments and special requirements for condominium loans. It explains that applicants must pay a conditional commitment fee, which may be refunded only if the commitment is not issued before appraisal work is completed. It states that applicants are notified if a commitment is denied and that commitments are canceled if the property fails to meet program requirements. The document also covers how changes to plans, specifications, or commitment price must be approved by RHS, including conditions under which such changes may be accepted. In addition, it requires builders or sellers to provide an approved builder’s warranty or a 10-year insured warranty.

The second section outlines condominium eligibility for RHS loans. A condominium unit must be in a project approved or accepted by HUD, Fannie Mae, or Freddie Mac, and the project must comply with applicable condominium laws. It also specifies that any right of first refusal in the condominium documents cannot interfere with RHS’s ability to foreclose, accept a deed in lieu of foreclosure, or sell or lease a unit acquired by RHS.

nodes.title: § 3550.71 (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules for financing condominiums, community land trusts, and manufactured homes under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains limits on RHS liability for unpaid condominium dues after foreclosure, restrictions on condominium associations in cases of condemnation or major loss, and requirements that taxes and insurance proceeds not override RHS mortgage rights. It also states conditions for condominium projects on leasehold land, such as a minimum percentage of units sold and limits on delinquent association dues.

The document then outlines rules for community land trusts, allowing eligible dwellings on trust-owned land to be financed if all program requirements are met and trust restrictions are approved by RHS and terminated upon foreclosure. Finally, it describes financing rules for manufactured homes, including eligible costs such as purchase, transport, set-up, site development, and certain subsequent loans, provided the mortgage covers both the unit and the site.

nodes.title: § 3550.71 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) loan eligibility requirements for condominium units, community land trusts, and manufactured homes under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains that condominium projects must meet sales and delinquency thresholds, including at least 70 percent of units sold and no more than 15 percent of owners more than one month delinquent on association dues. It then describes how dwellings on community land trust property may be financed if the loan meets subpart requirements and any land trust restrictions are reviewed by RHS and terminate upon foreclosure or deed in lieu. Finally, it outlines special rules for manufactured homes, noting that section 502 loans generally follow the same conditions as other loans, but may also finance eligible purchase, transportation, setup, site acquisition, site development, and certain subsequent repair or assumption-related loans when secured by a real estate mortgage covering both the unit and site.

nodes.title: § 3550.72 Community land trusts.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service regulations in 7 CFR Part 3550, focusing on eligibility and financing rules for certain homeownership situations. It explains condominium sale and delinquency requirements, including that at least 70 percent of units must be sold and that no more than 15 percent of owners may be more than one month delinquent on association dues at loan closing. It then describes financing for dwellings in community land trusts, requiring that land trust restrictions be reviewed and accepted by RHS and automatically terminated upon foreclosure or deed in lieu of foreclosure. Finally, it outlines rules for manufactured homes under section 502 loans, including eligible costs such as purchase, transportation, setup, site development, and certain subsequent loans related to REO properties or repairs.

nodes.title: § 3550.73 Manufactured homes.
nodes.summary:
This partial document explains several Rural Housing Service (RHS) loan eligibility and financing rules for specific property types under 7 CFR Part 3550.

Main points covered include:

- **Condominium unit requirements**: At least 70% of the units must be sold, and multiple unit purchases by one owner count as one sale. Also, no more than 15% of unit owners may be more than one month delinquent on homeowners association dues or assessments at loan closing.
- **Community land trusts**: Eligible dwellings on land owned by a community land trust may be financed if the loan meets all subpart requirements and any trust-imposed restrictions are reviewed by RHS before closing and automatically terminate upon foreclosure or a deed in lieu of foreclosure.
- **Manufactured homes**: The section details special rules for section 502 loans on manufactured homes, including:
  - Eligible costs RHS may finance, such as purchase, transport, setup, site development, and certain subsequent loans.
  - Prohibited uses of loan funds, such as financing standalone sites, remodeling at initial loan, or furniture.
  - Requirement that sales be made by an approved dealer-contractor.
  - Maximum loan term of 30 years.
  - Construction and development must comply with federal manufactured home standards.
  - Contract rules requiring a single construction contract and limiting multi-contract arrangements.
  - Lien release and title clearance requirements, including claimant releases and the manufacturer’s certificate of origin.

nodes.title: §3550.73 (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for manufactured home loans and nonprogram loans under 7 CFR Part 3550. It covers key loan restrictions, including what loan funds cannot be used for, such as existing units and sites in most cases, site-only purchases, initial alterations or remodeling, and furniture. It also explains that manufactured homes must be sold by approved dealer-contractors who provide full sales, service, and site development, and sets a maximum loan term of 30 years.

The document further describes construction and development requirements, stating that manufactured homes must comply with federal construction and safety standards and that mutual self-help or borrower construction is not allowed. It details contract requirements, including the need for a single construction contract covering both the unit and site work, rules for subcontracting, payment conditions, lien release procedures, and warranty obligations.

Finally, it introduces nonprogram loans (NP terms), which may be used when applicants or properties do not qualify for standard program credit but it is in the government’s interest. These loans can facilitate REO sales, loan assumptions, conversions of loans with unauthorized assistance, and continuation of loans when part of the property is transferred. It also sets loan term limits for NP loans based on occupancy status.

nodes.title: § 3550.73 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers warranty requirements for manufactured homes and the rules for nonprogram loans under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains that dealer-contractors must provide warranties identifying the unit by serial number, certify compliance with plans and specifications, confirm no hidden transportation damage, and ensure proper joining and sealing of multi-section homes, while also providing copies of manufacturer warranties. It then outlines when nonprogram loans may be used, such as to facilitate the sale of REO properties, allow assumption of existing program loans on new terms, convert loans with unauthorized assistance, or continue a loan when part of the security property is transferred. Finally, it states the term limits for these loans, including up to 30 years for owner-occupants and up to 10 years for other applicants, with possible extension to 20 years under certain sale-facilitating conditions.

nodes.title: § 3550.74 Nonprogram loans.
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers several loan and warranty-related provisions under 7 CFR Part 3550. It first explains warranty requirements for dealer-contractors of manufactured homes, including certification that the home meets plans and specifications, has no hidden transportation damage, and is properly joined and sealed, along with providing manufacturer warranties to the applicant.

It then describes “nonprogram loans” (NP loans), which may be used when applicants or properties do not qualify for regular program credit but extending credit is in the government’s interest. The section outlines the purposes of NP loans, such as facilitating sale of REO properties, assuming existing program loans, converting loans with unauthorized assistance, or continuing a loan when part of the security property is transferred. It also details loan terms, including maximum repayment periods, interest rates, and the fact that NP borrowers are not eligible for payment assistance or moratoriums.

Additional NP loan requirements are listed, including application fees, down payment rules, and restrictions on financing closing costs and certain fees. The document also notes reduced restrictions for NP loans, such as no requirement to prove inability to obtain other credit, no occupancy requirement, and no leasing restrictions. It further provides situations where certain fees and down payments may be waived.

Finally, the document introduces the certified loan application packaging process for section 502 loans, stating that applicants may choose to apply through this process, which involves Agency-certified loan application packagers and, in some cases, Agency-approved intermediaries.

nodes.title: § 3550.74 (b)(1) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers regulations for nonprogram (NP) loans under 7 CFR Part 3550, including special rules for retaining property while purchasing a new one with RHS credit, NP loan interest rates, and borrower ineligibility for payment assistance or moratoriums. It also outlines additional requirements such as application fees, down payment obligations, and prohibitions on financing certain closing-related costs. The text then describes reduced restrictions for NP applicants, such as not needing to prove inability to obtain other credit, not being required to occupy the property, and not being subject to leasing restrictions. It further notes that certain fees and down payments may be waived in specific loan conversion or continuation situations. Finally, it introduces the certified loan application packaging process for section 502 loans, explaining that applicants may choose to apply through this process, which involves certified loan packagers, their employers, and possibly Agency-approved intermediaries.

nodes.title: § 3550.75 Certified loan application packaging process.
nodes.summary: The partial document describes requirements and conditions for non-program (NP) loans under 7 CFR Part 3550, including loan terms, borrower eligibility, and special provisions. It explains that NP loans use the NP interest rate in effect at loan approval, borrowers are not eligible for payment assistance or a moratorium, and applicants may need to pay a nonrefundable application fee, make a down payment, and cannot finance certain closing-related costs. It also notes reduced restrictions for NP applicants, such as not needing to prove inability to obtain other credit, not being required to occupy the property, and not being subject to leasing restrictions. In some cases, certain fees and the down payment may be waived. The document then introduces the certified loan application packaging process for section 502 loans, explaining that applicants may optionally submit applications through this process and that it involves certified packagers, qualified employers, and possibly approved intermediaries.

nodes.title: § 3550.75 (Con.)
nodes.summary:
This excerpt outlines the process requirements for packaging Section 502 loan applications under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains the qualifications and responsibilities for three key roles: Agency-certified loan application packagers, qualified employers, and Agency-approved intermediaries.

Main points covered include:
- The eligibility criteria for an individual to become an Agency-certified loan application packager, such as experience in affordable housing lending or counseling, employment by a qualified employer, completion of an approved course and test, and possible submission through an intermediary.
- The requirements for a qualified employer, including being a nonprofit or public agency, maintaining tax-exempt status, handling conflicts of interest properly, preparing fair housing marketing plans, and complying with intermediary submission rules if required.
- The standards an organization must meet to be approved as an intermediary, including nonprofit or public agency status, statewide service capacity, financial viability, experience with Agency housing programs, qualified quality assurance staff, and effective quality control procedures.

Overall, the section sets the certification, organizational, and quality control framework needed to package loan applications for submission to the Agency.

nodes.title: § 3550.75 (b)(2) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements for Agency-approved intermediaries and loan application packaging courses under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains that intermediaries may be used to submit applications if required by a State Director, and sets out eligibility criteria for approval, including nonprofit or public agency status, experience in affordable housing and direct single-family housing programs, financial viability, quality control procedures, trained staff, and independence from financial interest in the property. It also describes required training courses for loan application packagers and intermediary quality assurance staff, covering the Section 502 direct loan program, civil rights and fair housing laws, application and eligibility rules, underwriting, payment subsidies, and the responsibilities of packagers and Agency staff.

nodes.title: § 3550.75 (b)(3) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains requirements for certified loan application packagers and intermediaries under the USDA housing loan program. It states that intermediaries must ensure quality assurance staff complete an Agency-approved packaging course, pass the test, have no financial interest in the property or transaction, and provide training and technical support to maintain quality and compliance. It also outlines the required content of Agency-approved training courses: for loan application packagers, the course must cover the Section 502 direct single-family housing loan program, related civil rights and lending laws, application and eligibility requirements, underwriting and payment subsidies, and the responsibilities of packagers and Agency staff. For intermediaries, the course must include these topics plus additional material on quality assurance, technical assistance, and training functions.

nodes.title: § 3550.75 (c) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for Agency-certified loan application packagers and intermediaries, including training requirements for non-Agency trainers, confidentiality obligations, annual review requirements to retain designation, and conditions under which designation may be revoked. It also includes an OMB control number notice and begins Subpart C on Section 504 origination and Section 306C Water and Waste Disposal grants, explaining program objectives, allowable uses of grant and loan funds, and eligibility requirements for repairing mobile or manufactured homes.

nodes.title: § 3550.100 OMB control number.
nodes.summary: This section of 7 CFR Part 3550 covers the paperwork approval information for the regulation and introduces Subpart C, which governs Section 504 origination and Section 306C Water and Waste Disposal grants. It explains the program’s objectives: providing loans and grants to help very low-income rural homeowner-occupants repair their homes. It also outlines what grant and loan funds may be used for, including removing health and safety hazards and making homes accessible for persons with disabilities. Finally, it specifies conditions under which mobile and manufactured homes are eligible for repair assistance, such as ownership, prior occupancy, and placement on a permanent foundation.

nodes.title: Subpart C - Section 504 Origination and Section 306C Water and Waste Disposal Grants
nodes.summary: This excerpt from 7 CFR Part 3550 covers OMB approval information and the beginning of Subpart C, which deals with Section 504 origination and Section 306C water and waste disposal grants. It explains the program’s objectives, focusing on helping very low-income rural homeowners repair their properties. It also outlines what grant and loan funds may be used for, including removing health and safety hazards, making homes accessible for people with disabilities, and general repairs under loan funds. In addition, it specifies eligibility rules for repairs to mobile or manufactured homes, such as ownership, prior occupancy, and placement on a permanent foundation.

nodes.title: § 3550.101 Program objectives.
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines USDA Rural Housing Service regulations in 7 CFR Part 3550, focusing on Section 504 origination and Section 306C Water and Waste Disposal grants. It begins with the OMB control number and explains the public reporting burden for the information collection requirements. The main substance describes the program objectives: Section 504 loans and grants are intended to help very low-income rural owner-occupants repair their homes, and certain water and waste disposal grants are also covered. It then defines how grant and loan funds may be used. Grant funds are restricted to removing health and safety hazards or making dwellings accessible for household members with disabilities, and any unused grant money must be returned. Loan funds may be used more broadly for general repairs and improvements, so long as the home remains modest in size and design. The document also explains when mobile or manufactured homes are eligible for repair assistance, requiring ownership of both the home and site, prior occupancy before applying, and placement on a permanent foundation or use of funds to create one.

nodes.title: § 3550.102 Grant and loan purposes.
nodes.summary:
The document excerpt outlines USDA Rural Housing Service regulations for Section 504 housing repair loans and grants and Section 306C water and waste disposal grants. It begins by noting the OMB approval and reporting burden for the information collection requirements. The main program objective is to help very low-income rural owner-occupants repair their homes, with added coverage for certain water and waste disposal grants.

It then explains how grant and loan funds may be used. Grant funds are limited to repairs or improvements that remove health and safety hazards or make homes accessible for people with disabilities, and any unused grant money must be returned. Loan funds may be used more broadly for general repairs and improvements, as long as the home remains modest in size and design. The text also allows repairs to mobile or manufactured homes if the applicant owns both the home and site, has occupied the home before applying, and the home is or will be on a permanent foundation.

The excerpt further lists eligible costs that may be covered, including loan or grant-related expenses such as legal, architectural, engineering, title, appraisal, surveying, environmental, tax monitoring, and closing fees; special design features or equipment for disabilities; utility connection and installation costs; certain real estate taxes and escrow-related expenses; and fees charged by nonprofit application-packaging organizations.

Finally, it specifies prohibited uses of Section 504 funds, such as constructing a new dwelling, repairing homes that would still have major hazards after repairs, moving a mobile or manufactured home to another site, paying for most off-site improvements, refinancing pre-existing debt, and paying fees to for-profit entities for loan packaging or applicant referrals.

nodes.title: § 3550.102 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains what Section 504 loan and grant funds may be used for, including repair and improvement costs, related application and closing expenses, accessibility modifications for disabilities, utility connection costs, tax and escrow-related costs, and application packaging fees for qualifying nonprofit organizations. It also lists prohibited uses, such as new construction, repairs that leave major hazards unresolved, moving mobile homes, most off-site improvements, refinancing prior debts, and fees to for-profit loan packagers or referral agents. The section then outlines applicant eligibility requirements: the applicant must own and occupy the dwelling, meet age requirements for grants, have income within very low-income limits, be a U.S. citizen or eligible non-citizen, lack other reasonable financing and sufficient personal resources, have legal capacity, not be suspended or debarred from federal programs, and, for loans, demonstrate repayment ability or use a cosigner or other household members to qualify.

nodes.title: § 3550.103 Eligibility requirements.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines eligibility requirements for USDA Rural Housing Service assistance under 7 CFR Part 3550, § 3550.103. It explains who can apply and the conditions they must meet, including owning and occupying the dwelling, age requirements for grants, income limits, U.S. citizenship or eligible alien status, need for assistance and use of personal resources, legal capacity, and compliance with suspension/debarment rules. It also covers loan-only requirements such as repayment ability and acceptable credit, including the option to add a cosigner or other household members if repayment ability is insufficient. Finally, it lists specific signs of unacceptable credit, such as recent delinquencies, foreclosure, tax liens, outstanding judgments or collections, certain debt settlements, and federal debt delinquency, while also noting bankruptcy situations that are not disqualifying under certain conditions.

nodes.title: § 3550.103 (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA RHS section 504 loan and grant eligibility rules, focusing on credit qualifications and the application process. It explains what constitutes acceptable credit, lists indicators of unacceptable credit such as delinquencies, foreclosures, tax liens, judgments, collection accounts, and certain debt settlements, and identifies exceptions like older bankruptcies or satisfied judgments. It also states that applicants rejected for credit reasons must be told why.

The document then describes how applications must be submitted in full, how incomplete applications are handled, how applicants can withdraw or be asked to reconfirm interest, and how eligibility determinations are communicated. Finally, it explains funding priorities when money is limited, giving priority to applications addressing health and safety hazards, then veterans preference where applicable, and otherwise processing requests on a first-come, first-served basis.

nodes.title: § 3550.103 (i)(2) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document describes regulations for section 504 loans and grants under 7 CFR Part 3550, focusing on credit-related eligibility, application submission, and processing procedures. It explains that certain prior adverse credit events may still be acceptable, including a non-foreclosure judgment satisfied more than 12 months before the application date, and that applicants denied due to unacceptable credit must be told the reason and source of the information. It then outlines how applications must be submitted in a complete written form specified by RHS, how incomplete applications are handled and returned, how applicants may withdraw or be asked to reconfirm interest, and how eligibility determinations are communicated. Finally, it sets funding priorities, giving preference to applications addressing health and safety hazards, with veterans preference used when priorities are otherwise equal, and states that funded requests are processed on a first-come, first-served basis.

nodes.title: § 3550.104 Applications.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines requirements and procedures for USDA Rural Housing Service Section 504 loans and grants. It covers credit-related rejection criteria, application submission and processing rules, notification of eligibility or ineligibility, and funding priorities when funds are limited. It also explains site requirements, including rural area eligibility and subdivision limits, dwelling standards such as modesty and post-repair safety conditions, and ownership requirements showing acceptable interest in the property through fee ownership or certain leasehold interests.

nodes.title: § 3550.105 Site requirements.
nodes.summary:
The document outlines key eligibility and property standards for Rural Housing Service assistance under 7 CFR Part 3550. It covers:

- **Site requirements**: Loans are limited to rural areas designated by RHS, and the site must not be large enough to subdivide into more than one lot under local zoning rules.
- **Dwelling requirements**: The home must be modest, not intended for income-producing use, have no in-ground pool, and stay within the applicable loan value limit. Repairs funded under section 504 do not have to meet all agency development standards, but major health or safety hazards cannot remain. Work must comply with local building codes, and certain equipment or materials must meet federal standards.
- **Ownership requirements**: Applicants must show an acceptable ownership interest, either through full fee ownership or a secure leasehold interest. Leasehold rules specify minimum remaining lease terms for loans and grants, with an exception for mutual help housing on Indian lands financed by HUD.

nodes.title: § 3550.106 Dwelling requirements.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines eligibility and property requirements for USDA RHS Section 504 assistance. It explains that loans are limited to rural areas designated by RHS, and the site must be large enough for only one dwelling under local zoning rules. It also sets standards for the dwelling itself: the home must be modest, not intended for income-producing use, have no in-ground pool, and fall within applicable market value limits. Repairs do not have to fully meet agency development standards, but the home cannot retain major health or safety hazards, and all work must comply with local construction codes and relevant standards for hazardous materials or equipment. Finally, it describes acceptable ownership interests, including full fee ownership with marketable title or a secure leasehold interest, with specific lease term requirements for loans and grants.

nodes.title: § 3550.107 Ownership requirements.
nodes.summary:
The partial document outlines Rural Housing Service (RHS) requirements for Section 504 housing assistance. It covers:
- **Site requirements**, including that loans are limited to rural areas and the property must not be large enough to subdivide into multiple sites under local zoning.
- **Dwelling requirements**, specifying that the home must be modest, not intended for income-producing use, not have an in-ground pool, and not exceed loan value limits; it also notes post-repair standards and compliance with local construction codes.
- **Ownership requirements**, explaining acceptable ownership interests such as full fee ownership, secure leaseholds, life estates, undivided interests, possessory rights, land purchase contracts, and alternative proof of ownership when standard documentation is unavailable.

nodes.title: § 3550.107 (Con.)
nodes.summary: This partial document explains Rural Housing Service rules for ownership, security, escrow, and insurance requirements for Section 504 loans and grants. It covers what types of property interests are acceptable for applicants, including life estates, undivided interests, possessory rights, land purchase contracts, and alternative proof of ownership when standard evidence is unavailable. It also describes when remainder or co-owner interests must sign mortgage or repayment documents, and when some interests may be excluded under limited conditions. The document then outlines security requirements for loans over $7,500, including mortgage or contract security, limits on total debt relative to property value, and exceptions to title clearance procedures for smaller or repair-only loans. Finally, it discusses escrow accounts for taxes and insurance, and hazard insurance obligations for borrowers with secured indebtedness above $15,000, including required coverage amounts and conditions.

nodes.title: § 3550.108 Security requirements (loans only).
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers several loan and property security requirements under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains acceptable evidence of ownership, including affidavits from community members and other instruments commonly accepted as proof. It then describes security requirements for section 504 loans, such as when property must be secured by a mortgage, leasehold interest, or land purchase contract, and notes that RHS does not require a first lien but does require total debts on the property to stay within its value, with limited exceptions. The document also discusses title clearance and legal services rules, including cases where those requirements do not apply.

In addition, it outlines escrow account provisions, allowing RHS to require borrowers to deposit funds for real estate taxes, hazard and flood insurance premiums, and related costs. Finally, it covers insurance requirements for borrowers with secured indebtedness above a certain amount, including maintaining hazard insurance, having RHS as loss payee, and insuring the dwelling and essential buildings at an appropriate coverage level.

nodes.title: § 3550.109 Escrow account (loans only).
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers requirements for proving ownership, securing loans, escrow accounts, and insurance for rural housing loans under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains acceptable evidence of ownership, including affidavits from community members and other ownership instruments. It then describes security requirements for loans, stating that indebtedness of $7,500 or more must be secured by a mortgage, leasehold interest, or land purchase contract, and outlines lien, title clearance, and legal services rules with some exceptions. The section also covers escrow accounts for paying taxes and insurance, and insurance requirements, including when borrowers must maintain hazard insurance, acceptable coverage amounts, and the inclusion of a loss payable clause protecting the government’s interest.

nodes.title: § 3550.110 Insurance (loans only).
nodes.summary: The partial document describes requirements for proving ownership, securing Section 504 loans, managing escrow accounts, and maintaining insurance on the property. It explains what evidence can establish ownership, when a mortgage or other security interest is required, and exceptions to title clearance procedures. It also covers RHS authority to require escrow deposits for taxes and insurance, borrower responsibilities for hazard insurance, coverage amounts, force-placed insurance if the borrower does not comply, flood insurance rules for properties in Special Flood Hazard Areas, and how insurance losses and remaining claim funds must be handled.

nodes.title: § 3550.110 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers Rural Housing Service requirements for insurance, loss handling, appraisals, and loan/grant limits under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains that borrowers must maintain required property insurance, including flood insurance in Special Flood Hazard Areas, and that RHS may force-place insurance if the borrower does not. It outlines what happens after property losses, including borrower responsibilities, supervised use of insurance proceeds, and RHS options if the borrower cannot afford repairs. It also states when appraisals are required, the standards appraisals must follow, and the maximum amounts allowed for section 504 loans and grants, including limits based on debt, property value, repayment ability, and lifetime grant totals.

nodes.title: § 3550.110 (d) (Con.)
nodes.summary: This portion of 7 CFR Part 3550 covers remaining section 504 repair-loan actions and rules. It explains what RHS may do if a borrower cannot afford needed repairs, including making a later repair loan, allowing subordinate or junior liens from another lender, making a protective advance, or accelerating the debt. It then sets out appraisal requirements for loans over $15,000 or when security adequacy must be verified, including optional appraisal fees and standards for appraisals. Finally, it establishes section 504 program limits: a $20,000 maximum total loan amount per borrower or dwelling, limits for transferees who assume loans, requirements that secured debt not exceed property value, a loan amount based on repayment ability at 1% over 20 years, and a $7,500 lifetime maximum grant amount with grants available only after the maximum loan amount has been considered.

nodes.title: § 3550.111 Appraisals (loans only).
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules for Section 504 repair assistance. It explains what actions RHS may take when a borrower cannot afford needed repairs, including making another loan, subordinating its lien, allowing a junior lien, making a protective advance, or accelerating the debt. It then describes appraisal requirements for secured loans, including when an appraisal is needed and how it must be performed. Finally, it sets limits on Section 504 assistance, including the maximum outstanding loan amount per borrower or dwelling, limits for transferees who assumed a loan, restrictions based on the property’s value and the borrower’s repayment ability, and the lifetime maximum grant amount.

nodes.title: § 3550.112 Maximum loan and grant.
nodes.summary: This partial document outlines USDA Rural Housing Service rules for Section 504 loans and grants and Water and Waste Disposal (WWD) individual grants. It covers what actions RHS may take if a borrower cannot afford repairs, when appraisals are required, maximum limits for loans and grants, interest rate and repayment term requirements, and repayment obligations for grants if the property is sold within three years. It also describes the purpose of the WWD grant program for colonia residents near the U.S.-Mexico border and provides key definitions for terms such as colonia, individual, rural areas, system, and WWD.

nodes.title: § 3550.113 Rates and terms (loans only).
nodes.summary: This section covers the rules for Section 504 loans and grants, including loan interest rates, repayment terms, and grant repayment requirements if a property is sold within three years. It also explains the purpose of the WWD individual grant program, which is to help residents of colonias along the U.S.-Mexico border access community water and waste disposal systems. In addition, it defines key terms used for WWD grants, such as colonia, individual, rural areas, system, and WWD.

nodes.title: § 3550.114 Repayment agreement (grants only).
nodes.summary: This section covers the rules for Section 504 loans and grants, including the fixed 1% interest rate for loans, loan repayment terms, and the requirement for grant recipients to repay the grant if the property is sold within three years. It also explains the purpose of the WWD grant program, which helps residents of border colonias access community water and waste disposal systems, and provides key definitions for terms such as colonia, individual, rural areas, system, and WWD.

nodes.title: § 3550.115 WWD grant program objectives.
nodes.summary: This part of 7 CFR Part 3550 covers the rules for Section 504 loans and grants, including interest rate, repayment terms, and grant repayment requirements. It explains that Section 504 loans have a 1 percent interest rate, with repayment periods generally as short as possible but no longer than 20 years, except combined loan-grant packages which must have a 20-year term. It also states that grant recipients must repay the full grant if the property is sold within three years. In addition, it describes the WWD individual grant program, which supports use of community water and waste disposal systems for colonias along the U.S.-Mexico border, and provides key definitions for terms such as colonia, individual, rural area, system, and WWD.

nodes.title: § 3550.116 Definitions applicable to WWD grants only.
nodes.summary: The document section covers rules for USDA Section 504 loans and grants, including interest rate and repayment term limits, plus grant repayment if property is sold within 3 years. It then outlines the Water and Waste Disposal (WWD) individual grant program for colonias, defining key terms such as colonia, individual, rural area, system, and WWD. The section also lists allowed uses of WWD grant funds, such as extending service lines, connecting plumbing, installing basic plumbing fixtures, closing abandoned septic tanks or wells, and making dwelling improvements needed for system use. It specifies grant restrictions, including a $5,000 lifetime maximum and prohibitions on using funds to pay unrelated debts or the applicant’s own labor. Finally, it notes a WWD eligibility requirement that applicants do not need to be 62 or older.

nodes.title: § 3550.117 WWD grant purposes.
nodes.summary: This part of the document explains the rules for Water and Waste Disposal (WWD) grants under 7 CFR Part 3550. It lists the specific uses of grant funds, including extending and connecting service lines, paying connection fees, installing plumbing and fixtures in homes that lack them, building or partitioning space for a bathroom, closing abandoned septic tanks and water wells when required for safety and law, and making residence improvements needed to use water or waste disposal systems. It also describes grant restrictions, including a $5,000 lifetime maximum per individual and prohibitions on using funds to pay unrelated debts or the applicant’s own labor. Finally, it states an eligibility requirement that applicants do not have to be 62 years of age or older.

nodes.title: § 3550.118 Grant restrictions.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for USDA Water and Waste Disposal (WWD) grants under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains what grant funds may be used for, including extending service lines to a home, connecting lines to plumbing, paying connection fees, installing basic plumbing fixtures, building or partitioning a small bathroom, closing abandoned septic tanks or wells when required for safety and law, and making home improvements needed to use water or waste disposal systems. It also sets grant restrictions, including a lifetime maximum of $5,000 per individual and prohibitions on using funds to pay unrelated debts or the applicant’s own labor. Finally, it states an eligibility requirement that WWD applicants do not need to be 62 years of age or older.

nodes.title: § 3550.119 WWD eligibility requirements.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers Rural Housing Service regulations for Water and Waste Disposal (WWD) grants and related servicing rules. It explains what grant funds may be used for, including extending and connecting service lines, installing basic plumbing and bathroom facilities, closing abandoned septic tanks and wells when required for health and safety, and making residence improvements needed to use the water or waste disposal system. It also outlines grant restrictions, such as a $5,000 lifetime maximum and prohibitions on using funds for unrelated debts or the recipient’s own labor. The document then lists WWD eligibility requirements, including owning and occupying a dwelling in a colonia, having household income below federal poverty guidelines, not being delinquent on federal debt, and verifying income through employment, benefits, and tax returns. Finally, it notes the OMB control number for the information collection and begins Subpart D on regular servicing, which sets policies for managing repayment of loans under sections 502 and 504 of the Housing Act of 1949.

nodes.title: § 3550.119 (Con.)
nodes.summary: This excerpt covers eligibility requirements for certain assistance, including ownership and occupancy of a dwelling in a colonia, income limits based on federal poverty guidelines, the requirement that the household not be delinquent on any Federal debt, and income verification through employment/benefit records and tax returns. It also notes an OMB control number for the regulation’s information collection. Finally, it begins Subpart D on Regular Servicing, stating that the Rural Housing Service policies govern repayment management for loans under sections 502 and 504 of the Housing Act of 1949.

nodes.title: §§ 3550.120 - 3550.149 [Reserved]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers eligibility and verification requirements for certain housing assistance, including ownership and occupancy of a dwelling in a colonia, income limits based on poverty guidelines, prohibition on delinquent Federal debt, and income verification through employment/benefit records and tax returns. It also notes the OMB approval and control number for the information collection requirements, then begins Subpart D on regular servicing, stating that the subpart sets Rural Housing Service policies for managing repayment of loans made under sections 502 and 504 of the Housing Act of 1949.

nodes.title: § 3550.150 OMB control number.
nodes.summary: The document excerpt outlines eligibility and verification requirements for certain housing assistance in colonias, including ownership and occupancy of a dwelling, income limits below federal poverty guidelines, and being free of delinquent federal debt. It also explains that household income must be verified through employment, benefits, and tax return records. In addition, it includes the OMB control number and a notice about public reporting burden for the information collection. The excerpt then begins Subpart D, which covers regular servicing policies for managing repayment of Rural Housing Service loans under sections 502 and 504 of the Housing Act of 1949.

nodes.title: Subpart D - Regular Servicing
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines eligibility and verification requirements for assistance related to housing in a colonia, including owning and occupying the dwelling, having household income below federal poverty guidelines, not being delinquent on federal debt, and verifying income through employment/benefits records and tax returns. It also notes an OMB control number for the regulation’s information collection requirements, followed by the start of Subpart D on regular servicing, which sets Rural Housing Service policies for managing repayment of loans under sections 502 and 504 of the Housing Act of 1949.

nodes.title: § 3550.151 Servicing goals.
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) loan eligibility and servicing rules. It explains requirements for households in colonias, including ownership and occupancy of the dwelling, income below federal poverty guidelines, no delinquent federal debt, and income verification through employment/benefit records and tax returns. It also notes an OMB control number for the information collection requirements.

The document then begins Subpart D, “Regular Servicing,” which outlines RHS policies for managing repayment of loans under sections 502 and 504 of the Housing Act of 1949. It describes loan payment rules, including monthly payment expectations, conversion from annual to monthly payments, acceptable payment methods, how partial and excess payments are handled, and how payments are applied across multiple loans. Finally, it addresses fees that RHS may charge and inspection authority over property securing RHS loans, including notice requirements before inspections.

nodes.title: § 3550.152 Loan payments.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for RHS loan payments, fees, and inspections under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains that loan repayments are generally monthly unless the loan documents specify otherwise, with borrowers on annual schedules able to request conversion to monthly payments. It describes acceptable payment methods and fees for cash payments converted to money orders. It also outlines how payments are applied: insufficient payments are held in suspense, then applied in a specific order to protective advances, interest, principal, and escrow once enough is received. For borrowers with multiple loans on the same property, payments are applied to the oldest loan first. Excess payments may be directed to principal if no fees are outstanding. The section on fees states that RHS may charge reasonable fees such as tax service fees, late fees, and returned check fees. The inspection section states that RHS or its agent may inspect secured property as needed to protect the government’s interest, with notice given to the borrower at or before the inspection.

nodes.title: § 3550.153 Fees.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for RHS loan payment processing, fees, and property inspections. It explains that borrowers generally must make monthly payments, how payments are applied when partial or multiple loan payments are received, and that excess payments may be directed to principal if no fees are outstanding. It also notes that RHS may charge reasonable fees such as tax service, late payment, and returned check fees. Finally, it states that RHS or its agent may inspect property securing the loan to protect the government’s interest, with notice given to the borrower at or before inspection.

nodes.title: § 3550.154 Inspections.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines rules for RHS loan servicing, including how borrowers must make payments, how payments are applied when amounts are insufficient, how multiple loans and extra payments are handled, and what fees may be charged. It also covers RHS inspection rights over secured property, the administration and requirements of escrow accounts, borrower responsibilities for maintaining the property and related expenses, and the process for reviewing and renewing payment subsidy assistance, including occupancy requirements and effective dates when renewals are delayed.

nodes.title: § 3550.155 Escrow account.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules on escrow accounts, borrower responsibilities, and payment subsidies. It explains when escrow accounts may be required or elected, including requirements to fund taxes, insurance, and reserves, and notes that escrow may be required during special servicing actions. It also outlines borrower obligations after receiving an RHS loan, such as maintaining the property and keeping escrow or paying taxes and insurance on time, with RHS able to charge borrowers for services if they fail to comply. Finally, it describes the annual review and renewal process for borrowers receiving payment subsidy, including the requirement that the borrower still occupy the property and how renewal effective dates are determined if there is a delay.

nodes.title: § 3550.156 Borrower obligations.
nodes.summary: This section covers key borrower account and assistance requirements under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains how escrow accounts are handled, including when RHS may require initial deposits, voluntary escrow by borrowers, and escrow requirements during special servicing actions. It outlines borrower obligations after receiving an RHS loan, such as maintaining the property and keeping escrow funds current or paying taxes and insurance directly, and states that RHS may arrange needed services and charge the borrower if obligations are not met. It also describes payment subsidy rules, including annual review of continued eligibility, the requirement that the borrower occupy the property, and how the effective date is determined if subsidy renewal is delayed.

nodes.title: § 3550.157 Payment subsidy.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for borrower escrow accounts, borrower responsibilities, payment subsidy assistance, and protections for borrowers on active military duty. It explains when RHS may require or allow escrow funding for taxes and insurance, the borrower’s duty to maintain the property and pay related expenses, and RHS’s ability to step in and charge the borrower if obligations are not met. It also describes how payment subsidies are reviewed, renewed, adjusted, or canceled based on occupancy, income changes, and household circumstances. Finally, it covers the special interest-rate protection for borrowers entering full-time active military duty, including subsidy calculations at the reduced rate and the borrower’s duty to notify RHS when military status ends.

nodes.title: § 3550.157 (a) (Con.)
nodes.summary: This excerpt explains rules for Rural Housing Service (RHS) payment subsidy and military-duty interest protections. It covers when borrowers must report changes in employment, income, or household composition, and how a subsidy may be adjusted if income changes significantly. It also describes eligibility for borrowers who were not originally receiving payment subsidy, including income limits, occupancy requirements, and loan term conditions. In addition, it states when RHS will cancel payment subsidy, such as if the borrower no longer occupies the property or transfers title. Finally, it outlines protections for borrowers entering full-time active military duty, including a capped 6 percent interest rate, how subsidy is calculated during service, and the requirement to notify RHS when active duty ends.

nodes.title: § 3550.158 Active military duty.
nodes.summary:
This section explains the rules for Rural Housing Service (RHS) payment subsidy assistance and related borrower obligations. It covers when borrowers must report changes in employment, household composition, or income, and how subsidy amounts may be adjusted based on income changes. It also describes eligibility for payment assistance for borrowers not currently receiving subsidy, including income limits, occupancy requirements, and loan term conditions. In addition, it states when RHS must cancel payment subsidy, such as when the borrower no longer occupies the property or transfers title.

The document also outlines special protections for borrowers entering full-time active military duty. Under the Soldiers and Sailors Relief Act, the interest rate may be reduced to six percent, with payment subsidy adjusted to cover the difference between the subsidized rate and the six percent rate. It explains when this reduced rate takes effect and requires borrowers to notify RHS when active military duty ends so the original note rate can resume, with possible reinstatement of subsidy if the borrower remains eligible.

nodes.title: § 3550.159 Borrower actions requiring RHS approval.
nodes.summary: The partial document describes borrower actions that require RHS approval under 7 CFR Part 3550. It covers three main areas: mineral leases, subordination of RHS interests, and partial release of security. For mineral leases, borrowers must get authorization, and RHS may approve if the property remains a suitable residence and the government’s security is protected, with special conditions if the lease could reduce property value. For subordination, RHS may allow its interests to be secondary to facilitate refinancing or additional private credit, provided the transaction is in the government’s best interest and various safeguards are met, including limits on total debt. For partial release of security, RHS may approve sales, exchanges, or easements if compensation is adequate, appraisals are current when needed, the remaining property remains acceptable housing, repayment is not jeopardized, environmental requirements are satisfied, and title issues are cleared when exchanges occur.

nodes.title: § 3550.159 (b) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules on handling security for loans and refinancing. It explains when RHS may subordinate its lien and limits on total debt based on whether the borrower is paying off the RHS loan. It also describes conditions for partial release of security property, including acceptable compensation, appraisal requirements, environmental compliance, title clearance, and how sale or transaction proceeds must be applied. In addition, it addresses leasing of security property and the effect of leases on loan servicing benefits. Finally, it outlines RHS requirements for refinancing with private credit, stating that borrowers must refinance when they qualify for reasonably available private credit and that RHS may review a borrower’s financial status to determine eligibility.

nodes.title: § 3550.159 (c) (Con.)
nodes.summary:
This section explains how proceeds from selling part of the security property, granting easements or rights-of-way, or receiving damage compensation must be used, in a specific order: first for borrower transaction costs, then for any prior lien debt, and then for RHS debt or approved improvements to the property. It also states that proposed development funded by such proceeds must follow applicable RHS planning and supervision rules.

The section further covers leasing the security property, requiring the borrower to notify RHS. If a lease lasts more than three years or includes an option to purchase, RHS may liquidate the loan, and the borrower loses eligibility for payment subsidies or special servicing benefits during the lease period.

Finally, it introduces refinancing with private credit, explaining that RHS direct loan programs are not meant to replace private lenders. Borrowers must refinance with private credit when RHS determines they can obtain reasonable alternative financing based on income, assets, and credit history. It also notes that RHS may periodically or at any time review a borrower’s financial status to determine whether refinancing is required.

nodes.title: § 3550.160 Refinancing with private credit.
nodes.summary:
This excerpt from 7 CFR Part 3550 covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules on handling proceeds from transactions involving security property, leasing security property, and refinancing RHS loans with private credit. It explains the order in which proceeds from sales, easements, damage compensation, and similar transactions must be used: first for borrower-paid transaction costs, then for prior lien debt, and then for RHS debt or approved improvements to the property. It also states that borrowers must notify RHS if they lease the property, and that long-term leases or leases with purchase options may lead RHS to liquidate the loan and can make the borrower ineligible for payment subsidies or special servicing benefits.

The second major topic is refinancing with private credit. The regulations state that RHS loans are not meant to replace private lending, so borrowers must refinance with private credit when RHS determines they qualify based on income, assets, and credit history. The text describes RHS review procedures, including required financial disclosures, the 90-day refinancing deadline if private credit is available, the borrower’s right to contest the decision, and the documentation needed if the borrower claims private credit is unavailable. It also notes that failure to provide the required information or demonstrate inability to obtain private credit may result in foreclosure. Finally, it mentions that RHS may subordinate its deferred recapture amount to help a borrower refinance, including certain associated closing costs.

nodes.title: § 3550.160 (c) (Con.)
nodes.summary:
This section describes the process for borrowers being reviewed by RHS to determine whether they can refinance with private credit. It explains that borrowers must provide financial information within 30 days, may face foreclosure if they do not respond, and must refinance within 90 days if RHS determines they are able to do so. Borrowers can contest this decision by submitting additional financial evidence within 30 days.

It also outlines what borrowers must submit if they claim they cannot obtain private credit, including details about lenders contacted, loan amounts requested and offered, terms and rates, and information given to lenders. If RHS finds the evidence insufficient or the borrower fails to provide it, foreclosure may begin.

Finally, the section states that RHS may subordinate its interest in a deferred recapture amount to allow refinancing, including amounts needed to repay RHS debt and reasonable closing costs.

nodes.title: § 3550.160 (e) (Con.)
nodes.summary: This excerpt from 7 CFR Part 3550 covers several mortgage servicing and loan closure topics under RHS rules. It explains allowable costs that can be financed into a loan, including loan servicing costs and necessary repairs or improvements to the security property. It then addresses when a borrower seeking additional credit will be considered, especially if private refinancing is involved. The section on final payment details what must be repaid to satisfy the account, how security instruments may be released, the availability and possible fee for payoff statements, acceptable payment methods, and who is responsible for recording costs. Finally, it begins the recapture rules, stating that certain borrowers must repay subsidy benefits when they transfer title or stop occupying the property, including in foreclosure or deed-in-lieu situations.

nodes.title: § 3550.161 Final payment.
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers rules for USDA Rural Housing Service loan servicing and closing matters. It explains what expenses may be included in a loan, when a borrower seeking refinancing with private credit can be considered for additional credit, and how a loan is fully paid off. It also describes release of security instruments, borrower-requested payoff statements, acceptable payment methods, and who pays recording costs for releasing a mortgage. Finally, it outlines recapture requirements, stating that certain borrowers must repay subsidy amounts when they transfer title or stop occupying the property, including foreclosure-related situations.

nodes.title: § 3550.162 Recapture.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service rules on loan servicing, final payment, and subsidy recapture for housing loans. It explains what expenses may be included in refinancing or additional credit requests, how full payment of a borrower’s account is calculated, when security instruments may be released, and how payoff statements and payment methods are handled. It also sets out borrower responsibility for recording costs and describes recapture requirements for subsidy amounts when a borrower transfers title or stops occupying the property.

nodes.title: § 3550.162 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt explains how loan subsidy recapture is calculated and collected under USDA housing rules. It covers the general amount to be recaptured, including principal reduction attributed to subsidy and, in some cases, subsidy plus a portion of property value appreciation. It also describes special rules for foreclosure and deed-in-lieu situations, where only the subsidy received is recaptured from property proceeds and the proceeds are applied in a specific order. Additional provisions address how to value appreciation for cross-collateralized loans, exclude SCRA interest reductions from recapture, allow deferral of recapture when a borrower refinances and continues occupying the home, and outline how recapture is handled when loans are assumed under different terms.

nodes.title: § 3550.162 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document covers rules for recapture of subsidy and transfer of loans under USDA/RHS housing programs. It explains when recapture payments may be deferred if a borrower refinances or pays off the loan but stays in the property, how deferred recapture is handled if the agency mortgage is subordinated, and how recapture is treated in loan assumptions under different terms. It then outlines the policy for transfer of security and assumption of indebtedness, including the general requirement for RHS approval due to due-on-sale clauses, what happens when a transfer is authorized or unauthorized, and exceptions where due-on-sale clauses are not triggered, such as certain transfers to family members or survivors after death.

nodes.title: § 3550.163 Transfer of security and assumption of indebtedness.
nodes.summary:
This section explains the rules for transferring RHS-secured property and assuming the related debt. It states that RHS mortgages generally include due-on-sale clauses, so RHS approval is usually required before title can be transferred with an assumption of the loan. RHS may approve assumptions on program or nonprogram terms depending on the transferee’s eligibility and the property’s status. It also describes what happens if a transfer occurs without authorization, including possible liquidation of the loan unless RHS decides it is in the government’s best interest to continue servicing it in the original borrower’s name.

The section then lists exceptions where due-on-sale clauses are not triggered, such as transfers to a spouse or children, transfers due to death, divorce-related transfers, certain transfers for dependents of a deceased borrower, and transfers into an inter vivos trust without changing occupancy rights. For these exempt transfers, the transferee is not required to assume the loan but may choose to do so under existing or new terms, depending on eligibility. It also notes that later title transfers are generally treated as sales.

Finally, the section outlines assumption requirements, including when loans may be assumed on program or nonprogram terms and how the assumption amount is determined, generally based on the lesser of the indebtedness or the current market value minus liens and any required down payment.

nodes.title: § 3550.163 (c)(1) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt outlines rules for transferring and assuming Rural Housing Service (RHS) loans when property ownership changes. It covers permissible transfers without triggering loan liquidation, including transfers to a spouse or ex-spouse through divorce or separation, transfers to certain dependents after a borrower’s death, and transfers into an inter vivos trust without giving up occupancy rights. It explains that transferees in these cases generally do not have to assume the loan if payments continue, but they may choose to assume it either under the original note terms or, if eligible, under new program terms. It also notes that later transfers of title are usually treated as sales. Finally, it describes assumption requirements, including when loans may be assumed on program or non-program terms and how the assumed amount is calculated based on market value, liens, down payment, or existing indebtedness.

nodes.title: § 3550.163 (d) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The passage explains rules for assuming Rural Housing Service (RHS) loans, including how interest rates are set depending on whether the assumption is on program or nonprogram terms, when additional financing or appraisals may be required, and how liability is released for borrowers and cosigners when a debt is assumed. It also covers cases where RHS may deny assumptions if other unsecured loans remain unpaid, how excess property is handled when a secured property is larger than a minimum adequate site, and how remaining debt may be restructured. The section then begins § 3550.164 on unauthorized assistance, defining it and introducing rules about assistance obtained through false information.

nodes.title: § 3550.164 Unauthorized assistance.
nodes.summary: The partial document describes Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules for loan assumptions and handling unauthorized assistance. It explains how interest rates are set when a loan is assumed, when additional financing or appraisal fees may apply, and how liability is released or remaining debt is handled, including special rules for excess property and unsecured loans. It then defines unauthorized assistance and distinguishes between cases caused by false information and inaccurate information. For each, it outlines the required remedies, such as account adjustments, repayment deadlines, reamortization, loan conversion to nonprogram terms, and conditions under which a loan may continue.

nodes.title: § 3550.164 (b) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The partial document explains rules for handling unauthorized RHS loans, subsidies, and grants when they result from false or inaccurate information. It covers how RHS adjusts accounts, requires repayment, may convert or continue loans under certain eligibility conditions, and when reamortization or acceleration is allowed. It also describes repayment options for unauthorized grants, including promissory notes or lump-sum repayment, and notes RHS may seek legal judgment or the best available mortgage if the assistance is unsecured. The document then includes the OMB control number for the information collection requirements and begins Subpart E on special servicing, stating that RHS may use servicing actions such as payment assistance, delinquency workout agreements, protective advances, and payment moratoriums to help borrowers avoid loan liquidation.

nodes.title: § 3550.164 (c) (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers handling of unauthorized grants and loans, including repayment options, account adjustments, loan acceleration, reamortization, and remedies when no security exists. It also includes the OMB control number and reporting burden statement for the regulation. Finally, it introduces Subpart E on special servicing, explaining that RHS may use servicing actions such as payment assistance, delinquency workout agreements, protective advances, and payment moratoriums to help borrowers avoid liquidation.

nodes.title: §§ 3550.165 - 3550.199 [Reserved]
nodes.summary: The partial document covers Rural Housing Service (RHS) rules on handling unauthorized assistance, account servicing, and special servicing actions under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains that recipients of unauthorized grants or loans may repay in full or through a promissory note, and RHS may pursue judgment or adjust accounts retroactively, accelerate loans if repayment is not made, or reamortize loans in some cases. It also notes that for unsecured unauthorized loans or grants, RHS may seek the best mortgage available. The document then provides the OMB control number for the regulation’s information collection requirements. Finally, it introduces Subpart E on Special Servicing, stating that RHS may use servicing options such as payment assistance, delinquency workout agreements, protective advances, and payment moratoriums to help borrowers avoid loan liquidation.

nodes.title: § 3550.200 OMB control number.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines USDA Rural Housing Service regulations on handling unauthorized grants and loans, including repayment options, retroactive account adjustments, loan acceleration, reamortization in some cases, and actions for unsecured unauthorized assistance. It also includes the OMB control number notice for the information collection requirements. In addition, it begins Subpart E on Special Servicing, explaining that RHS may offer servicing actions such as payment assistance, delinquency workout agreements, protective advances, and payment moratoriums to help borrowers avoid loan liquidation.

nodes.title: Subpart E - Special Servicing
nodes.summary: The partial document covers regulations under 7 CFR Part 3550 related to unauthorized assistance, account servicing, OMB reporting requirements, and special servicing actions. It explains that recipients of unauthorized grants or loans may repay in a lump sum or through a promissory note, and RHS can take further action such as seeking judgment, accelerating loans, or reamortizing them in some cases. It also states that RHS will retroactively adjust accounts to determine unauthorized assistance and may seek the best mortgage if the loan or grant is unsecured. In addition, the document notes the OMB control number for the information collection requirements. Finally, it introduces Subpart E on special servicing, explaining that RHS may offer servicing options like payment assistance, delinquency workout agreements, protective advances, and payment moratoriums to help borrowers avoid liquidation.

nodes.title: § 3550.201 Purpose of special servicing actions.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service regulations on handling unauthorized assistance, account servicing, and special servicing actions for rural housing loans and grants. It explains options for recipients of unauthorized grants or loans, including repayment in a lump sum or by promissory note, how RHS may adjust accounts, accelerate loans, or seek judgments or mortgages when repayment is not made, and notes OMB reporting requirements. It then outlines special servicing purposes and options such as payment assistance, delinquency workout agreements, protective advances, and payment moratoriums. The text also defines past-due accounts, late fees, and conditions under which loans may be accelerated and foreclosed, and describes general servicing actions like converting payment schedules, establishing escrow accounts, and changing interest calculation methods. Finally, it introduces payment assistance eligibility and refinancing options for borrowers who were previously ineligible for assistance.

nodes.title: § 3550.202 Past due accounts.
nodes.summary: The partial document outlines servicing rules for Rural Housing Service loans, focusing on overdue accounts, fee assessments, liquidation, and payment assistance. It explains when an account is considered past due, when late fees apply, and the conditions under which a loan may be accelerated and pursued for voluntary liquidation or foreclosure for both monthly and annual payment borrowers. It also notes that subsidy recapture must be considered in acceleration cases. In addition, it describes general servicing actions RHS may take during reamortization, such as converting annual payments to monthly payments, requiring escrow accounts for taxes and insurance, and changing daily simple interest accounts to amortized schedules. Finally, it covers payment assistance eligibility and the option for certain ineligible borrowers to refinance to obtain assistance under specified conditions.

nodes.title: § 3550.203 General servicing actions.
nodes.summary: The partial document covers servicing rules for Rural Housing Service loans, focusing on what happens when accounts become past due, including late fees and acceleration/liquidation procedures for monthly and annual payment borrowers. It also explains general servicing actions RHS may take during reamortization, such as converting payment schedules, requiring escrow accounts, and changing interest calculation methods. Finally, it describes payment assistance eligibility and notes that some borrowers who are not initially eligible may refinance to obtain payment assistance under certain conditions.

nodes.title: § 3550.204 Payment assistance.
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes servicing rules for past due and delinquent Rural Housing Service (RHS) loans. It explains when an account is considered past due, when late fees apply, and when loans may be accelerated into liquidation or foreclosure for monthly and annual payment borrowers. It also covers how subsidy recapture affects acceleration.

The document further outlines general servicing actions RHS may take when a loan is reamortized, such as changing annual-payment borrowers to monthly payments, requiring escrow accounts for taxes and insurance, and converting daily simple interest loans to amortized schedules.

It then addresses payment assistance, including when eligible borrowers may receive it and when ineligible borrowers may refinance to obtain assistance. It also details delinquency workout agreements, which allow borrowers to avoid liquidation by paying past-due amounts either in a lump sum or through a structured repayment plan, with cancellation possible if they fall further behind.

Finally, it explains protective advances, where RHS may pay certain costs to protect its interest, such as taxes, insurance, and emergency repairs, and how those advances are repaid, charged, and interest-bearing.

nodes.title: § 3550.204 (Con.)
nodes.summary: The excerpt covers rules for handling borrower delinquencies and protective advances under 7 CFR Part 3550. It explains when a borrower may be eligible for a delinquency workout agreement, including requirements for repaying past-due amounts either in a lump sum or through a repayment plan that cures the account within a limited period, and the possibility of cancellation if the borrower falls more than 30 days behind again. It also describes RHS protective advances, which allow the agency to pay certain costs like taxes, insurance, and emergency repairs to protect its interest, and outlines how those advances are repaid, charged to loans, and interest-bearing.

nodes.title: § 3550.205 Delinquency work out agreements.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt covers Rural Housing Service rules on handling delinquent and at-risk loans. It explains when a borrower may qualify for assistance to avoid losing property, including a requirement that the property be program-eligible and that the borrower be in danger of losing it due to circumstances beyond their control. It then describes delinquency workout agreements, which allow borrowers with past-due accounts to bring loans current either through a single payment or through monthly payments plus an added amount within a specified timeframe, with the agreement subject to cancellation if the borrower falls more than 30 days behind again.

The passage also addresses protective advances, allowing RHS to pay certain costs, such as taxes, insurance, and emergency-related expenses, to protect the government’s interest. It specifies that advances for non-tax/insurance costs are limited to situations where the borrower cannot obtain another loan. Finally, it outlines repayment rules for protective advances, including charging advances against the largest loan when multiple loans exist, requiring repayment with the next scheduled payment or under an adjusted repayment schedule, and applying interest at the loan’s note rate.

nodes.title: § 3550.206 Protective advances.
nodes.summary:
The excerpt outlines USDA Rural Housing Service procedures for handling borrower financial distress and protecting the government’s interest. It covers:

- **Delinquency workout agreements**: options for borrowers with past-due accounts to avoid liquidation by either paying the arrears in one lump sum or, if unable, making regular payments plus an extra amount to cure the delinquency within a set timeframe. It also notes that agreements can be canceled if the borrower falls more than 30 days behind again.
- **Protective advances**: RHS authority to advance funds for expenses such as taxes, insurance, escrow shortages, and certain emergency repairs, then charge those costs back to the borrower. It explains repayment timing, interest, and handling when a borrower has multiple loans.
- **Payment moratoriums**: RHS may temporarily defer scheduled payments for up to two years for eligible borrowers facing hardship beyond their control, such as income loss or unexpected medical/property expenses. The section lists eligibility conditions, review requirements, and circumstances under which a moratorium can be canceled.

nodes.title: § 3550.207 Payment moratorium.
nodes.summary:
The partial document covers USDA Rural Housing Service servicing rules for single-family housing loans, focusing on two main topics: payment moratoriums and reamortization.

It explains when RHS may allow a borrower to defer scheduled payments for up to two years, including eligibility requirements such as a significant drop in repayment income, unexpected unreimbursed expenses from illness or property damage, occupancy of the dwelling, and the loan not being accelerated. It also describes how RHS periodically reviews whether the moratorium should continue, when it may be canceled, how payments are resumed and reamortized, possible forgiveness of accrued interest, and that loans may be liquidated if the borrower still cannot resume payments after two consecutive years.

The document then outlines reamortization at the promissory note interest rate, stating when it may be authorized and under what conditions it must not harm the Government’s lien priority. It lists several permitted uses, including repaying unauthorized assistance, covering deferred amounts from a moratorium, curing delinquent accounts under workout agreements, bringing assumed loans current, and handling remaining debt when part of the secured property is transferred.

nodes.title: § 3550.207(b) (Con.)
nodes.summary:
The partial document describes USDA Rural Housing Service loan servicing rules focused on moratoriums and reamortization. It explains that before a moratorium ends, the borrower must provide updated financial information to determine whether they can resume payments. If the borrower can restart payments, the loan is reamortized to include deferred amounts and escrow requirements, and some accrued interest may be forgiven if the new payment is still unaffordable even with subsidy. If the borrower remains unable to make scheduled payments after two consecutive years on moratorium and all servicing options have been used, the account will be liquidated.

The document also covers reamortization using the promissory note interest rate. This may be authorized when needed to help the borrower meet obligations, as long as government lien priority is not harmed. It lists permitted uses such as repaying unauthorized assistance, covering principal and interest accrued during a moratorium, curing delinquent accounts under workout agreements, handling assumptions where the due-on-sale clause is not triggered, and addressing remaining debt when part of the security property is transferred and the sale price is insufficient.

nodes.title: § 3550.208 Reamortization using promissory note interest rate.
nodes.summary:
